questions.q: Which cell type makes up the majority of the pancreatic islets and what hormone does it produce?
opts: A. Alpha cells; glucagon
B. Beta cells; insulin
C. Delta cells; somatostatin
D. PP cells; pancreatic polypeptide
E. Alpha cells; insulin
F. Option F
questions.exp: Beta cells constitute 75% of each islet and produce insulin. Alpha cells (20%) produce glucagon, Delta cells (4%) produce somatostatin, and PP cells (1%) produce pancreatic polypeptide.

questions.q: A patient is in a prolonged fasting state. Which of the following best describes the primary action of glucagon on the liver?
opts: A. Stimulates glycogen synthesis and inhibits glycogenolysis
B. Facilitates uptake of glucose into hepatocytes via GLUT-4
C. Stimulates glycogenolysis and gluconeogenesis to raise blood glucose
D. Promotes fatty acid synthesis from excess acetyl-CoA
E. Inhibits lipolysis in adipose tissue
F. Option F
questions.exp: Glucagon is released in response to low glucose and stimulates the liver to perform glycogenolysis (glycogen → glucose) and gluconeogenesis (amino acids/glycerol → glucose), raising blood glucose. Options A and D describe insulin's actions.

questions.q: Somatostatin is secreted by which cell type in the pancreatic islets, and what is its primary function?
opts: A. Alpha cells; stimulates glucagon release
B. Beta cells; stimulates insulin secretion
C. PP cells; regulates appetite
D. Delta cells; inhibits both glucagon and insulin production
E. Delta cells; stimulates glycogenolysis
F. Option F
questions.exp: Delta cells (4% of each islet) secrete somatostatin, which inhibits both glucagon and insulin production, acting as a brake on islet hormone secretion.

questions.q: During lipolysis stimulated by glucagon, triglycerides are broken down into free fatty acids and glycerol. What metabolic fate does glycerol most likely undergo?
opts: A. Direct entry into the Krebs cycle as acetyl-CoA
B. Conversion to glucose in the liver via gluconeogenesis
C. Oxidation via beta-oxidation in skeletal muscle
D. Storage as glycogen in the liver
E. Direct conversion to ketone bodies
F. Option F
questions.exp: Glycerol produced by lipolysis is converted to glucose in the liver — a form of gluconeogenesis. This is one of the reasons glucagon-stimulated lipolysis raises blood glucose.

questions.q: Which of the following correctly describes the proportions of cell types within a pancreatic islet?
opts: A. Alpha 75%, Beta 20%, Delta 4%, PP 1%
B. Beta 75%, Alpha 20%, Delta 4%, PP 1%
C. Beta 50%, Alpha 30%, Delta 15%, PP 5%
D. Alpha 50%, Beta 40%, Delta 8%, PP 2%
E. Beta 75%, Delta 20%, Alpha 4%, PP 1%
F. Option F
questions.exp: Beta cells = 75%, Alpha cells = 20%, Delta cells = 4%, PP cells = 1%. This is a commonly tested factual point.

questions.q: Which cells in the body lack insulin receptors and therefore do not require insulin for glucose uptake?
opts: A. Hepatocytes and adipocytes
B. Skeletal muscle cells and adipocytes
C. Red blood cells, brain, liver, and kidney cells
D. Pancreatic alpha cells and intestinal epithelial cells
E. Cardiomyocytes and smooth muscle cells
F. Option F
questions.exp: RBCs, brain, liver, and kidney cells do not have insulin receptors and take up glucose independently of insulin. Skeletal muscle and adipose tissue do require insulin for GLUT-4-mediated uptake.

questions.q: Which of the following best defines catabolism?
opts: A. The synthesis of large molecules from smaller subunits, requiring ATP
B. The breakdown of large organic molecules releasing energy, with ~40% efficiency transferred to ATP
C. The conversion of ADP to ATP via substrate-level phosphorylation only
D. The storage of excess glucose as glycogen in liver and muscle
E. The synthesis of triglycerides from excess carbohydrate substrates
F. Option F
questions.exp: Catabolism breaks down large organic molecules. Approximately 40% of the energy released is transferred to ATP, with 60% lost as heat. Option A describes anabolism.

questions.q: NAD+ acts as an electron carrier in metabolic reactions. What is the reduced form of NAD+?
opts: A. FADH2
B. NAD
C. NADH
D. NADP+
E. CoA
F. Option F
questions.exp: NAD+ accepts electrons and is reduced to NADH. NADH subsequently donates electrons to the electron transport chain. FADH2 (A) is the reduced form of FAD, a different carrier.

questions.q: A student states that anabolic reactions require ATP. Which of the following is an example of an anabolic reaction?
opts: A. Glycolysis
B. Beta-oxidation of fatty acids
C. Glycogenesis (synthesis of glycogen from glucose)
D. The Krebs cycle
E. Fermentation of pyruvate to lactate
F. Option F
questions.exp: Anabolic (biosynthesis) reactions join smaller molecules into larger ones and require ATP. Glycogenesis builds glycogen from glucose monomers. Glycolysis (A), beta-oxidation (B), the Krebs cycle (D), and fermentation (E) are all catabolic.

questions.q: In carbohydrate digestion, where does the process begin and where are monosaccharides ultimately absorbed?
opts: A. Begins in the stomach; absorbed in the large intestine
B. Begins in the mouth (salivary amylase); absorbed as monosaccharides in the small intestine
C. Begins in the small intestine; absorbed in the stomach
D. Begins in the liver; absorbed throughout the GI tract
E. Begins in the pancreas; absorbed in the duodenum only
F. Option F
questions.exp: Carbohydrate digestion begins with salivary amylase in the mouth and is completed to monosaccharides that are absorbed by the intestinal epithelium in the small intestine.

questions.q: What is the net ATP gain per molecule of glucose through glycolysis?
opts: A. 4 ATP
B. 2 ATP
C. 36 ATP
D. 0 ATP
E. 6 ATP
F. Option F
questions.exp: Glycolysis uses 2 ATP in the investment phase and produces 4 ATP in the payoff phase, giving a net gain of 2 ATP per glucose molecule. 36 ATP (C) is the total aerobic respiration yield.

questions.q: Which enzyme catalyses the rate-limiting step of glycolysis and what are its allosteric inhibitors?
opts: A. Hexokinase; inhibited by AMP and ADP
B. Pyruvate kinase; inhibited by ATP and glucose
C. Phosphofructokinase-1 (PFK-1); inhibited by ATP and citrate
D. Aldolase; inhibited by fructose-1,6-bisphosphate
E. Enolase; inhibited by fluoride and phosphate
F. Option F
questions.exp: PFK-1 is the rate-limiting enzyme of glycolysis, inhibited by high ATP and citrate (indicating sufficient energy), and activated by AMP, ADP, and fructose-6-phosphate.

questions.q: Which of the following steps in glycolysis are irreversible?
opts: A. Phosphoglucose isomerase, enolase, and phosphoglycerate mutase
B. Hexokinase, phosphofructokinase-1, and pyruvate kinase
C. Aldolase, triose phosphate isomerase, and enolase
D. G3P dehydrogenase, phosphoglycerate kinase, and enolase
E. All steps of glycolysis are reversible
F. Option F
questions.exp: The three irreversible steps are catalysed by hexokinase (step 1), PFK-1 (step 3), and pyruvate kinase (step 10). These are also the three regulated steps.

questions.q: In step 6 of glycolysis, G3P is converted to 1,3-bisphosphoglycerate. Which coenzyme is reduced in this reaction?
opts: A. FAD → FADH2
B. NAD+ → NADH
C. GDP → GTP
D. ADP → ATP
E. CoA → Acetyl-CoA
F. Option F
questions.exp: G3P dehydrogenase catalyses this step, using NAD+ which is reduced to NADH. This is the only NADH-producing step in the payoff phase of glycolysis.

questions.q: What is the immediate product of glycolysis that can then enter either aerobic or anaerobic pathways?
opts: A. Acetyl-CoA
B. Oxaloacetate
C. Lactate
D. Pyruvate
E. Citrate
F. Option F
questions.exp: Glycolysis produces pyruvate (2 per glucose). Under aerobic conditions pyruvate is converted to acetyl-CoA for the Krebs cycle; under anaerobic conditions it is fermented to lactate.

questions.q: PFK-1 is activated by which of the following molecules, indicating low cellular energy?
opts: A. ATP and citrate
B. NADH and FADH2
C. AMP and ADP
D. Glucose-6-phosphate and fructose-1,6-bisphosphate
E. Glucagon and epinephrine
F. Option F
questions.exp: AMP and ADP signal low energy states and activate PFK-1 to accelerate glycolysis and generate more ATP. ATP and citrate (A) are inhibitors, not activators.

questions.q: Which step of glycolysis produces the first ATP molecule via substrate-level phosphorylation?
opts: A. Step 3: fructose-6-phosphate → fructose-1,6-bisphosphate
B. Step 7: 1,3-BPG → 3-phosphoglycerate
C. Step 10: PEP → pyruvate
D. Step 6: G3P → 1,3-BPG
E. Step 1: glucose → glucose-6-phosphate
F. Option F
questions.exp: Step 7 (phosphoglycerate kinase) is the first ATP-producing step, transferring a phosphate from 1,3-bisphosphoglycerate to ADP. Step 3 (A) actually consumes ATP.

questions.q: How many molecules of NADH are produced per glucose molecule during glycolysis?
opts: F. Option F
questions.exp: Glycolysis produces 2 NADH per glucose — one per G3P molecule, and each glucose yields 2 G3P molecules from step 5 onwards.

questions.q: A researcher discovers a competitive inhibitor of enolase. Which step of glycolysis would be blocked?
opts: A. Fructose-6-phosphate → fructose-1,6-bisphosphate
B. 3-phosphoglycerate → 2-phosphoglycerate
C. 2-phosphoglycerate → phosphoenolpyruvate (PEP)
D. Glucose → glucose-6-phosphate
E. PEP → pyruvate
F. Option F
questions.exp: Enolase catalyses step 9: the dehydration of 2-phosphoglycerate to PEP, releasing H₂O. Inhibiting enolase blocks this step, preventing PEP formation and halting glycolysis before the final ATP-producing step.

questions.q: Glucose stored in the liver and skeletal muscle is stored in what form?
opts: A. Triglycerides
B. Free fatty acids
C. Glycogen
D. Amino acids
E. Ketone bodies
F. Option F
questions.exp: Excess glucose is stored as glycogen — in the liver (~75g) and skeletal muscle (~250g). Triglycerides (A) are stored in adipose tissue when glycogen stores are full.

questions.q: During anaerobic respiration, pyruvate is converted to lactate via fermentation. What is the primary purpose of this conversion?
opts: A. To generate additional ATP via substrate-level phosphorylation
B. To regenerate NAD+ so that glycolysis can continue
C. To produce acetyl-CoA for entry into the Krebs cycle
D. To allow pyruvate to enter the mitochondrial matrix
E. To generate CO2 as a waste product
F. Option F
questions.exp: Fermentation converts pyruvate to lactate, which oxidises NADH back to NAD+. This regenerated NAD+ is essential for glycolysis to continue — without it, glycolysis halts due to NAD+ depletion.

questions.q: Why do erythrocytes rely exclusively on fermentation for ATP production?
opts: A. They lack insulin receptors and cannot take up glucose
B. They lack mitochondria, so the aerobic pathways are unavailable
C. Their cytoplasm is too acidic for Krebs cycle enzymes
D. They express only glucokinase, which inhibits aerobic respiration
E. They are continuously supplied with oxygen and have no need for fermentation
F. Option F
questions.exp: Erythrocytes (red blood cells) lack mitochondria, so they cannot perform the Krebs cycle or oxidative phosphorylation. Glycolysis followed by fermentation is their only ATP source.

questions.q: The Cori cycle describes which metabolic process occurring in the liver?
opts: A. Conversion of fatty acids to ketone bodies during starvation
B. Synthesis of urea from ammonia produced by amino acid catabolism
C. Conversion of lactic acid back to pyruvate or glucose
D. Production of bile salts from cholesterol
E. Conversion of glycogen to glucose in response to glucagon
F. Option F
questions.exp: The Cori cycle in the liver converts lactic acid (produced by anaerobic respiration in muscles) back into pyruvate or glucose, recycling the carbon skeleton and preventing lactate accumulation.

questions.q: Which enzyme complex converts pyruvate to acetyl-CoA for entry into the Krebs cycle?
opts: A. Pyruvate kinase
B. Citrate synthase
C. Pyruvate dehydrogenase complex (PDC)
D. Phosphofructokinase-1
E. Malate dehydrogenase
F. Option F
questions.exp: The pyruvate dehydrogenase complex (PDC) converts pyruvate to acetyl-CoA, releasing CO2 and reducing NAD+ to NADH. Thiamine (B1) is an essential cofactor for PDC.

questions.q: During intense exercise, muscles use ATP faster than oxygen can be delivered. Which metabolic pathway primarily supplies ATP under these conditions?
opts: A. Oxidative phosphorylation via the ETC
B. Beta-oxidation of fatty acids
C. Gluconeogenesis in the liver
D. Glycolysis followed by fermentation (anaerobic)
E. The Krebs cycle within muscle mitochondria
F. Option F
questions.exp: When oxygen delivery is insufficient, muscle relies on anaerobic glycolysis followed by fermentation of pyruvate to lactate. This regenerates NAD+ and sustains ATP production without oxygen.

questions.q: In aerobic respiration, electrons are transferred from pyruvate to which electron acceptors to extract additional energy before the Krebs cycle?
opts: A. ATP and CoA
B. NAD+, GDP, and FAD
C. Oxygen and water
D. AMP and citrate
E. Glucose-6-phosphate and fructose-1,6-bisphosphate
F. Option F
questions.exp: Extra energy is extracted when electrons from pyruvate are transferred to NAD+, GDP, and FAD. These reduced carriers (NADH, FADH2) then donate electrons to the ETC.

questions.q: What is the net yield of NADH, FADH2, GTP, and CO2 per turn of the Krebs cycle (per acetyl-CoA)?
opts: A. 2 NADH, 2 FADH2, 2 GTP, 4 CO2
B. 3 NADH, 1 FADH2, 1 GTP, 2 CO2
C. 4 NADH, 2 FADH2, 1 GTP, 3 CO2
D. 1 NADH, 1 FADH2, 2 GTP, 2 CO2
E. 3 NADH, 2 FADH2, 2 GTP, 2 CO2
F. Option F
questions.exp: Per acetyl-CoA entering the Krebs cycle: 3 NADH (steps 3, 4, 8), 1 FADH2 (step 6), 1 GTP (step 5), and 2 CO2 (steps 3 and 4) are produced.

questions.q: In which step of the Krebs cycle is FADH2 produced?
opts: A. Isocitrate → alpha-ketoglutarate (step 3)
B. Alpha-ketoglutarate → succinyl-CoA (step 4)
C. Succinate → fumarate (step 6)
D. Malate → oxaloacetate (step 8)
E. Citrate → isocitrate (step 2)
F. Option F
questions.exp: Succinate dehydrogenase catalyses step 6 (succinate → fumarate), reducing FAD to FADH2. This is the only FADH2-producing step and is unique because succinate dehydrogenase is embedded in the inner mitochondrial membrane.

questions.q: Which molecule combines with acetyl-CoA to initiate the Krebs cycle, and what is the product?
opts: A. Malate; isocitrate
B. Succinyl-CoA; succinate
C. Oxaloacetate; citrate
D. Fumarate; alpha-ketoglutarate
E. Alpha-ketoglutarate; succinyl-CoA
F. Option F
questions.exp: Citrate synthase condenses acetyl-CoA with oxaloacetate to form citrate, the first product of the Krebs cycle. Oxaloacetate is regenerated at the end of each turn.

questions.q: CO2 is released at which steps of the Krebs cycle?
opts: A. Steps 1 and 2
B. Steps 3 and 4
C. Steps 5 and 6
D. Steps 7 and 8
E. Steps 2 and 5
F. Option F
questions.exp: CO2 is released at step 3 (isocitrate → alpha-ketoglutarate, by isocitrate dehydrogenase) and step 4 (alpha-ketoglutarate → succinyl-CoA, by alpha-KG dehydrogenase).

questions.q: Because glucose yields two pyruvate molecules, how many total NADH are produced from the Krebs cycle per glucose molecule?
opts: F. Option F
questions.exp: One turn of the Krebs cycle yields 3 NADH. Since glucose produces 2 pyruvate → 2 acetyl-CoA, the cycle runs twice: 2 × 3 = 6 NADH per glucose from the Krebs cycle alone.

questions.q: Before entering the Krebs cycle, pyruvate undergoes a preparatory reaction. Which correctly describes this step?
opts: A. Pyruvate gains CO2 to form oxaloacetate, utilising ATP
B. Pyruvate is reduced to lactate by lactate dehydrogenase
C. Pyruvate combines with coenzyme A, releases CO2, and converts NAD+ to NADH, yielding acetyl-CoA
D. Pyruvate is phosphorylated by pyruvate kinase to form PEP
E. Pyruvate accepts two electrons from FADH2 to form acetyl-CoA
F. Option F
questions.exp: The pyruvate dehydrogenase complex converts pyruvate to acetyl-CoA using coenzyme A, releasing CO2 and reducing NAD+ to NADH. Option A describes the gluconeogenesis step catalysed by pyruvate carboxylase.

questions.q: GTP is produced at which step of the Krebs cycle?
opts: A. Citrate → isocitrate
B. Isocitrate → alpha-ketoglutarate
C. Succinyl-CoA → succinate
D. Fumarate → malate
E. Malate → oxaloacetate
F. Option F
questions.exp: Succinyl-CoA synthetase catalyses step 5 (succinyl-CoA → succinate), coupling this to the phosphorylation of GDP to GTP — the only substrate-level phosphorylation in the Krebs cycle.

questions.q: The electron transport chain is located in which cellular structure?
opts: A. The cytoplasm of the cell
B. The outer mitochondrial membrane
C. The inner mitochondrial membrane
D. The mitochondrial matrix
E. The endoplasmic reticulum
F. Option F
questions.exp: The four enzyme complexes and coenzymes of the ETC are embedded in the inner mitochondrial membrane, which is where the proton gradient is established.

questions.q: What is the terminal electron acceptor in the electron transport chain?
opts: A. NAD+
B. FAD
C. CO2
D. Oxygen (O2)
E. Cytochrome c
F. Option F
questions.exp: Oxygen is the terminal electron acceptor. Electrons, O2, and H+ from the matrix combine to form water at the end of the ETC. Without oxygen, the chain backs up and stops.

questions.q: ATP synthase generates ATP using which driving force?
opts: A. Direct oxidation of NADH in the mitochondrial matrix
B. The proton (H+) gradient across the inner mitochondrial membrane
C. Substrate-level phosphorylation from GTP
D. The transfer of electrons from FADH2 to oxygen
E. The rotation of cytochrome c
F. Option F
questions.exp: The proton gradient (H+ accumulation in the intermembrane space) drives H+ back through ATP synthase. This flow causes rotation, enabling ATP synthase to combine ADP + Pi → ATP.

questions.q: How many ATP are produced per NADH entering the ETC, compared to per FADH2?
opts: A. NADH = 2 ATP; FADH2 = 3 ATP
B. NADH = 3 ATP; FADH2 = 2 ATP
C. NADH = 2 ATP; FADH2 = 2 ATP
D. NADH = 4 ATP; FADH2 = 3 ATP
E. NADH = 3 ATP; FADH2 = 3 ATP
F. Option F
questions.exp: NADH enters at Complex I (more energy), yielding ~3 ATP. FADH2 enters at Complex II, bypassing Complex I, yielding ~2 ATP. The difference reflects the number of protons pumped.

questions.q: Which vitamins are involved in the function of the electron transport chain?
opts: A. Vitamins A, C, D, and E
B. Vitamins B2, B3, B6, and B12
C. Vitamins K, B1, B7, and folate
D. Vitamins C and E only
E. Vitamins B1, B2, B9, and B12
F. Option F
questions.exp: The ETC involves vitamins B2 (riboflavin, part of FAD), B3 (niacin, part of NAD), B6, and B12. These B vitamins are essential cofactors for the electron carrier complexes.

questions.q: A patient has severe iron deficiency anaemia. Why might this also cause fatigue due to impaired energy metabolism?
opts: A. Iron is required for glycolysis, which cannot proceed without it
B. The protein complexes of the ETC require iron for functioning; low iron impairs ATP production
C. Iron deficiency prevents gluconeogenesis in the liver
D. Iron is a cofactor for pyruvate kinase in glycolysis
E. Iron is needed for the synthesis of coenzyme A
F. Option F
questions.exp: The protein complexes of the ETC require iron (in haem and iron-sulfur clusters) to function. Low iron leads to impaired electron transport, reduced ATP production, and fatigue beyond just anaemia.

questions.q: What is the approximate total ATP yield per molecule of glucose through all pathways of aerobic respiration?
opts: A. 2 ATP
B. 8 ATP
C. 20 ATP
D. 36 ATP
E. 100 ATP
F. Option F
questions.exp: Approximately 36 ATP are produced per glucose through glycolysis (2 net), the Krebs cycle (2 GTP), and the ETC (from 10 NADH and 2 FADH2). Note that glycolytic NADH contributes net 0 due to transport costs.

questions.q: The two coenzymes in the ETC — ubiquinone and cytochrome c — serve which function?
opts: A. They directly synthesise ATP from ADP and Pi
B. They act as electron carriers and proton pumps, transferring H+ into the intermembrane space
C. They reduce oxygen to water independently of the protein complexes
D. They transport pyruvate across the inner mitochondrial membrane
E. They couple the Krebs cycle to glycolysis
F. Option F
questions.exp: Ubiquinone and cytochrome c act as mobile electron carriers and proton pumps, shuttling electrons between the fixed complexes and transferring H+ into the intermembrane space to build the proton gradient.

questions.q: Why does the net ATP contribution from glycolytic NADH to the ETC effectively equal zero?
opts: A. The NADH from glycolysis is degraded before reaching the mitochondria
B. The 2 ATP produced in glycolysis are consumed transporting NADH from the cytoplasm into the mitochondria
C. Glycolytic NADH donates electrons directly to FADH2, bypassing the ETC
D. Glycolytic NADH cannot be oxidised by the ETC due to membrane impermeability
E. NADH from glycolysis is used exclusively for gluconeogenesis
F. Option F
questions.exp: The 2 ATP produced in glycolysis are used to transport the NADH generated during glycolysis from the cytoplasm into the mitochondria, resulting in a net contribution of 0 from these NADH molecules.

questions.q: Gluconeogenesis is the synthesis of new glucose molecules from which precursors?
opts: A. Fatty acids and ketone bodies only
B. Pyruvate, lactate, glycerol, and glucogenic amino acids (alanine and glutamine)
C. Glycogen stores in the liver and skeletal muscle only
D. Acetyl-CoA derived from beta-oxidation
E. Dietary fructose and galactose converted to glucose-6-phosphate
F. Option F
questions.exp: Gluconeogenesis synthesises glucose from pyruvate, lactate, glycerol, and glucogenic amino acids — primarily alanine and glutamine. Note that fatty acids (as acetyl-CoA) cannot be converted to glucose in humans.

questions.q: Why must blood glucose be maintained through gluconeogenesis?
opts: A. The liver cannot oxidise fatty acids and depends entirely on glucose
B. The brain can only run off glucose and requires constant supply for homeostasis
C. Red blood cells cannot perform glycolysis without continuous glucose from gluconeogenesis
D. The kidneys require glucose for active transport of electrolytes
E. Insulin secretion is completely dependent on hepatic glucose production
F. Option F
questions.exp: The brain can only use glucose as its fuel (except in prolonged starvation when ketones can substitute), so gluconeogenesis is essential to maintain blood glucose homeostasis.

questions.q: In gluconeogenesis, hexokinase is replaced by which enzyme, and why is this important?
opts: A. Replaced by glucokinase; glucokinase is more sensitive to low glucose concentrations
B. Replaced by glucose-6-phosphatase; allows independent regulation of glycolysis and gluconeogenesis
C. Replaced by pyruvate carboxylase; enables the reverse of the pyruvate kinase reaction
D. Replaced by fructose-1,6-bisphosphatase; bypasses the rate-limiting step of glycolysis
E. Replaced by phosphoglycerate kinase; allows ATP to drive the reaction forward
F. Option F
questions.exp: Hexokinase is replaced by glucose-6-phosphatase in gluconeogenesis. This allows the cell to independently regulate glycolysis and gluconeogenesis — both substituted enzymes are rate-limiting steps.

questions.q: Which enzyme catalyses the conversion of pyruvate to oxaloacetate in gluconeogenesis, and what does it require?
opts: A. PEPCK; requires GTP
B. Pyruvate kinase; releases ATP
C. Pyruvate carboxylase; utilises ATP and adds CO2
D. Malate dehydrogenase; reduces NAD+
E. Citrate synthase; combines with acetyl-CoA
F. Option F
questions.exp: Pyruvate carboxylase converts pyruvate to oxaloacetate by adding CO2, utilising ATP in the process. This is the first committed step of gluconeogenesis from pyruvate.

questions.q: Fructose-2,6-bisphosphate is the main regulator of fructose-1,6-bisphosphatase. Which conditions stimulate its production?
opts: A. Low ATP and high AMP concentrations
B. High ATP, producing fructose-2,6-bisphosphate as a byproduct
C. High AMP concentration, indicating low energy
D. High citrate levels signalling excess acetyl-CoA
E. Elevated insulin signalling in the fed state
F. Option F
questions.exp: Fructose-2,6-bisphosphate is a byproduct of high ATP. When present in LOW concentration it stimulates fructose-1,6-bisphosphatase (gluconeogenesis). It is inhibited by AMP.

questions.q: During which of the following conditions would gluconeogenesis be most active?
opts: A. Shortly after a high-carbohydrate meal with elevated insulin levels
B. After 18 hours of fasting, when it becomes the primary glucose source
C. During brief intense exercise when ATP levels are transiently elevated
D. In the fed state with a high insulin-to-glucagon ratio
E. During glycogen synthesis in the liver postprandially
F. Option F
questions.exp: Gluconeogenesis increases progressively during fasting and becomes the primary source of blood glucose by approximately 18 hours. In the fed state (A, D) glycogen synthesis and glycolysis predominate.

questions.q: Which hormone is released by intestinal cells when food reaches the small intestine, and what are its two primary actions relevant to fat digestion?
opts: A. Glucagon; stimulates glycogenolysis and suppresses hunger
B. Insulin; promotes glucose uptake and stimulates bile release
C. Cholecystokinin (CCK); stimulates pancreatic lipase release and contracts the gallbladder to release bile salts
D. Somatostatin; inhibits glucagon and stimulates pancreatic enzyme release
E. Ghrelin; stimulates appetite and promotes gastric acid secretion
F. Option F
questions.exp: CCK is released by intestinal mucosal cells and stimulates pancreatic lipase release and gallbladder contraction to release bile salts. It also reaches the brain to suppress hunger.

questions.q: After absorption through the intestinal epithelium, fatty acids are repackaged into which structures that allow transport in aqueous environments?
opts: A. Triglycerides transported freely in plasma
B. Chylomicrons — phospholipid vesicles containing triglycerides and cholesterol
C. HDL particles synthesised in the intestinal wall
D. Free fatty acid-albumin complexes
E. VLDL assembled in the intestinal cell
F. Option F
questions.exp: Inside intestinal cells, fatty acids and cholesterol are packaged into chylomicrons (phospholipid vesicles), enabling transport in the aqueous lymphatic and circulatory systems.

questions.q: After leaving intestinal cells via exocytosis, chylomicrons first enter which system before reaching the circulation?
opts: A. The hepatic portal vein directly
B. The renal tubular system
C. The lymphatic system via lacteals in the intestinal villi
D. The thoracic duct immediately bypassing lymphatics
E. The mesenteric veins draining to the inferior vena cava
F. Option F
questions.exp: Chylomicrons leave intestinal cells by exocytosis and enter the lymphatic system via lacteals in the intestinal villi, before being transported to the circulatory system.

questions.q: Beta-oxidation of fatty acids ultimately produces which molecule that enters the Krebs cycle?
opts: A. Pyruvate
B. Oxaloacetate
C. Lactate
D. Acetyl-CoA
E. Glucose-6-phosphate
F. Option F
questions.exp: Lipolysis and beta-oxidation convert triglycerides into acetyl-CoA, which then enters the Krebs cycle for energy production. This is why fat is such a dense energy source.

questions.q: In which cellular location does lipolysis (beta-oxidation) occur?
opts: A. The cytoplasm and then mitochondrial matrix
B. The cell nucleus
C. The smooth endoplasmic reticulum
D. The outer mitochondrial membrane
E. The lysosomal compartment
F. Option F
questions.exp: Lipolysis begins in the cytoplasm with hydrolysis of triglycerides. Fatty acyl-carnitine then crosses the inner mitochondrial membrane into the matrix where beta-oxidation to acetyl-CoA takes place.

questions.q: Carnitine plays a key role in lipid metabolism. What is its function?
opts: A. It emulsifies dietary fat in the small intestine alongside bile salts
B. It shuttles fatty acyl groups across the inner mitochondrial membrane into the matrix
C. It activates pancreatic lipase for triglyceride digestion
D. It inhibits ketogenesis when acetyl-CoA levels are low
E. It phosphorylates fatty acids to initiate beta-oxidation
F. Option F
questions.exp: Fatty acid + carnitine together with coenzyme A form fatty acetyl-carnitine, which is transported into the mitochondrial matrix for beta-oxidation. Without carnitine, long-chain fatty acids cannot enter the mitochondria.

questions.q: Ketogenesis is initiated when which condition occurs?
opts: A. Glycolysis is upregulated, producing excess pyruvate
B. Gluconeogenesis overwhelms the liver's capacity for glucose synthesis
C. Too much acetyl-CoA is produced from fatty acid oxidation, overloading the Krebs cycle
D. Excess dietary protein is converted to amino acids that enter the Krebs cycle
E. Insulin levels are high and fatty acid synthesis is stimulated
F. Option F
questions.exp: When fatty acid oxidation produces more acetyl-CoA than the Krebs cycle can handle, the excess is diverted to ketogenesis to produce ketone bodies. This occurs in starvation and uncontrolled diabetes.

questions.q: Beta-hydroxybutyrate is the primary ketone body in blood. In which clinical scenarios would ketone bodies serve as a significant fuel source?
opts: A. Shortly after a high-carbohydrate meal with high insulin levels
B. During mild exercise with adequate glucose supply
C. In prolonged starvation or uncontrolled diabetes with low glucose availability
D. During glycogenolysis following a brief fast
E. In the early postprandial period when chylomicrons are abundant
F. Option F
questions.exp: Ketone bodies are produced and used as fuel when glucose levels are too low — particularly during prolonged starvation and in diabetes. Organs that normally depend on glucose (brain, heart, muscle) can use ketones as an alternative.

questions.q: A patient with poorly controlled type 1 diabetes presents with sweet-smelling breath. What is the most likely metabolic explanation?
opts: A. Excess glucose is exhaled as glycosylated CO2
B. Ketones produced faster than they can be used are broken down to CO2 and acetone, which is exhaled
C. High lactate from anaerobic metabolism produces an acidic volatile compound
D. Glycogen breakdown releases volatile glucose fragments
E. Elevated triglycerides in the blood produce exhaled fatty acid vapour
F. Option F
questions.exp: When ketones are produced faster than they can be used, they are broken down to CO2 and acetone. Acetone is volatile and is exhaled, producing a characteristic sweet or 'pear drop' smell — a sign of diabetic ketoacidosis.

questions.q: The liver stores approximately how much glycogen, and what is its primary role?
opts: A. 250g; used exclusively for hepatic energy needs
B. 75g; circulated for blood glucose regulation between meals
C. 75g; used only during intense exercise
D. 500g; provides sustained fuel for the brain
E. 250g; released into circulation to maintain blood glucose during fasting
F. Option F
questions.exp: The liver stores approximately 75g of glycogen, which is released into the blood to maintain blood glucose levels between meals. Skeletal muscle stores ~250g but this is for local muscle use only.

questions.q: Which glucose transporter mediates glucose uptake in skeletal muscle and adipose tissue in response to insulin?
opts: A. GLUT-1
B. GLUT-2
C. GLUT-3
D. GLUT-4
E. SGLT-1
F. Option F
questions.exp: Insulin promotes glucose uptake in skeletal muscle and adipose tissue via GLUT-4 transporters. GLUT-1 and GLUT-3 are used by the brain (insulin-independent).

questions.q: What is the sequence of events triggered by a rise in blood glucose above 4.4-5.0 mmol/L that leads to insulin secretion?
opts: A. Glucose opens K+ channels → hyperpolarisation → Ca2+ efflux → insulin exocytosis
B. Glucose enters beta cells → ATP produced → ATP closes K+ channels → depolarisation → Ca2+ influx → insulin granule exocytosis
C. Glucose binds insulin receptors on beta cells → cAMP → protein kinase A → insulin release
D. Glucose activates glucagon receptors → GLUT-4 insertion → insulin synthesis
E. Glucose stimulates somatostatin → inhibits glucagon → insulin released by default
F. Option F
questions.exp: Glucose enters beta cells via transporters, undergoes metabolism to produce ATP, which closes ATP-sensitive K+ channels causing depolarisation. This opens voltage-gated Ca2+ channels; Ca2+ influx triggers insulin granule exocytosis. Ca2+ is the trigger for insulin release.

questions.q: During the fasting state, what is the correct sequence of hypoglycaemia defences, from first to last?
opts: A. Increase glucagon → increase epinephrine → decrease insulin → increase cortisol/GH
B. Decrease insulin → increase glucagon → increase epinephrine → increase cortisol and growth hormone
C. Increase cortisol → decrease insulin → increase glucagon → increase epinephrine
D. Increase epinephrine → decrease insulin → increase glucagon → increase cortisol/GH
E. Decrease insulin → increase cortisol → increase glucagon → increase epinephrine
F. Option F
questions.exp: The defences in order: (1) decrease insulin — first defence, (2) increase glucagon — hormonal defence, (3) increase epinephrine — backup for glucagon, (4) increase cortisol and growth hormone — slower, sustained recovery.

questions.q: After an overnight fast of approximately 12 hours, which process is at its peak contribution to blood glucose?
opts: A. Dietary glucose absorption from the gut
B. Gluconeogenesis, which has become the sole source
C. Glycogenolysis, which peaks at around 12 hours
D. Ketone oxidation supplying the brain exclusively
E. Beta-oxidation converting triglycerides directly to glucose
F. Option F
questions.exp: Glycogenolysis is active from 5–26 hours of fasting and peaks at around 12 hours. Gluconeogenesis rises progressively and becomes the primary source by 18 hours. Dietary glucose (A) runs out after ~4 hours.

questions.q: In the fed (postprandial) state, which organ is the primary site of glucose uptake?
opts: A. Liver
B. Brain
C. Skeletal muscle
D. Adipose tissue
E. Kidney
F. Option F
questions.exp: In the fed state, skeletal muscle is the primary site of glucose uptake, with high insulin promoting GLUT-4-mediated uptake and glycogen synthesis. Hepatic glucose production is limited postprandially.

questions.q: Insulin inhibits which hepatic processes to promote the storage of glucose?
opts: A. Glycogenesis and fatty acid synthesis
B. Glycolysis and glucose uptake
C. Glycogenolysis and gluconeogenesis
D. Beta-oxidation and ketogenesis only
E. Insulin has no direct hepatic effects
F. Option F
questions.exp: Insulin suppresses glycogenolysis and gluconeogenesis in the liver, while simultaneously stimulating glycogenesis and fatty acid synthesis — promoting glucose storage.

questions.q: A high-protein meal causes co-elevation of both insulin and glucagon. What is the physiological rationale?
opts: A. Protein directly stimulates beta cells, while glucagon is needed to prevent hypoglycaemia from the insulin response
B. Glucagon promotes amino acid uptake; insulin prevents the hyperglycaemia that would result from gluconeogenesis of those amino acids
C. Both hormones are needed to stimulate protein synthesis in skeletal muscle
D. Protein activates CCK, which triggers both insulin and glucagon release simultaneously
E. Glucagon promotes fatty acid synthesis while insulin promotes glycogen storage after protein intake
F. Option F
questions.exp: After a high-protein meal, glucagon promotes amino acid uptake and prevents hypoglycaemia, while insulin prevents hyperglycaemia that could result from gluconeogenesis of amino acids. Both hormones serve complementary roles.

questions.q: The brain uses which glucose transporters for insulin-independent glucose uptake?
opts: A. GLUT-2 and GLUT-4
B. GLUT-1 and GLUT-3
C. SGLT-1 and SGLT-2
D. GLUT-4 and GLUT-5
E. GLUT-2 and GLUT-3
F. Option F
questions.exp: The brain uses GLUT-1 and GLUT-3 for glucose uptake — both are insulin-independent, hence the brain is independent of insulin but entirely dependent on glucose availability (or ketones in prolonged fasting).

questions.q: A researcher blocks glucagon receptors in an animal model. Which metabolic consequence would most likely result?
opts: A. Increased glycogen synthesis in skeletal muscle
B. Enhanced glucose uptake in adipose tissue
C. Increased insulin secretion from pancreatic beta cells
D. Increased hepatic fatty acid oxidation
E. Impaired hepatic gluconeogenesis
F. Option F
questions.exp: Glucagon normally stimulates hepatic gluconeogenesis and glycogenolysis. Blocking glucagon receptors would impair gluconeogenesis, potentially causing hypoglycaemia, especially during fasting.

questions.q: A patient with thiamine (B1) deficiency presents with confusion. Thiamine is an essential cofactor for the PDC. Which metabolic consequence would you most expect?
opts: A. Accumulation of acetyl-CoA leading to increased fatty acid synthesis
B. Impaired conversion of pyruvate to acetyl-CoA, causing pyruvate and lactate to accumulate
C. Reduced activity of PFK-1, blocking early glycolysis
D. Increased flux through the Krebs cycle via compensatory enzymes
E. Increased conversion of pyruvate to acetyl-CoA, causing hypoglycaemia
F. Option F
questions.exp: Without thiamine, PDC cannot convert pyruvate to acetyl-CoA. Pyruvate accumulates and is shunted to lactate, causing lactic acidosis. This also deprives the Krebs cycle of substrate, impairing aerobic ATP production.

questions.q: A patient develops hypoxia. Which of the following best describes the downstream cellular pathology?
opts: A. Hypoxia → excess NADH → ATP overproduction → cellular swelling
B. Hypoxia → ETC disruption → reduced ATP → Na/K ATPase failure → cellular swelling and calcium influx → mitochondrial dysfunction
C. Hypoxia → gluconeogenesis upregulation → excess glucose → osmotic cell damage
D. Hypoxia → increased beta-oxidation → ketosis → metabolic alkalosis
E. Hypoxia → glycogen accumulation → disrupted calcium signalling
F. Option F
questions.exp: Low oxygen disrupts the ETC → reduced ATP → failure of the Na+/K+ ATPase pump → less ion pumping → cellular swelling (cytotoxic oedema). Calcium then floods into the cell, causing decreased mitochondrial function and cell death.

questions.q: Which of the following statements about pyruvate kinase deficiency in red blood cells is correct?
opts: A. It leads to excess ATP production, causing cell lysis
B. It causes accumulation of 2,3-BPG, lowering the affinity of haemoglobin for oxygen
C. It increases glycolytic flux, causing lactic acidosis
D. It stimulates gluconeogenesis in erythrocytes
E. It results in excess NADH, inhibiting the electron transport chain
F. Option F
questions.exp: Pyruvate kinase deficiency leads to accumulation of 2,3-BPG (a glycolytic intermediate) in red blood cells, which lowers the affinity of haemoglobin for oxygen. This results in haemolytic anaemia.

questions.q: High doses of salicylate can cause which metabolic effect on oxidative phosphorylation?
opts: A. They directly inhibit ATP synthase, preventing ADP phosphorylation
B. They block Complex IV of the ETC, preventing electron transfer to oxygen
C. They cause uncoupling of the ETC, accelerating electron transport but inhibiting ATP synthesis and generating heat
D. They competitively inhibit NADH binding at Complex I
E. They cause permanent phosphorylation of ATP synthase
F. Option F
questions.exp: High doses of salicylate cause uncoupling of the ETC — electron transport is accelerated but ATP synthesis is inhibited, with energy dissipated as heat. This is different from direct inhibition of ETC complexes.

questions.q: In prolonged starvation beyond 24 hours, which of the following best describes the fuel used by the brain?
opts: A. Exclusively glucose from ongoing hepatic glycogenolysis
B. Exclusively fatty acids obtained from lipolysis
C. Primarily glucose, with ketone bodies as an increasingly important alternative fuel
D. Exclusively amino acids obtained from muscle protein catabolism
E. Exclusively lactate produced by red blood cells via fermentation
F. Option F
questions.exp: The brain normally runs on glucose. In prolonged fasting, it increasingly utilises ketone bodies as an alternative fuel when glucose becomes scarce. It cannot use fatty acids directly as they don't cross the blood-brain barrier.

questions.q: A patient follows a prolonged low-carbohydrate, high-protein diet. Which statement best explains how amino acids contribute to cellular energy production?
opts: A. Amino acids are oxidised directly in the electron transport chain
B. All amino acids must first be converted to glucose before entering glycolysis
C. Glucogenic amino acids are converted to intermediates that feed into the Krebs cycle
D. Amino acids are predominantly converted to fatty acids for beta-oxidation
E. Amino acids donate electrons directly to NADH molecules
F. Option F
questions.exp: Glucogenic amino acids (such as alanine and glutamine) are converted to intermediates such as pyruvate and oxaloacetate that feed directly into the Krebs cycle, enabling energy production.

questions.q: A clinician notes that hypoglycaemia is more dangerous than hyperglycaemia. Which metabolic reason best supports this?
opts: A. Hyperglycaemia has no acute cellular consequences, whereas hypoglycaemia rapidly depletes all hepatic glycogen
B. The brain is entirely dependent on glucose (except in prolonged starvation), making hypoglycaemia immediately life-threatening
C. Hypoglycaemia causes immediate ketoacidosis, which is more dangerous than osmotic effects of hyperglycaemia
D. Red blood cells lack insulin receptors, so hyperglycaemia cannot directly damage them
E. Gluconeogenesis cannot compensate for hypoglycaemia because it requires 48 hours to activate
F. Option F
questions.exp: The brain relies almost exclusively on glucose and cannot rapidly switch to alternative fuels. This makes hypoglycaemia immediately life-threatening — even brief severe hypoglycaemia can cause brain damage or death.

questions.q: Which of the following correctly summarises the metabolic fate of excess dietary glucose?
opts: A. All excess glucose is immediately oxidised via glycolysis and the Krebs cycle
B. Excess glucose is stored as glycogen in liver and muscle; if stores are full, it is converted to triglycerides in adipose tissue
C. Excess glucose is exclusively excreted by the kidneys to prevent hyperglycaemia
D. Excess glucose is converted to amino acids for protein synthesis in the liver
E. Excess glucose is converted entirely to ketone bodies via acetyl-CoA accumulation
F. Option F
questions.exp: Excess glucose is first stored as glycogen in the liver (75g capacity) and muscle (250g). When glycogen stores are saturated, excess glucose is converted via acetyl-CoA to triglycerides and stored in adipose tissue.

questions.q: Which cells make up approximately 95% of the alveolar surface area but only about 40% of alveolar cells?
opts: A. Type I pneumocytes
B. Type II pneumocytes
C. Type III pneumocytes (macrophages)
D. Capillary endothelial cells
E. Clara cells
F. Option F
questions.exp: Type I pneumocytes are squamous-shaped, ideal for gas exchange, and cover 95% of the alveolar surface area despite being only ~40% of alveolar cells. Type II pneumocytes (B) are cuboidal, produce surfactant, and make up 60% of cells but only 5% of surface area.

questions.q: Which alveolar cell type produces surfactant?
opts: A. Type I pneumocyte
B. Type II pneumocyte
C. Alveolar macrophage
D. Capillary endothelium
E. Goblet cell
F. Option F
questions.exp: Type II pneumocytes produce surfactant, which allows O2 and CO2 to dissolve into it, reduces surface tension of alveolar fluid, and prevents alveolar collapse during expiration. They also replenish Type I cells when they die.

questions.q: What is the primary role of surfactant in the alveoli?
opts: A. Immune defence against pathogens
B. Reduces surface tension and prevents alveolar collapse
C. Increases alveolar surface area
D. Facilitates direct diffusion of nitrogen
E. Activates stretch receptors
F. Option F
questions.exp: Surfactant reduces the surface tension of alveolar fluid, preventing collapse during expiration. It also allows O2 and CO2 to dissolve into it, aiding gas exchange. Without it, alveoli would collapse on each expiration (as in neonatal respiratory distress syndrome).

questions.q: What is the correct order of layers in the respiratory exchange membrane from alveolus to capillary?
opts: A. Alveolar epithelium → shared basal membrane → capillary epithelium
B. Capillary epithelium → surfactant layer → alveolar epithelium
C. Alveolar epithelium → capillary endothelium → basal membrane
D. Shared basal membrane → alveolar epithelium → capillary epithelium
E. Surfactant → alveolar macrophages → capillary endothelium
F. Option F
questions.exp: The respiratory exchange membrane has three layers: Layer 1 — alveolar epithelium (Type I/II pneumocytes with surfactant); Layer 2 — shared basal membrane (connective tissue, fluid, collagen); Layer 3 — capillary epithelium (simple squamous for optimal gas exchange).

questions.q: Alveolar macrophages (Type III cells) are unique in that they:
opts: A. Produce surfactant in emergencies
B. Form the primary gas exchange surface
C. Can move between alveoli and perform phagocytosis
D. Are the most numerous alveolar cell type
E. Secrete mucus to trap pathogens
F. Option F
questions.exp: Alveolar macrophages (Type III cells) are motile and can move between alveoli. Their primary function is phagocytosis — removing inhaled particles, bacteria, and debris from the alveolar surface.

questions.q: The approximate partial pressure of O2 in the alveoli is:
opts: A. 40 mmHg
B. 45 mmHg
C. 104 mmHg
D. 158 mmHg
E. 597 mmHg
F. Option F
questions.exp: Alveolar O2 partial pressure is approximately 104 mmHg. This is higher than deoxygenated blood entering the pulmonary capillaries (~40 mmHg), creating a gradient that drives O2 into the blood. Atmospheric O2 is ~158 mmHg but is diluted by humidification and CO2.

questions.q: The partial pressure of CO2 in deoxygenated blood entering the pulmonary capillaries is approximately:
opts: A. 40 mmHg
B. 45 mmHg
C. 60 mmHg
D. 104 mmHg
E. 20 mmHg
F. Option F
questions.exp: Deoxygenated venous blood entering the pulmonary capillaries has a CO2 partial pressure of ~45 mmHg. Alveolar CO2 is ~40 mmHg, so the gradient drives CO2 out of the blood into the alveoli. Despite this small gradient, CO2 is 20x more soluble than O2, equalising transfer rates.

questions.q: Why does CO2 transfer across the respiratory membrane efficiently despite having a much smaller partial pressure gradient than O2?
opts: A. CO2 molecules are smaller than O2
B. CO2 has approximately 20x greater solubility than O2
C. CO2 uses active transport
D. CO2 binds preferentially to Type I pneumocytes
E. CO2 has higher atmospheric partial pressure
F. Option F
questions.exp: CO2 is approximately 20x more soluble than O2 in aqueous solution. This means that despite a much smaller partial pressure gradient (5 mmHg vs 64 mmHg for O2), the amount of CO2 that crosses the membrane is similar to O2. CO2's dipole structure also contributes to its high solubility.

questions.q: Arterial Oxygen Delivery (DO2) is calculated as:
opts: A. DO2 = SpO2 × PaO2
B. DO2 = CO × CaO2
C. DO2 = Hb × 1.34 × PaO2
D. DO2 = SV × PaO2 / Hb
E. DO2 = CO / CaO2
F. Option F
questions.exp: DO2 = Cardiac Output (CO) × Arterial Oxygen Content (CaO2). CaO2 = (1.34 × SpO2 × Hb concentration) + (0.003 × PaO2). This reflects both how much blood is pumped (CO) and how much O2 each unit of blood carries (CaO2).

questions.q: The Alveolar Gas Equation is PAO2 = PiO2 − PaCO2/R. What does 'R' represent?
opts: A. Respiratory rate
B. The ratio of CO2 produced to O2 consumed (respiratory quotient)
C. The resistance of the airways
D. The gas constant in thermodynamics
E. The recoil coefficient of alveolar walls
F. Option F
questions.exp: R is the respiratory quotient — the ratio of CO2 produced to O2 consumed, which depends on diet (fats, carbohydrates, proteins). It is approximately 0.8 for a mixed diet. It accounts for the fact that we produce less CO2 than we consume O2.

questions.q: The A-a gradient refers to:
opts: A. The difference in O2 between arterial and venous blood
B. The difference between alveolar and arterial partial pressure of O2
C. The gradient driving CO2 out of the blood
D. The difference between atmospheric and alveolar O2
E. The ratio of ventilation to perfusion
F. Option F
questions.exp: The A-a gradient (Alveolar-arterial gradient) is the difference between PAO2 (calculated from the alveolar gas equation) and PaO2 (measured from arterial blood gas). A normal gradient is 5–10 mmHg. An elevated gradient suggests a pulmonary cause of hypoxaemia (V/Q mismatch, shunt), while a normal gradient suggests hypoventilation.

questions.q: A patient presents with hypoxaemia. Their A-a gradient is normal. The most likely cause is:
opts: A. Pulmonary embolism
B. Pneumonia causing a shunt
C. Hypoventilation
D. V/Q mismatch
E. Pulmonary fibrosis
F. Option F
questions.exp: A normal A-a gradient with hypoxaemia suggests hypoventilation (e.g. from opioid overdose, neuromuscular disease, obesity hypoventilation). The alveoli are underventilated but functioning normally, so no barrier to gas exchange exists. An elevated A-a gradient points to intrinsic lung pathology.

questions.q: V/Q mismatch refers to a mismatch between:
opts: A. Ventricular output and pulmonary resistance
B. Alveolar ventilation (V) and alveolar perfusion (Q)
C. Vital capacity and tidal volume
D. Venous and arterial O2 content
E. Vascular tone and gas solubility
F. Option F
questions.exp: V/Q mismatch refers to a mismatch between alveolar ventilation (V) and alveolar perfusion (Q). Ideally, V/Q = 1.0; normal is ~0.8. Low V/Q means perfusion exceeds ventilation (wasted perfusion). High V/Q means ventilation exceeds perfusion (wasted ventilation). V/Q mismatch is the most common cause of hypoxaemia.

questions.q: In a normal upright lung, how are ventilation and perfusion distributed?
opts: A. Both are greater at the apex
B. Both are greater at the base
C. Ventilation is greater at the apex; perfusion is greater at the base
D. Ventilation is greater at the base; perfusion is greater at the apex
E. They are uniformly distributed throughout the lung
F. Option F
questions.exp: In an upright lung, ventilation is better at the top (apex) and perfusion (blood flow) is better at the bottom (base) due to gravity. This creates a V/Q ratio of ~3 at the apex and ~0.6 at the base. The normal overall V/Q is ~0.8.

questions.q: Which of the following best describes a pulmonary 'shunt'?
opts: A. Blood bypasses the right ventricle
B. Alveoli that are ventilated but not perfused
C. Alveoli that are perfused but filled/blocked, preventing gas exchange
D. Excessive ventilation of upper lung zones
E. Hyperventilation of healthy alveoli
F. Option F
questions.exp: A shunt occurs when alveoli are perfused but cannot participate in gas exchange because they are filled (with blood, water/oedema, or pus from infection). Blood passes through without being oxygenated. This differs from dead space, where alveoli are ventilated but not perfused.

questions.q: What percentage of oxygen is transported dissolved in blood plasma?
opts: F. Option F
questions.exp: Only ~1.5% of oxygen is dissolved in blood plasma due to O2's low solubility in aqueous solution. The vast majority (~98%) is transported bound to haemoglobin as oxyhaemoglobin (HbO2). This is why anaemia profoundly reduces oxygen-carrying capacity.

questions.q: What percentage of oxygen is transported bound to haemoglobin?
opts: F. Option F
questions.exp: Approximately 98% of oxygen in the blood is transported bound to haemoglobin as oxyhaemoglobin (Hb + O2 ↔ HbO2). This reversible reaction allows efficient loading at the lungs (high PO2) and unloading at the tissues (low PO2).

questions.q: What percentage of CO2 is transported as bicarbonate (HCO3−) in plasma?
opts: F. Option F
questions.exp: Approximately 70% of CO2 is transported as bicarbonate (HCO3−) in plasma. CO2 diffuses into erythrocytes, where carbonic anhydrase converts it to H2CO3, which rapidly dissociates to H+ and HCO3−. Bicarbonate then diffuses out of the RBC into the plasma (chloride shift).

questions.q: What percentage of CO2 is transported as carbaminohaemoglobin?
opts: F. Option F
questions.exp: Approximately 20% of CO2 is transported as carbaminohaemoglobin (CO2 + Hb ↔ HbCO2). CO2 binds to amino acid moieties (not the iron atom) on the globin portion of haemoglobin. This gives deoxygenated blood its characteristic blueish-purple colour.

questions.q: The enzyme carbonic anhydrase in erythrocytes is how many times more effective than in plasma?
opts: E. The same effectiveness
F. Option F
questions.exp: Carbonic anhydrase in erythrocytes catalyses the conversion of CO2 + H2O → H2CO3 → H+ + HCO3−, and is approximately 5,000× more effective than in plasma. This is why bicarbonate production is so efficient in RBCs, enabling CO2 to be transported rapidly away from tissues.

questions.q: During the chloride shift in peripheral tissues, which ion moves INTO the erythrocyte?
opts: A. Sodium (Na+)
B. Bicarbonate (HCO3−)
C. Chloride (Cl−)
D. Potassium (K+)
E. Hydrogen (H+)
F. Option F
questions.exp: In peripheral tissues, bicarbonate (HCO3−) accumulates in the RBC and diffuses OUT into plasma down its concentration gradient. To maintain electrical neutrality, chloride (Cl−) shifts INTO the RBC. At the alveoli, the process reverses — carbonic anhydrase drives CO2 production, pulling HCO3− back in and pushing Cl− out.

questions.q: What is the clinical significance of the chloride shift?
opts: A. It increases haemoglobin affinity for O2
B. It is essential for the CO2-carrying capacity of blood by allowing bicarbonate to be distributed throughout plasma
C. It prevents lactic acid accumulation
D. It activates peripheral chemoreceptors
E. It increases surfactant production
F. Option F
questions.exp: The chloride shift is essential because it allows bicarbonate formed inside RBCs to be exported into the plasma, distributing CO2 transport across the entire blood volume (not just intracellularly). This effectively makes the RBC a 'bicarbonate factory,' dramatically increasing CO2 carrying capacity.

questions.q: Haemoglobin consists of how many protein subunits?
opts: F. Option F
questions.exp: Haemoglobin has 4 subunits arranged in a quaternary structure: 2 alpha and 2 beta protein chains, each containing an iron-containing porphyrin (heme) group. Each subunit can bind one O2 molecule, giving a maximum of 4 O2 per Hb molecule. One erythrocyte can hold approximately 300,000 Hb molecules.

questions.q: How many oxygen molecules can one haemoglobin molecule carry?
opts: F. Option F
questions.exp: One haemoglobin molecule has 4 heme groups, each containing an iron atom (Fe2+) that can bind one O2 molecule, giving a maximum of 4 O2 per haemoglobin. One erythrocyte contains ~300,000 haemoglobin molecules.

questions.q: The 'taut' (T) state of haemoglobin is associated with:
opts: A. High affinity for O2, promoting O2 binding
B. Low affinity for O2, promoting O2 release
C. High affinity for CO2, promoting CO2 binding at the lungs
D. Relaxed configuration after first O2 binding
E. Increased affinity seen in alkalosis
F. Option F
questions.exp: The T (taut/tense) state of haemoglobin has low O2 affinity, promoting O2 release. The R (relaxed) state has high O2 affinity, promoting O2 binding. When the first O2 binds, a conformational change from T→R occurs, making subsequent O2 binding easier (cooperative binding). Loss of the first O2 triggers R→T, facilitating further release.

questions.q: Cooperative binding of oxygen to haemoglobin means:
opts: A. All 4 O2 bind simultaneously
B. Binding of one O2 makes subsequent O2 binding easier due to conformational change
C. Haemoglobin competes with myoglobin for O2
D. O2 and CO2 bind to the same site
E. All subunits must lose O2 before any is released
F. Option F
questions.exp: Cooperative binding means that when the first O2 binds to haemoglobin, it causes a T→R conformational change that increases affinity for subsequent O2 molecules. This creates the characteristic sigmoid shape of the O2 dissociation curve. The same applies to O2 unloading — losing one O2 triggers R→T, facilitating further release.

questions.q: Myoglobin differs from haemoglobin in that it:
opts: A. Contains 4 iron atoms
B. Is found primarily in plasma
C. Is a monomeric protein that binds only one O2
D. Has a sigmoid dissociation curve
E. Can carry CO2 as carbamino compounds
F. Option F
questions.exp: Myoglobin is a monomeric protein (single polypeptide in a tertiary alpha helix structure) that can bind only 1 oxygen molecule. It is found in muscle (cardiac myocytes and oxidative skeletal muscle). It has a hyperbolic dissociation curve (not sigmoid) and higher O2 affinity than haemoglobin.

questions.q: On the oxygen-haemoglobin dissociation curve, a RIGHT shift indicates:
opts: A. Increased haemoglobin affinity for O2
B. Decreased haemoglobin affinity for O2, promoting O2 unloading to tissues
C. Increased O2 loading in the lungs
D. Decreased H+ concentration
E. Decreased temperature
F. Option F
questions.exp: A right shift of the O2 dissociation curve means decreased haemoglobin affinity for O2 — for any given PO2, less O2 is bound to haemoglobin, promoting O2 unloading into peripheral tissues. Right shift is caused by: increased H+, increased temperature, increased 2,3-DPG, and increased CO2 (Bohr effect).

questions.q: Which of the following causes a LEFT shift of the oxygen-haemoglobin dissociation curve?
opts: A. Increased H+
B. Increased temperature
C. Increased 2,3-DPG
D. Decreased H+ (alkalosis)
E. Increased CO2
F. Option F
questions.exp: A left shift (increased O2 affinity) is caused by decreased H+, decreased temperature, and decreased 2,3-DPG. Left shift enhances O2 loading in the lungs but impairs O2 unloading to peripheral tissues. Examples: fetal haemoglobin (HbF) has higher affinity, alkalosis, hypothermia, carbon monoxide poisoning.

questions.q: 2,3-Diphosphoglycerate (2,3-DPG) shifts the O2 dissociation curve to the right by:
opts: A. Directly binding to the iron atom in haem
B. Binding to deoxyhemoglobin in an electrostatic pocket between 2 beta-globin chains, stabilising the T state
C. Activating carbonic anhydrase
D. Increasing plasma pH
E. Reducing CO2 production
F. Option F
questions.exp: 2,3-DPG is a byproduct of glycolysis in RBCs (which lack mitochondria). It binds to deoxyhemoglobin in an electrostatic pocket between the 2 beta-globin chains, stabilising the T (taut) state and reducing O2 affinity, thereby shifting the curve right and promoting O2 release. Elevated in high-altitude adaptation, anaemia, and chronic hypoxia.

questions.q: The upper flat portion of the O2 dissociation curve (>60 mmHg) represents:
opts: A. T state with low O2 affinity
B. Cooperative binding causing rapid O2 uptake
C. A safety margin where PaO2 can drop significantly without major saturation loss
D. Complete O2 unloading to tissues
E. Maximal effect of 2,3-DPG
F. Option F
questions.exp: The upper flat portion (>60 mmHg) is a safety margin — even if PaO2 drops considerably (e.g. at altitude or with mild lung disease), SpO2 remains high because most haemoglobin is already saturated. In this region, most haemoglobin is bound, so less remains to take up additional O2.

questions.q: The steep middle portion of the O2 dissociation curve (20–60 mmHg) represents:
opts: A. The T state with very low affinity
B. Efficient O2 unloading due to cooperative binding and conformational changes
C. The plateau of nearly complete saturation
D. The threshold for peripheral chemoreceptor activation
E. Maximal CO2 solubility
F. Option F
questions.exp: The steep middle portion (20–60 mmHg) reflects cooperative binding: once one O2 is released, the R→T conformational change promotes further release, causing rapid changes in saturation with small PO2 changes. This enables efficient O2 delivery to tissues where PO2 is low (typically 10–40 mmHg).

questions.q: The Bohr effect describes how increased CO2 affects haemoglobin by:
opts: A. Increasing O2 affinity and shifting the curve left
B. Directly displacing O2 from haem iron
C. Lowering pH (increasing H+), converting haemoglobin to the T state and promoting O2 release
D. Increasing 2,3-DPG production immediately
E. Reducing carbonic anhydrase activity
F. Option F
questions.exp: The Bohr effect: increased CO2 in tissues → increased H+ (lower pH) → CO2 and H+ bind to haemoglobin → conformational change to T state → decreased O2 affinity → O2 released to tissues. In the lungs, CO2 is exhaled → pH rises → shift to R state → increased O2 loading. Accounts for ~20% of O2 exchange.

questions.q: The Haldane effect states that:
opts: A. CO2 increases O2 affinity
B. Oxygenation of haemoglobin reduces its capacity to carry CO2
C. Increased temperature shifts the curve left
D. CO2 binds to the iron atom in haem
E. Fetal haemoglobin has lower O2 affinity than adult haemoglobin
F. Option F
questions.exp: The Haldane effect: oxygenation of Hb (at the lungs) reduces its affinity for CO2, causing CO2 to be released for exhalation. In tissues, deoxygenation of Hb increases its CO2 affinity, allowing more CO2 to be picked up. The Haldane effect accounts for ~50% of CO2 exchange in lungs and tissues.

questions.q: Together, the Bohr and Haldane effects produce which clinically important outcome?
opts: A. O2 and CO2 both bind Hb simultaneously at all sites
B. O2 promotes CO2 offloading; CO2 promotes O2 offloading — together facilitating gas exchange at both lungs and tissues
C. Increased surfactant production
D. Arterial vasoconstriction
E. Activation of J receptors
F. Option F
questions.exp: Together: O2 causes CO2 offloading (Haldane); CO2 causes O2 offloading (Bohr). At pulmonary capillaries, O2 binding releases CO2 for exhalation. At tissue capillaries, CO2 uptake facilitates O2 release. This elegant coupling maximises gas exchange efficiency at both sites.

questions.q: PaO2 refers to:
opts: A. The % of O2 molecules bound to haemoglobin
B. The O2 molecules dissolved in arterial plasma
C. Total O2 content of arterial blood
D. Alveolar partial pressure of O2
E. Mixed venous O2 tension
F. Option F
questions.exp: PaO2 is the partial pressure of O2 dissolved in arterial plasma (measured directly by arterial blood gas). Normal range is 75–100 mmHg. SpO2 is different — it measures the % of haemoglobin binding sites occupied by O2 (saturation), measured by pulse oximetry.

questions.q: SpO2 as measured by pulse oximetry is:
opts: A. The partial pressure of dissolved O2 in plasma
B. The total O2 content of blood in ml/dL
C. The percentage of haemoglobin binding sites occupied by O2
D. The alveolar O2 partial pressure
E. Cardiac output × O2 content
F. Option F
questions.exp: SpO2 measures the percentage of haemoglobin binding sites occupied by O2 (oxygen saturation). Pulse oximetry uses the fact that oxyhaemoglobin and deoxyhaemoglobin absorb light (LED) at different wavelengths. Normal SpO2 is 95–99%. It differs from PaO2, which is dissolved plasma O2.

questions.q: A significant limitation of pulse oximetry is that:
opts: A. It cannot detect any O2 in severely anaemic patients
B. It measures PaO2 instead of SpO2
C. Carboxyhaemoglobin (CO poisoning) can produce a falsely normal reading
D. It is inaccurate above SpO2 of 90%
E. It requires arterial blood sampling
F. Option F
questions.exp: Carboxyhaemoglobin (HbCO, from CO poisoning) absorbs light similarly to oxyhaemoglobin, so pulse oximetry can show a falsely normal SpO2 while the patient is severely hypoxic. Other limitations: overestimates in darker skin tones, inaccurate with poor perfusion, thick nail polish.

questions.q: The oxygen content of arterial blood (CaO2) is calculated as:
opts: A. CaO2 = Hb × PaO2 × 0.003
C. CaO2 = CO × SpO2
D. CaO2 = PAO2 − PaO2
E. CaO2 = Hb × 4 / RBC count
F. Option F
questions.exp: CaO2 = (1.34 × SpO2 × Hb concentration) + (0.003 × PaO2). The 1.34 ml/g represents the O2 carrying capacity of haemoglobin. With normal Hb of 15g/100ml and SpO2 of 99%: CaO2 ≈ (1.34 × 0.99 × 15) + (0.003 × 100) ≈ 19.9 + 0.3 ≈ 20.2 ml/dL.

questions.q: Central cyanosis occurs due to:
opts: A. Poor peripheral circulation with normal lung function
B. Inadequate oxygenation of blood in the lungs
C. Excessive 2,3-DPG production
D. Carbon monoxide poisoning
E. Elevated SpO2 above 99%
F. Option F
questions.exp: Central cyanosis (bluish discolouration of lips, tongue, mucous membranes) results from inadequate oxygenation of blood in the lungs — causing systemic hypoxaemia. Causes include severe respiratory disease, cardiac shunts. Peripheral cyanosis (fingertips, extremities) results from poor peripheral circulation with adequate central oxygenation.

questions.q: Peripheral cyanosis differs from central cyanosis in that:
opts: A. It affects the tongue and mucous membranes equally
B. It is caused by inadequate lung oxygenation
C. It results from poor circulation to the extremities with normal lung function
D. It indicates higher SpO2 than central cyanosis
E. It only occurs in CO poisoning
F. Option F
questions.exp: Peripheral cyanosis affects the extremities (fingertips, lips) due to poor circulation — increased O2 extraction in poorly perfused tissues causes more deoxyhaemoglobin locally. Central oxygenation (SpO2) is normal. Causes include heart failure, Raynaud's phenomenon, cold exposure. Central cyanosis affects mucous membranes and indicates systemic hypoxaemia.

questions.q: The porphyrin ring in haemoglobin contains which metal-binding atoms essential for O2 binding?
opts: A. Zinc atoms
B. Copper atoms
C. Nitrogen atoms that coordinate iron (Fe2+)
D. Magnesium atoms
E. Calcium atoms
F. Option F
questions.exp: The porphyrin ring is a heterocyclic compound containing 4 nitrogen atoms. These nitrogens are coordination sites for iron (Fe2+) — the central metal ion. The iron is held in a hydrophobic pocket of the globin protein, and the heme binding site is specifically designed for O2. CO has 210× higher affinity for this site than O2.

questions.q: Carbon monoxide (CO) poisoning is dangerous because:
opts: A. CO directly destroys surfactant
B. CO has 210× higher affinity for haemoglobin than O2, and HbCO binding prevents O2 release to tissues
C. CO activates peripheral chemoreceptors to cause hyperventilation
D. CO causes irreversible damage to carbonic anhydrase
E. CO shifts the dissociation curve right
F. Option F
questions.exp: CO has approximately 210× higher affinity for haemoglobin than O2. HbCO binding also causes a conformational change (shift to R state) that prevents O2 molecules on other subunits from being released to tissues. Pulse oximetry falsely reads HbCO as HbO2 — making CO poisoning dangerous and easily missed.

questions.q: The respiratory control centre's rhythm-generating function is primarily located in the:
opts: A. Apneustic centre of the pons
B. Pneumotaxic centre of the upper pons
C. Ventral Respiratory Group (VRG) of the medulla
D. Pre-Bötzinger complex (pacemaker cells) of the medulla/pons
E. Cerebral motor cortex
F. Option F
questions.exp: The Pre-Bötzinger complex contains pacemaker cells in the pons and medulla that generate the basic respiratory rhythm. The Dorsal Respiratory Group (DRG) integrates sensory input; the VRG controls expiration; the Pneumotaxic centre regulates rate/depth; and the Apneustic centre stimulates deep inspiration.

questions.q: The Dorsal Respiratory Group (DRG) of the medulla primarily:
opts: A. Controls the expiratory muscles
B. Sets respiratory depth via the apneustic centre
C. Integrates input from stretch receptors and peripheral chemoreceptors
D. Generates the basic respiratory rhythm
E. Inhibits all respiratory neurons during expiration
F. Option F
questions.exp: The Dorsal Respiratory Group (DRG) in the medulla integrates afferent input from stretch receptors (lungs) and peripheral chemoreceptors (carotid/aortic bodies). It primarily controls inspiration. The Ventral Respiratory Group (VRG) controls expiration and rhythm.

questions.q: The Pneumotaxic Centre in the upper pons functions to:
opts: A. Stimulate long, deep inspirations
B. Inhibit DRG neurons to regulate respiratory rate and depth
C. Generate the basic respiratory rhythm
D. Respond to peripheral chemoreceptors
E. Control expiratory muscle contraction
F. Option F
questions.exp: The Pneumotaxic Centre in the upper pons inhibits DRG neurons, limiting inspiration and regulating the overall respiratory rate and depth. This prevents overinflation (apneusis). The Apneustic Centre in the lower pons has the opposite effect — it stimulates long, sustained inspirations.

questions.q: The Apneustic Centre in the lower pons functions to:
opts: A. Inhibit inspiration to prevent overinflation
B. Regulate expiratory rate
C. Stimulate long and deep inspiration
D. Detect CO2 levels in cerebrospinal fluid
E. Coordinate voluntary breathing with speech
F. Option F
questions.exp: The Apneustic Centre in the lower pons stimulates long and deep inspirations. It is normally inhibited by the Pneumotaxic Centre. Damage to the Pneumotaxic Centre results in apneustic breathing (prolonged inspiratory gasps) due to unchecked Apneustic Centre activity.

questions.q: Central chemoreceptors respond primarily to:
opts: A. Dissolved arterial O2 (PaO2)
B. CO2 that diffuses across the blood-brain barrier, altering CSF pH
C. Peripheral vascular resistance
D. SpO2 changes below 95%
E. Plasma bicarbonate directly
F. Option F
questions.exp: Central chemoreceptors (in the medulla) are NOT stimulated by PO2. They respond to CO2 that diffuses across the blood-brain barrier, lowering CSF pH (increasing H+). They are the primary driver of respiratory rate and depth. Central chemoreceptors are therefore primarily pH/CO2 sensors.

questions.q: Peripheral chemoreceptors (carotid and aortic bodies) are primarily stimulated by:
opts: A. CSF pH changes only
B. Dissolved arterial O2 tension (PaO2)
C. CO2 directly without pH change
D. SpO2 above 95%
E. Bicarbonate concentrations
F. Option F
questions.exp: Peripheral chemoreceptors (in the carotid and aortic bodies) respond to dissolved arterial O2 tension (PaO2). Importantly, PaO2 must drop significantly — to around 60 mmHg — before peripheral chemoreceptors drive a meaningful ventilatory response. Afferents travel via the vagus and glossopharyngeal nerves to the Nucleus Tractus Solitarius.

questions.q: The Hering-Breuer Inflation Reflex prevents overinflation by:
opts: A. Activating J receptors in alveolar walls when pulmonary capillaries engorge
B. Stretch receptors in bronchial smooth muscle sending vagal afferent signals to limit further inspiration
C. Peripheral chemoreceptors detecting high PaO2
D. Central chemoreceptors detecting low CSF pH
E. The apneustic centre inhibiting the DRG
F. Option F
questions.exp: The Hering-Breuer Inflation Reflex: stretch receptors in bronchial/bronchiolar smooth muscle detect overinflation → vagal afferent feedback to the Dorsal Respiratory Group → limits further inspiration. The complementary Deflation Reflex: understretched receptors stimulate more inspiration when lung volume falls too low.

questions.q: J (juxtacapillary) receptors in alveolar walls are stimulated by:
opts: A. High PaO2 during exercise
B. Pulmonary capillary engorgement and fluid in alveoli (e.g. heart failure)
C. High CO2 in CSF
D. Excessive mucus secretion
E. Stretch during deep inspiration
F. Option F
questions.exp: J receptors (juxtacapillary receptors) are sensory endings of the vagus nerve in alveolar walls. They are stimulated when pulmonary capillaries become engorged (e.g. left heart failure) or when fluid accumulates in the alveoli. Stimulation causes the sensation of dyspnoea (difficulty breathing).

questions.q: Bronchiole diameter is sensitive to CO2 such that:
opts: A. High CO2 causes bronchoconstriction, diverting air away
B. High CO2 causes bronchodilation, allowing faster CO2 expulsion and pH regulation
C. Low CO2 causes bronchodilation to increase ventilation
D. CO2 has no direct effect on airway diameter
E. CO2 activates J receptors to constrict airways
F. Option F
questions.exp: Increased CO2 in the airways causes bronchodilation, allowing CO2 to be expelled more rapidly and restoring pH. This is part of local V/Q matching — well-ventilated alveoli (low CO2) experience mild bronchoconstriction, while poorly ventilated alveoli with high CO2 receive bronchodilation to improve airflow.

questions.q: During the respiratory exam, percussion note 'Stony Dull' most suggests:
opts: A. Normal lung tissue
B. Hyperinflated lung (e.g. emphysema)
C. Pleural effusion (fluid in pleural space)
D. Pneumothorax
E. Consolidation from pneumonia
F. Option F
questions.exp: Percussion notes: Resonant = normal lung; Dull = consolidation or mild effusion; Stony Dull = large pleural effusion (fluid); Hyper-resonant = pneumothorax or emphysema. Stony dull is the dullest note and is characteristic of large amounts of fluid (pleural effusion, haemothorax).

questions.q: On auscultation, bronchial breath sounds are characterised by:
opts: A. Expiration longer than inspiration (2:3 ratio)
B. Equal inspiration and expiration (1:1 ratio)
C. Inspiration much longer than expiration
D. Absence of all breath sounds
E. High-pitched wheeze throughout the cycle
F. Option F
questions.exp: Bronchial breath sounds have a 1:1 inspiration:expiration ratio — equal in length and volume, often with a pause between. They are normally heard over the trachea/large bronchi. When heard over peripheral lung (where vesicular breathing is normal), bronchial sounds indicate consolidation (lung tissue becoming solid, transmitting sound better).

questions.q: Normal (vesicular) breath sounds are characterised by:
opts: A. 1:1 inspiration to expiration
B. Expiration equal to inspiration throughout
C. Inspiration 2:3 relative to expiration
D. Only heard at the trachea
E. A harsh, high-pitched quality
F. Option F
questions.exp: Normal vesicular breath sounds have inspiration:expiration ratio of approximately 2:3 — inspiration is audible for longer, expiration trails off softly. They are heard over peripheral lung tissue. Bronchovesicular sounds (1:1, softer) are heard over the large airways and between scapulae.

questions.q: What does Tactile Vocal Fremitus (or vocal resonance) assess?
opts: A. Tracheal deviation
B. Chest expansion symmetry
C. Transmission of voice through lung tissue to the chest wall
D. Percussion note quality
E. Respiratory rate variability
F. Option F
questions.exp: Tactile Vocal Fremitus assesses how well voice vibrations (patient says '99') transmit through lung tissue to the chest wall. Increased fremitus = consolidation (dense tissue conducts sound better). Decreased/absent fremitus = pleural effusion, pneumothorax, or emphysema (air/fluid blocks transmission).

questions.q: When examining percussion and auscultation, the clinician should start on the 'normal side' because:
opts: A. The right lung always has greater volume
B. Left-sided pathology is more common
C. Starting on the contralateral (unaffected) side provides a baseline to compare against the abnormal side
D. Percussion notes are louder on the right
E. Vocal fremitus is always stronger on the left
F. Option F
questions.exp: Starting on the 'normal' (contralateral) side allows the examiner to establish a baseline resonance and breath sound quality first, then compare with the potentially abnormal side. This is fundamental to respiratory examination — differences between sides are more informative than absolute findings.

questions.q: Which of the following raw materials is NOT required for erythrocyte production?
opts: A. Folate
B. Vitamin B12
C. Iron
D. Copper
E. Vitamin D
F. Option F
questions.exp: Erythrocyte production requires: folate, B12, B6, copper, glucose, lipids, and amino acids. Iron is essential for haem synthesis. Vitamin D is not a specific requirement for erythropoiesis. Approximately 2 million erythrocytes are produced every second in healthy adults.

questions.q: The normal respiratory quotient (R) value for a mixed diet is approximately:
opts: F. Option F
questions.exp: The respiratory quotient (RQ/R) is the ratio of CO2 produced to O2 consumed. For a mixed diet, R ≈ 0.8. Pure fat metabolism gives RQ ≈ 0.7; pure carbohydrate gives 1.0. This value is used in the alveolar gas equation (PAO2 = PiO2 − PaCO2/R) to estimate alveolar O2.

questions.q: Tissue hypoxia occurs when:
opts: A. SpO2 exceeds 99%
B. O2 delivery falls or tissues cannot consume O2 adequately
C. CO2 is below 35 mmHg
D. Haemoglobin rises above 15 g/dL
E. V/Q ratio exceeds 1.0
F. Option F
questions.exp: Tissue hypoxia occurs when O2 delivery (DO2 = CO × CaO2) falls below tissue O2 demand, or when tissues cannot extract/utilise O2 (e.g. cyanide poisoning, mitochondrial dysfunction). SpO2 alone doesn't predict tissue hypoxia — anaemia (low Hb) or low cardiac output can cause tissue hypoxia despite normal SpO2.

questions.q: In internal respiration (gas exchange at tissues), the PO2 in peripheral tissues is approximately:
opts: A. 100 mmHg
B. 104 mmHg
C. 45 mmHg
D. 40 mmHg
E. 10 mmHg
F. Option F
questions.exp: Peripheral tissues have a PO2 of approximately 40 mmHg at rest (can drop to 10 mmHg in highly active tissue), versus arterial blood at ~100 mmHg. This gradient drives O2 diffusion from capillaries to tissues. CO2 in tissues is ~45 mmHg (blood arriving is ~40 mmHg), driving CO2 into the blood.

questions.q: External respiration refers to:
opts: A. Gas exchange between blood and peripheral tissues
B. Gas exchange between alveoli and pulmonary capillaries
C. Voluntary control of breathing
D. The role of carbonic anhydrase in RBCs
E. Diffusion of CO2 across the blood-brain barrier
F. Option F
questions.exp: External respiration is gas exchange between the alveoli and pulmonary capillaries — O2 enters the blood and CO2 is exhaled. Internal respiration is gas exchange between blood and peripheral tissues. External respiration depends on the alveolar-capillary partial pressure gradients and the respiratory membrane's permeability.

questions.q: Pulmonary arteries carry which type of blood to the lungs?
opts: A. Oxygenated blood
B. Deoxygenated blood
C. Blood rich in bicarbonate only
D. Blood with high PO2
E. Filtered plasma only
F. Option F
questions.exp: The pulmonary arteries are unique — they carry deoxygenated blood from the right ventricle to the lungs, where it undergoes gas exchange at the respiratory membrane. The pulmonary veins then return oxygenated blood to the left atrium. This is opposite to systemic circulation where arteries carry oxygenated blood.

questions.q: Which type of epithelium lines the capillaries of the respiratory membrane, and why is it important?
opts: A. Pseudostratified columnar — secretes mucus for gas solubility
B. Cuboidal — produces surfactant
C. Simple squamous — thin for optimal/rapid gas exchange
D. Stratified squamous — provides mechanical protection
E. Transitional — allows volume changes
F. Option F
questions.exp: The capillary epithelium of the respiratory membrane is simple squamous epithelium — a single layer of flat cells. This minimises diffusion distance, allowing rapid, efficient gas exchange. The alveolar surface (Type I pneumocytes) is also squamous for the same reason. Together with the thin shared basal membrane, the total diffusion distance is only ~0.5 micrometres.
