questions.q: In the alveolar gas composition, compared to atmospheric air, alveolar air has:
opts: A. Higher O2 and lower CO2
B. The same composition as atmospheric air
C. Lower O2 and higher CO2 due to gas exchange and humidification
D. Higher N2 and lower water vapour
E. Lower CO2 than atmospheric air
F. Option F
questions.exp: Alveolar air has lower O2 (~104 mmHg vs 158 mmHg atmospheric) and higher CO2 (~40 mmHg vs 3 mmHg atmospheric) due to: gas exchange (O2 absorbed, CO2 added), dilution with dead space gas, and humidification (water vapour adds ~47 mmHg, diluting other gases).

questions.q: Bronchodilation in response to CO2 in the airway serves to:
opts: A. Increase CO2 retention in the alveoli
B. Divert blood flow away from well-ventilated alveoli
C. Allow faster expulsion of CO2 and regulate pH
D. Stimulate peripheral chemoreceptors
E. Activate J receptors to signal dyspnoea
F. Option F
questions.exp: Local CO2-induced bronchodilation is a form of autoregulation — poorly ventilated alveoli accumulate CO2, causing local bronchodilation to improve airflow to those alveoli, expel CO2 faster, and restore pH. This is part of ventilation-perfusion matching: airways dilate where CO2 is high to match better blood flow.

questions.q: Which of the following correctly describes V/Q ratios across the upright lung?
opts: A. V/Q is highest at the base because blood flow is highest there
B. V/Q is highest at the apex because ventilation is relatively higher and perfusion is lower there
C. V/Q is uniform throughout the lung at 0.8
D. V/Q is highest at the base because ventilation is greatest there
E. V/Q is lowest at the apex due to high perfusion
F. Option F
questions.exp: In an upright lung: ventilation is better at the apex (alveoli are more inflated), while perfusion is better at the base (gravity). This gives a high V/Q (~3) at the apex and a low V/Q (~0.6) at the base. The overall mean V/Q is ~0.8. The apex is prone to dead space effect; the base is prone to shunt effect.

questions.q: Haemoglobin saturation (SpO2) is defined as:
opts: A. The partial pressure of O2 in arterial blood
B. The % of haemoglobin binding sites occupied by O2 out of the maximum available
C. Total O2 content in ml/dL
D. The ratio of HbCO to HbO2
E. The difference between alveolar and arterial O2
F. Option F
questions.exp: Haemoglobin saturation is the % of O2 binding sites on haemoglobin that are occupied by O2, out of the total available. Normal is 95–99%. SpO2 ≠ PaO2. SpO2 can be normal even with severe anaemia (low Hb) — the available haemoglobin is saturated, but O2 content (CaO2) is still low.

questions.q: How many erythrocytes are produced per second in a healthy adult?
opts: B. 2 million
C. 20 million
D. 2 billion
F. Option F
questions.exp: Approximately 2 million erythrocytes are produced every second in healthy adults — about 200 billion per day. This rate is regulated by erythropoietin (EPO) from the kidneys. RBCs have a lifespan of ~120 days and lack mitochondria, relying on glycolysis for energy (producing 2,3-DPG as a byproduct).

questions.q: The reaction CO2 + H2O ⇌ H2CO3 ⇌ H+ + HCO3− in erythrocytes is catalysed by:
opts: A. Phosphofructokinase
B. Carbaminohaemoglobin
C. Carbonic anhydrase
D. Pyruvate kinase
E. Hexokinase
F. Option F
questions.exp: Carbonic anhydrase (CA) in erythrocytes catalyses CO2 + H2O → H2CO3 → H+ + HCO3−. It is ~5,000× more effective in RBCs than in plasma. This enzyme is essential for CO2 transport as bicarbonate (70% of total CO2 transport). CA inhibitors like acetazolamide can alter acid-base balance.

questions.q: Carbaminohaemoglobin forms when CO2 binds to:
opts: A. The iron (Fe2+) atom in haem
B. Amino acid moieties on the globin portion of haemoglobin
C. 2,3-DPG binding pockets on beta-globin chains
D. Dissolved CO2 in plasma
E. Type I pneumocytes
F. Option F
questions.exp: CO2 binds to amino acid moieties (terminal amino groups) on the globin protein portion of haemoglobin — NOT to the iron atom (which binds O2). This forms carbaminohaemoglobin (HbCO2), accounting for ~20% of CO2 transport. The colour of deoxygenated blood is blueish-purple due to deoxyhaemoglobin and carbaminohaemoglobin.

questions.q: Oxygen delivery to the tissues depends on which two main variables?
opts: A. SpO2 and haemoglobin concentration only
B. Cardiac output and arterial O2 content (CaO2)
C. Respiratory rate and tidal volume
D. V/Q ratio and diffusion capacity
E. Alveolar ventilation and FiO2
F. Option F
questions.exp: DO2 = Cardiac Output (CO) × CaO2. CaO2 reflects how much O2 each unit of blood carries (haemoglobin saturation, Hb concentration, and dissolved O2). Even with normal SpO2, tissue hypoxia can occur if CO is low (cardiogenic shock) or Hb is low (severe anaemia).

questions.q: Which of the following would NOT cause a right shift of the O2-haemoglobin dissociation curve?
opts: A. Increased H+ concentration
B. Increased temperature
C. Increased 2,3-DPG
D. Increased CO2 (Bohr effect)
E. Alkalosis (decreased H+)
F. Option F
questions.exp: Alkalosis (decreased H+, increased pH) causes a LEFT shift — increased O2 affinity. Right shift causes: increased H+ (acidosis), increased temperature, increased 2,3-DPG, increased CO2. Right shift = decreased O2 affinity = O2 unloading promoted. Left shift = increased O2 affinity = O2 loading enhanced but unloading impaired.

questions.q: A patient with a normal A-a gradient and hypercapnia (elevated PaCO2) most likely has:
opts: A. Pulmonary embolism
B. Pneumonia with consolidation
C. Hypoventilation (e.g. from opioids or neuromuscular disease)
D. Pulmonary fibrosis
E. Pulmonary oedema
F. Option F
questions.exp: Normal A-a gradient + hypercapnia = hypoventilation. The lungs are functioning normally (no intrinsic lung disease), but the patient is not breathing adequately. Causes: opioid overdose, neuromuscular disease (e.g. Guillain-Barré), obesity hypoventilation, central respiratory depression.

questions.q: In the upright lung, which regions are prone to dead space ventilation?
opts: A. The lung bases, due to low V/Q
B. The lung apices, due to high V/Q (well ventilated but poorly perfused)
C. The middle zones, due to equal V/Q
D. Alveolar ducts throughout
E. The hilar regions
F. Option F
questions.exp: The lung apices have a high V/Q (well ventilated, poorly perfused) — approaching dead space (ventilation without perfusion). The bases have low V/Q (well perfused, relatively underventilated) — approaching shunt. Understanding this distribution is key for interpreting V/Q scans and positional hypoxaemia.

questions.q: The 'sigmoid' shape of the O2 dissociation curve is beneficial because:
opts: A. It means SpO2 drops immediately with small PO2 changes, alerting to hypoxia
B. The flat upper portion provides a safety margin, and the steep middle portion allows efficient O2 unloading
C. It ensures O2 is always fully saturated regardless of PO2
D. It creates a linear relationship for predictable drug dosing
E. It prevents haemoglobin from binding CO2 simultaneously
F. Option F
questions.exp: The sigmoid shape has two key advantages: (1) The flat upper portion (>60 mmHg) — SpO2 remains high even with significant PaO2 drops (safety margin at altitude, mild hypoxia); (2) The steep middle portion (20–60 mmHg) — small PO2 changes in tissues cause large O2 release (efficient unloading). Linear or hyperbolic curves would lack these properties.

questions.q: Which statement about 2,3-DPG is TRUE?
opts: A. It is produced in the TCA cycle of RBCs
B. It binds to oxyhemoglobin to increase O2 affinity
C. It is elevated in anaemia and chronic hypoxia as a compensatory mechanism
D. It shifts the O2 dissociation curve to the left
E. It binds to the iron atom in haem
F. Option F
questions.exp: 2,3-DPG is a byproduct of glycolysis in RBCs (which lack mitochondria, not TCA cycle). It is elevated in anaemia, chronic hypoxia (high altitude), and lung disease as a compensatory mechanism — shifting the curve right to facilitate O2 release to tissues despite lower SpO2. Stored blood has low 2,3-DPG (left shift), which is clinically relevant in massive transfusion.

questions.q: Voluntary breathing control is mediated through which pathway?
opts: A. Pre-Bötzinger complex → DRG → phrenic nerve
B. Peripheral chemoreceptors → vagus → NTS
C. Cerebral motor cortex → corticospinal tracts → respiratory neurons → respiratory muscles
D. Hering-Breuer reflex → apneustic centre
E. J receptors → vagus → VRG
F. Option F
questions.exp: Voluntary breathing (e.g. breath-holding, hyperventilation, forceful inspiration) is controlled by the cerebral motor cortex, which sends impulses via corticospinal tracts to respiratory neurons and ultimately to respiratory muscles. This pathway can override the automatic (medullary) rhythm temporarily.

questions.q: The main muscles of quiet inspiration account for approximately what percentage of inspiratory effort?
opts: A. Diaphragm 40%, intercostals 60%
B. Diaphragm 80%, intercostals 20%
C. Diaphragm 50%, accessory muscles 50%
D. Intercostals 90%, diaphragm 10%
E. Diaphragm 60%, sternocleidomastoids 40%
F. Option F
questions.exp: The diaphragm provides approximately 80% of inspiratory effort during quiet breathing, innervated by the phrenic nerve (C3, 4, 5). Intercostals account for the remainder. During exercise, accessory muscles (scalenes, sternocleidomastoids, pectoralis major, trapezius) are recruited to increase tidal volume.

questions.q: The phrenic nerve originates from which spinal levels?
opts: A. C1, C2, C3
B. C3, C4, C5
C. T1, T2, T3
D. C5, C6, C7
E. T3, T4, T5
F. Option F
questions.exp: The phrenic nerve arises from C3, C4, C5 ('C3, 4, 5 keeps the diaphragm alive'). It innervates the diaphragm, the primary muscle of inspiration. High cervical spinal cord injuries (above C3) are fatal without ventilatory support, as the phrenic nerve is damaged. Injuries below C5 typically preserve diaphragmatic function.

questions.q: Oxygen capacity is defined as:
opts: A. The % of haemoglobin binding sites occupied
B. The amount of O2 per unit of blood (maximum that could be carried)
C. The partial pressure of O2 in arterial blood
D. The total O2 dissolved in plasma
E. The difference between inspired and expired O2 concentration
F. Option F
questions.exp: Oxygen capacity = the maximum amount of O2 per unit of blood — determined primarily by haemoglobin concentration. Oxygen saturation (SpO2) = the % of available sites occupied. Oxygen content (CaO2) = how much O2 is actually carried. SpO2 tells you saturation but not content; CaO2 tells you oxygen-carrying capacity.

questions.q: Which of the following best explains why CO2 can maintain efficient transport despite having only a 5 mmHg partial pressure gradient across the respiratory membrane (vs 64 mmHg for O2)?
opts: A. CO2 uses facilitated diffusion via membrane transporters
B. CO2 has 20× greater solubility and dipole structure allowing efficient diffusion despite the small gradient
C. CO2 binds more strongly to haemoglobin than O2
D. CO2 molecules are smaller and diffuse faster independently of solubility
E. CO2 actively pumped by Type II pneumocytes
F. Option F
questions.exp: CO2 diffusion rate = solubility × partial pressure gradient. Although CO2 gradient is small (5 mmHg vs 64 mmHg for O2), CO2 is ~20× more soluble in biological fluids and has dipole characteristics, resulting in similar total CO2 and O2 transfer. This is why CO2 retention (hypercapnia) is usually a problem of ventilation, not diffusion.

questions.q: In heart failure, activation of J (juxtacapillary) receptors by engorged pulmonary capillaries causes:
opts: A. Increased tidal volume and respiratory rate leading to better oxygenation
B. The sensation of dyspnoea (difficulty breathing) via vagal afferents
C. Bronchodilation to improve gas exchange
D. Activation of the Hering-Breuer deflation reflex
E. Release of surfactant from Type II pneumocytes
F. Option F
questions.exp: J receptors (juxtacapillary receptors) are vagal sensory endings in alveolar walls. When stimulated by capillary engorgement (heart failure) or alveolar oedema, they send signals via the vagus nerve to the brainstem, producing the sensation of dyspnoea — the breathlessness patients describe in heart failure. This is a reflex protective response.

questions.q: The Haldane effect accounts for approximately what percentage of CO2 exchange in lungs and tissues?
opts: F. Option F
questions.exp: The Haldane effect (effect of O2 on CO2 transport) accounts for approximately 50% of CO2 exchange. The Bohr effect accounts for ~20% of O2 exchange. Together with passive diffusion, these coupled effects ensure efficient bidirectional gas exchange at both pulmonary and tissue capillaries.

questions.q: A pulmonary irritant reflex (mediated by vagal sensory endings in the airways) results in:
opts: A. Apnoea and airway relaxation
B. Deeper breaths, coughing, sneezing, bronchiolar constriction, and mucus secretion
C. Inhibition of the DRG
D. Immediate bronchodilation
E. Activation of J receptors
F. Option F
questions.exp: Pulmonary irritant reflexes are mediated by sensory nerve endings (from the vagus) in the trachea, bronchi, and bronchioles. When stimulated by irritants (dust, chemicals, cold air), they cause: deeper breaths, coughing, sneezing, bronchiolar constriction, and mucus secretion. This mechanism underlies bronchospasm in asthma and COPD.

questions.q: A 7-year-old presents with recurrent sinus infections and nasal congestion. Imaging shows opacification of the cavity adjacent to the cheekbone. Which sinus is most likely affected?
opts: A. Frontal sinus
B. Sphenoidal sinus
C. Maxillary sinus
D. Ethmoidal sinus
E. Temporal sinus
F. Option F
questions.exp: The maxillary sinus is adjacent to the cheekbone (maxillary bone) and is the most commonly affected sinus in sinusitis. It is named from its associated bone. The frontal sinus is superior (forehead), sphenoidal is posterior, and ethmoidal cells are medial. The maxillary sinus is also the largest and drains poorly due to its ostium being near the roof.

questions.q: Which of the following BEST describes all four functions of the paranasal sinuses?
opts: A. Gas exchange, immune defence, speech resonance, lighten skull
B. Produce mucus, lighten skull weight, protection/dampening, warm and humidify air
C. Produce surfactant, warm air, lighten skull, immune surveillance
D. Filter air, regulate pH, produce mucus, absorb oxygen
E. Warm air, produce saliva, lighten skull, phagocytosis
F. Option F
questions.exp: The four main functions of the paranasal sinuses are: (1) produce mucus — they are lined with mucosa; (2) lighten the weight of the skull — they are air-filled cavities within bones; (3) protection — dampening of trauma; (4) warm and humidify air for better oxygen intake. They do not perform gas exchange or produce surfactant.

questions.q: A patient has a fracture at the root of the nose, described as 'between the eyebrows.' Which anatomical landmark is at the root of the nose?
opts: A. Apex
B. Bridge
C. Dorsum nasi
D. Root (between eyebrows)
E. Philtrum
F. Option F
questions.exp: The root of the nose is the junction between the forehead and the nose — located between the eyebrows. The bridge connects the root to the rest of the nose; the dorsum nasi is the length of the nose; the apex is the tip; and the philtrum is the groove connecting the apex to the upper lip.

questions.q: Which cartilage forms the lateral walls of the nostrils (nares) and surrounds the apex of the nose?
opts: A. Septal cartilage
B. Alar cartilage
C. Cricoid cartilage
D. Thyroid cartilage
E. Epiglottic cartilage
F. Option F
questions.exp: The alar cartilage forms the apex and surrounds the nares (nostrils), constituting the lateral cartilaginous walls. The septal cartilage is flexible hyaline cartilage forming the nasal septum, connected to the nasal bone along the dorsum nasi. These are distinct structures — alar = lateral/tip; septal = midline partition.

questions.q: The nasal conchae (turbinates) serve which primary physiological purposes?
opts: A. Produce surfactant and prevent alveolar collapse
B. Increase surface area and disrupt airflow to clean, warm, and humidify inhaled air
C. Filter large particulate matter via phagocytosis
D. Regulate the gag reflex and prevent aspiration
E. Provide structural rigidity to the nasal septum
F. Option F
questions.exp: The superior, middle, and inferior nasal conchae are bony projections that increase the surface area of the nasal cavity and create turbulent airflow — causing air to bounce across the mucosal epithelium. This maximises cleaning (trapping debris), warming, and humidification of inhaled air. The meatuses beneath them also conserve water to prevent nasal dehydration.

questions.q: Which bones form the superior and middle nasal conchae, and what is unique about the inferior concha?
opts: A. All three are part of the ethmoid bone
B. Superior and middle are part of the ethmoid bone; inferior is a separate bone
C. Inferior and middle are part of the ethmoid; superior is a separate bone
D. All three are part of the maxillary bone
E. Superior concha is part of the frontal bone; the rest are ethmoid
F. Option F
questions.exp: The superior and middle nasal conchae are processes of the ethmoid bone. The inferior concha is a separate, independent bone — one of the facial bones in its own right. This anatomical distinction is clinically relevant as the inferior concha is the most commonly affected by turbinate hypertrophy causing nasal obstruction.

questions.q: A patient is told they have 'pseudostratified columnar ciliated epithelium with goblet cells' lining most of their airway. This is called respiratory epithelium. Which of the following is NOT a function of this epithelium?
opts: A. Producing mucus to trap debris
B. Cilia beating mucus toward the throat to be swallowed
C. Secreting defensins and lysozyme for immune defence
D. Direct gas exchange with capillaries
E. Secreting serous fluid for mucociliary clearance
F. Option F
questions.exp: Respiratory epithelium (pseudostratified ciliated columnar with goblet cells) performs: mucus production, mucociliary clearance (cilia beating mucus to throat), and innate immunity (defensins, lysozyme). It does NOT perform gas exchange — that role belongs to the thin Type I pneumocytes of the alveolar wall. Gas exchange requires the thin respiratory membrane, not pseudostratified epithelium.

questions.q: A patient who has been vaping heavily for 2 years now presents with chronic productive cough and recurrent chest infections. The most likely underlying mechanism is:
opts: A. Destruction of alveolar Type I pneumocytes
B. Paralysis of mucociliary cilia, causing mucus accumulation and impaired clearance
C. Increased surfactant production leading to atelectasis
D. Direct damage to the cricoid cartilage
E. Reduced goblet cell numbers causing dry airways
F. Option F
questions.exp: Vaping and cigarette smoke paralyse the cilia of the mucociliary escalator. Without ciliary beating, mucus accumulates rather than being swept toward the throat, leading to a productive cough (to physically expel mucus) and impaired pathogen clearance — increasing infection risk. This is the mechanism behind smoker's cough and contributes to COPD pathogenesis.

questions.q: The nasopharynx is lined by which type of epithelium, and what makes its wall structurally distinct from the oropharynx?
opts: A. Stratified squamous; collapsible walls for swallowing
B. Pseudostratified ciliated columnar (respiratory epithelium); rigid/non-collapsible walls
C. Simple squamous; thin for gas exchange
D. Transitional epithelium; highly distensible
E. Keratinised stratified squamous; resistant to desiccation
F. Option F
questions.exp: The nasopharynx is lined by respiratory epithelium (pseudostratified ciliated columnar), reflecting its role as an airway. Its walls are rigid and non-collapsible — important for maintaining airway patency during breathing. In contrast, the oropharynx and laryngopharynx are lined with non-keratinised stratified squamous epithelium to withstand abrasion from food.

questions.q: Which two key structures are found in the nasopharynx?
opts: A. Palatine tonsils and uvula
B. Adenoids (pharyngeal tonsils) / Waldeyer's ring and the orifice of the auditory (Eustachian) tube
C. Epiglottis and arytenoids
D. Vocal cords and vestibular folds
E. Soft palate and fauces
F. Option F
questions.exp: The nasopharynx contains: (1) Pharyngeal tonsils (adenoids) and Waldeyer's ring — lymphoid tissue that traps pathogens using ciliated epithelium; (2) The orifice of the auditory (Eustachian/pharyngotympanic) tube, which connects the nasopharynx to the middle ear. This connection explains why nasopharyngeal infections frequently cause otitis media (middle ear infections).

questions.q: A child presents with recurrent ear infections following every upper respiratory tract infection. The anatomical connection most responsible is:
opts: A. The piriform fossa connecting the laryngopharynx to the middle ear
B. The auditory (Eustachian) tube connecting the nasopharynx to the middle ear
C. The choanae connecting the nasal cavity to the middle ear
D. The aryepiglottic folds connecting the larynx to the temporal bone
E. The palatoglosssal arch connecting the oral cavity to the ear
F. Option F
questions.exp: The auditory (Eustachian/pharyngotympanic) tube opens in the nasopharynx and connects to the middle ear. In children, this tube is shorter, more horizontal, and wider — making it easy for bacteria from nasopharyngeal infections to ascend into the middle ear, causing otitis media. This is why children have more frequent ear infections than adults.

questions.q: The uvula, located in the oropharynx, is primarily composed of connective tissue, glands, and muscle. Its main function is to:
opts: A. Filter inhaled air and trap pathogens
B. Prevent food and liquids from entering the nasal cavity during swallowing
C. Produce saliva for digestion
D. Vibrate during speech to produce consonant sounds
E. Act as a valve to prevent regurgitation into the oesophagus
F. Option F
questions.exp: The uvula (a fleshy projection from the back of the soft palate) elevates during swallowing to seal off the nasopharynx, preventing food and liquids from entering the nasal cavity (nasopharyngeal regurgitation). It also keeps the throat moist with saliva and plays a minor role in speech articulation and the gag reflex.

questions.q: The fauces is best described as:
opts: A. The opening between the nasopharynx and the nasal cavity
B. The connection between the oral cavity and the pharynx, bounded by the palatoglossal and palatopharyngeal arches
C. The gap between the vocal cords through which air passes
D. The groove between the epiglottis and the tongue base
E. The posterior wall of the laryngopharynx
F. Option F
questions.exp: The fauces is the gateway from the oral cavity (mouth) to the oropharynx (throat). It is bounded by: the palatoglossal arch (anterior pillar), the palatopharyngeal arch (posterior pillar), the soft palate superiorly, and the tongue base inferiorly. The palatine tonsils lie in the tonsillar fossae within the fauces.

questions.q: The laryngopharynx extends from the tip of the epiglottis to which vertebral level, where it becomes the oesophagus?
opts: A. C2
B. C4
C. C6
D. T1
E. T4
F. Option F
questions.exp: The laryngopharynx extends from the tip of the epiglottis to the level of C6, where it becomes continuous with the oesophagus at the cricoid cartilage. The laryngopharynx includes the piriform fossae (common sites for foreign body lodgement), aryepiglottic folds, and the arytenoids anteriorly.

questions.q: A patient swallows a fish bone and it lodges in a common anatomical recess. Where is it most likely to be found?
opts: A. The tonsillar fossa of the oropharynx
B. The piriform fossa of the laryngopharynx
C. The vallecula between the tongue and epiglottis
D. The epiglottic groove
E. The nasopharyngeal recess
F. Option F
questions.exp: The piriform fossa ('pear-shaped recess') is a common site for foreign body lodgement — fish bones and coins frequently become impacted here. The piriform fossae are lateral recesses of the laryngopharynx, on either side of the laryngeal inlet. The vallecula (between tongue and epiglottis) is another common site but the piriform fossa is the classic answer for foreign body lodgement.

questions.q: The oropharynx and laryngopharynx share the same type of epithelial lining. What is it, and why is it appropriate for these regions?
opts: A. Pseudostratified ciliated columnar — for mucociliary clearance of food debris
B. Simple squamous — for rapid diffusion of nutrients
C. Non-keratinised stratified squamous — for protection against abrasion from food passage
D. Transitional — to allow distension during swallowing
E. Columnar with goblet cells — to lubricate food passage
F. Option F
questions.exp: Both the oropharynx and laryngopharynx are lined with non-keratinised stratified squamous epithelium. This is appropriate because food passes through these regions — stratified squamous epithelium withstands mechanical abrasion far better than respiratory epithelium. The nasopharynx (airway only, no food) has respiratory epithelium instead.

questions.q: Which is the LARGEST cartilage of the larynx, and what is its clinical landmark?
opts: A. Cricoid — forms a complete ring and is palpable as a firm band below the thyroid
B. Thyroid — the anterior laryngeal prominence (Adam's apple)
C. Epiglottis — the flexible flap most visible during laryngoscopy
D. Arytenoid — the paired cartilages that control vocal cord tension
E. Cuneiform — the accessory cartilage supporting the epiglottis
F. Option F
questions.exp: The thyroid cartilage is the largest laryngeal cartilage and forms the laryngeal prominence — the 'Adam's apple' — visible anteriorly on the neck, especially in males. The cricoid cartilage (inferior) is the only complete cartilaginous ring in the respiratory tract. The epiglottis is flexible elastic cartilage that covers the trachea during swallowing.

questions.q: During a meal, a patient suddenly develops a violent coughing fit after a small piece of food 'goes down the wrong way.' The structure that failed to close properly is:
opts: A. The uvula
B. The soft palate
C. The epiglottis
D. The cricoid cartilage
E. The aryepiglottic folds alone
F. Option F
questions.exp: The epiglottis is a flexible elastic cartilage that normally covers the laryngeal inlet during swallowing, directing food into the oesophagus. Failure of this mechanism allows food or liquid to enter the trachea (aspiration), triggering a violent cough reflex. The glottis (vocal cords) also closes as a secondary protective mechanism. The uvula seals the nasopharynx, not the trachea.

questions.q: The glottis is composed of:
opts: A. The thyroid and cricoid cartilages
B. The vestibular folds (true vocal cords) and the space between them
C. The epiglottis and arytenoid cartilages only
D. The aryepiglottic folds and the piriform fossae
E. The cricothyroid membrane and the tracheal rings
F. Option F
questions.exp: The glottis comprises the vocal folds (true vocal cords — folded mucous membrane over the vocalis muscle and ligament) and the rima glottidis (the space between them). Air passing through the glottis vibrates the vocal cords to produce sound. The arytenoid, cuneiform, and corniculate cartilages attach the epiglottis to the vocal cords and allow muscles to move them for speech.

questions.q: The cricoid cartilage is clinically important because:
opts: A. It is the most superior laryngeal cartilage and first palpable on the neck
B. It is the only complete cartilaginous ring in the respiratory tract, forming the inferior boundary of the larynx
C. It produces the laryngeal prominence visible in adult males
D. It is composed of elastic cartilage and covers the larynx during swallowing
E. It connects the larynx to the hyoid bone
F. Option F
questions.exp: The cricoid cartilage is the only complete ring of cartilage in the entire respiratory tract — all other laryngeal and tracheal cartilages are incomplete (C-shaped). It forms the inferior ring of the larynx and is important in emergency airway management (cricothyrotomy is performed through the cricothyroid membrane between the thyroid and cricoid cartilages).

questions.q: The trachea contains 16–20 C-shaped hyaline cartilage rings. The posterior gap is filled by the fibroelastic membrane and the trachealis muscle. What is the functional advantage of this posterior gap?
opts: A. Allows the vagus nerve to enter the tracheal wall directly
B. Allows the trachea to stretch and expand during inhalation, and the trachealis can contract to force air out
C. Provides a direct connection to the oesophagus for gas-air exchange during swallowing
D. Reduces resistance to airflow by widening the lumen during exercise
E. Acts as a sphincter to prevent aspiration of gastric contents
F. Option F
questions.exp: The posterior gap in the tracheal rings is filled by the fibroelastic membrane, which allows the trachea to stretch and expand during deep inhalation and when large boluses of food pass through the adjacent oesophagus. The trachealis muscle spans this gap — it can contract to reduce the tracheal diameter, increasing airflow velocity to assist in coughing and clearing debris.

questions.q: The carina is clinically significant because:
opts: A. It is the bifurcation of the trachea into the primary bronchi and is highly sensitive to foreign bodies, triggering coughing
B. It is the site of vocal cord attachment
C. It marks the junction between the larynx and pharynx
D. It is where the pulmonary plexus is formed
E. It is the only site where gas exchange can occur in the conducting zone
F. Option F
questions.exp: The carina is the ridge at the bifurcation of the trachea into the left and right primary bronchi. It contains a high density of cough receptors (vagal afferents) and is highly sensitive to foreign bodies — making it the most sensitive part of the airway for triggering the cough reflex. It is an important landmark on chest X-ray (widening suggests lymphadenopathy).

questions.q: A patient inhales a peanut that passes the carina. Into which primary bronchus is it most likely to lodge, and why?
opts: A. Left primary bronchus — it is wider and more vertical
B. Right primary bronchus — it is wider, shorter, and more vertical than the left
C. Left primary bronchus — it is longer and the path of least resistance
D. Neither — the carina reflexly prevents foreign body passage
E. Right primary bronchus — it has fewer branching points to slow descent
F. Option F
questions.exp: Inhaled foreign bodies most commonly lodge in the right primary bronchus because it is wider, shorter, and more vertical (more in line with the trachea) than the left. The left primary bronchus is narrower and angles more sharply to the left to pass under the aortic arch. This anatomical difference is clinically important for aspiration pneumonia and bronchoscopic retrieval.

questions.q: Which of the following correctly distinguishes terminal bronchioles from the bronchi above them?
opts: A. Terminal bronchioles have cartilage rings but no epithelium
B. Terminal bronchioles have no cartilage but have muscular walls that regulate airflow
C. Terminal bronchioles are lined with simple squamous epithelium for gas exchange
D. Terminal bronchioles contain goblet cells but no smooth muscle
E. Terminal bronchioles are only found in the respiratory zone
F. Option F
questions.exp: Terminal bronchioles are the final generation of the conducting zone (>1000 per lung). Key distinction: they have NO cartilage (unlike bronchi) but have muscular walls of smooth muscle that regulate airflow. This smooth muscle is why bronchoconstriction in asthma is so impactful. Gas exchange begins only in the respiratory bronchioles beyond the terminal bronchioles.

questions.q: The conducting zone of the respiratory tract spans from the nose/mouth to the terminal bronchioles. Its primary functions are:
opts: A. Gas exchange and oxygen transfer to blood
B. Routing air, filtering particles, warming, and humidifying — but NOT gas exchange
C. Producing surfactant and preventing alveolar collapse
D. Immune surveillance and phagocytosis exclusively
E. Regulating blood pH via CO2 retention
F. Option F
questions.exp: The conducting zone (nose → terminal bronchioles, ~20-23 generations) routes, filters, warms, and humidifies air. It does NOT perform gas exchange — that is the exclusive role of the respiratory zone (respiratory bronchioles → alveolar ducts → alveolar sacs → alveoli). The conducting zone also forms the anatomical dead space (~150 mL).

questions.q: Alveolar pores (pores of Kohn) connect adjacent alveoli. Their primary function is:
opts: A. Allow macrophages to migrate between alveoli
B. Equalise pressure between adjacent alveoli and provide collateral ventilation
C. Allow surfactant to spread between alveolar sacs
D. Provide a route for gas exchange when the main airway is blocked
E. Both A and B
F. Option F
questions.exp: Alveolar pores (Kohn's pores) equalise pressure between adjacent alveoli and allow collateral ventilation — air can bypass blocked airways through these pores to ventilate alveoli that might otherwise collapse. They also allow alveolar macrophages to migrate between alveoli (so E is partially correct, but B is the primary and most complete answer).

questions.q: The right lung has how many lobes and fissures, compared to the left?
opts: A. Right: 2 lobes, 1 fissure; Left: 3 lobes, 2 fissures
B. Right: 3 lobes, 2 fissures (horizontal and oblique); Left: 2 lobes, 1 fissure (oblique)
C. Right: 3 lobes, 1 fissure; Left: 2 lobes, 2 fissures
D. Both: 3 lobes, 2 fissures each
E. Right: 4 lobes, 3 fissures; Left: 2 lobes, 1 fissure
F. Option F
questions.exp: Right lung: 3 lobes (superior, middle, inferior) separated by 2 fissures (horizontal/transverse fissure separates superior and middle; oblique fissure separates middle and inferior). Left lung: 2 lobes (superior and inferior) separated by 1 oblique fissure. The left lung is smaller due to the cardiac notch (heart indent), but has the lingula — a tongue-like projection of the superior lobe.

questions.q: A bronchopulmonary segment is defined as:
opts: A. The smallest functional unit supplied by one large bronchiole
B. A division of a lobe, each supplied by its own tertiary bronchus and artery, surgically resectable
C. The space between two fissures in the same lung
D. A group of alveoli sharing one alveolar duct
E. The region of lung drained by one pulmonary vein
F. Option F
questions.exp: A bronchopulmonary segment is a division of a lobe, each with its own tertiary (segmental) bronchus and segmental artery. Critically, each segment is surrounded by connective tissue septa and has its own venous drainage — making it an independently resectable surgical unit (segment resection = segmentectomy). A pulmonary lobule (smaller) is supplied by one large bronchiole.

questions.q: Visceral pleura differs from parietal pleura in that it:
opts: A. Lines the thoracic wall and diaphragm
B. Covers the lung surface and extends into the fissures between lobes
C. Contains pain-sensitive nerve endings
D. Connects to the visceral layer at the hilum and extends to the mediastinum
E. Secretes pleural fluid via parietal mesothelial cells
F. Option F
questions.exp: Visceral pleura covers the lung surface (parenchyma) and extends into the interlobar fissures. It has no pain-sensitive nerve fibres (only autonomic). Parietal pleura lines the thoracic wall, mediastinum, and diaphragm; it is pain-sensitive (somatic innervation) — explaining why pleuritis causes sharp, localised chest pain. The two layers connect at the hilum.

questions.q: A patient with a stab wound to the left chest develops sudden breathlessness and a hyperresonant left chest on percussion. The most likely diagnosis is:
opts: A. Left-sided pleural effusion — blood filling the pleural cavity
B. Left-sided pneumothorax — air in the pleural cavity disrupting negative intrapleural pressure
C. Left-sided haemothorax — blood compressing the lung
D. Left lower lobe consolidation from aspiration
E. Mediastinal shift from pericardial tamponade
F. Option F
questions.exp: A stab wound allows air into the pleural cavity (pneumothorax), eliminating the negative intrapleural pressure that holds the lung open via surface tension with the thoracic wall. The lung collapses. Hyperresonance on percussion (air instead of tissue) distinguishes pneumothorax from haemothorax (stony dull) or consolidation (dull). Treatment is chest drain or needle decompression.

questions.q: Three functions of the pleura are lubrication, surface tension/expansion, and organ separation. Which best explains why the lung expands during inspiration?
opts: A. Pleural fluid lubricates the lung surface, allowing frictionless gliding
B. The adhesive property of pleural fluid creates surface tension, coupling lung expansion to thoracic wall movement
C. Organ separation prevents the heart from compressing the left lung during systole
D. Negative intrapleural pressure alone causes lung inflation without pleural involvement
E. The parietal pleura contracts actively during inspiration to pull the lung outward
F. Option F
questions.exp: The adhesive property of the thin layer of pleural fluid (from mesothelial cells) creates surface tension between the visceral and parietal pleura. This 'couples' the lung to the thoracic wall — when the chest wall expands (diaphragm and intercostals contract), the lung is pulled outward with it. This is why if the pleural space fills with air or fluid, this coupling is lost and the lung collapses.

questions.q: Boyle's Law (P1V1 = P2V2) underpins the mechanics of ventilation. During inspiration, which pressure changes occur?
opts: A. Thoracic volume decreases → intrathoracic pressure rises → air flows out
B. Thoracic volume increases → intrathoracic pressure falls below atmospheric → air flows in
C. Intrapleural pressure rises from −4 to 0 mmHg → lung expansion
D. Atmospheric pressure drops → a pressure gradient drives air into the lungs
E. Diaphragm relaxes → thoracic volume increases → air flows in
F. Option F
questions.exp: Boyle's Law: as the thoracic volume increases (diaphragm descends, intercostals raise ribs), intrathoracic (intra-alveolar) pressure falls below atmospheric pressure (760 mmHg) → air flows in down the pressure gradient. Expiration is the reverse — elastic recoil reduces volume, raises pressure, and air flows out. Intrapleural pressure remains approximately −4 mmHg below atmospheric throughout.

questions.q: The intrapleural pressure is approximately −4 mmHg (negative) relative to atmosphere. This negativity is maintained because:
opts: A. Active pumping by mesothelial cells creates a vacuum in the pleural space
B. Elastic recoil and surface tension of the lung pull inwards while pleural fluid surface tension and thoracic wall recoil pull outwards — net outward force creates negativity
C. The diaphragm actively maintains a negative pressure between breaths
D. The lungs are larger than the thoracic cavity, compressing the pleural space
E. Venous return to the right heart creates a suction effect in the chest
F. Option F
questions.exp: Intrapleural negativity arises from opposing forces: the lung's elastic recoil + surface tension pull inward (tending to collapse the lung), while pleural fluid surface tension + thoracic wall elasticity (chest wall springs outward at FRC) pull outward. The net outward force is slightly greater, creating the −4 mmHg negative pressure that keeps the lungs inflated and coupled to the chest wall.

questions.q: Which of the following correctly defines transpulmonary pressure and its role?
opts: A. The pressure difference between the atmosphere and the alveolus, driving gas exchange
B. The pressure difference between alveolar pressure and intrapleural pressure, which holds the airways open
C. The total pressure of all gases in the alveolus
D. The pressure exerted by surfactant on the alveolar wall
E. The negative pressure generated by the diaphragm during inspiration
F. Option F
questions.exp: Transpulmonary pressure = alveolar pressure − intrapleural pressure. It represents the distending pressure across the lung wall, and is one of the key forces holding airways open. Along with radial traction from surrounding parenchyma and surfactant effects, transpulmonary pressure prevents small airway collapse — particularly important in emphysema where elastic recoil is lost.

questions.q: Forces that promote small airway closure include which of the following trio?
opts: A. Transpulmonary pressure, surfactant, radial traction
B. Gravity, loss of elastic recoil, water surface tension of airway lining fluid
C. Bronchodilation, positive intrapleural pressure, decreased FRC
D. Increased mucus, bronchoconstriction, surfactant deficiency
E. Parasympathetic stimulation, high altitude, hyperventilation
F. Option F
questions.exp: The three forces promoting airway closure are: (1) Gravity (dependent regions); (2) Loss of elastic recoil (e.g. emphysema — elastic tissue destruction removes the traction that keeps airways open); (3) Water surface tension of airway lining fluid (which acts to collapse the airway, opposed by surfactant). Surfactant and transpulmonary pressure are the opposing forces keeping airways open.

questions.q: Functional Residual Capacity (FRC) is the volume of air remaining in the lungs at the end of a normal quiet breath. Its primary physiological importance is:
opts: A. It represents the maximum volume of air that can be forcibly exhaled
B. It acts as a buffer — keeping small airways open and preventing large swings in alveolar PO2 and PCO2 between breaths
C. It is the dead space volume that does not participate in gas exchange
D. It equals tidal volume plus inspiratory reserve volume
E. It is the volume at which the diaphragm generates maximum force
F. Option F
questions.exp: FRC is where lung elastic recoil inward balances chest wall recoil outward. Physiological importance: (1) Small airways remain open above closing capacity; (2) Acts as an O2 reserve and buffer — without FRC, alveolar PO2 and PCO2 would fluctuate wildly with each breath; (3) Serves as an oxygen tank reserve. FRC is reduced in obesity, supine positioning, atelectasis, and restrictive lung disease.

questions.q: On spirometry, a patient has an FEV1/FVC ratio of 0.65 (65%). The most likely diagnosis is:
opts: A. Restrictive lung disease such as pulmonary fibrosis
B. Normal spirometry — within the expected range
C. Obstructive lung disease such as asthma or emphysema
D. Neuromuscular weakness causing low FVC
E. Pulmonary hypertension reducing FEV1
F. Option F
questions.exp: FEV1/FVC ratio (FER): normal ~0.75 (75%). FER <0.7 (70%) indicates obstructive lung disease — airflow is disproportionately reduced (FEV1 falls more than FVC), as in asthma, COPD, and emphysema. FER >0.8 (80%) suggests restrictive lung disease (both FEV1 and FVC fall proportionally, maintaining a high ratio). This ratio is the key spirometry discriminator.

questions.q: A patient with known pulmonary fibrosis undergoes spirometry. Which pattern would you expect?
opts: A. FEV1/FVC <0.7, reduced FEV1, normal FVC
B. FEV1/FVC >0.8, reduced FVC, relatively preserved FEV1
C. Normal FEV1/FVC, reduced FRC, increased TLC
D. FEV1/FVC <0.7, increased TLC, reduced DLCO
E. Normal spirometry with reduced DLCO only
F. Option F
questions.exp: Pulmonary fibrosis causes restrictive lung disease: stiff lungs reduce all volumes proportionally. FVC is reduced (lungs cannot fully expand); FEV1 is also reduced, but since both fall equally, the FEV1/FVC ratio is preserved or elevated (>0.8). TLC and RV are also reduced. This contrasts with obstructive disease where air trapping increases RV and TLC.

questions.q: Tidal volume is best defined as:
opts: A. The maximum air that can be inhaled from FRC
B. The air remaining in the lungs after maximum forced expiration
C. The volume of air inhaled or exhaled during a single normal, quiet breath
D. The total air exhaled after a maximum inhalation
E. The sum of inspiratory and expiratory reserve volumes
F. Option F
questions.exp: Tidal volume (TV) is the volume of air moved in or out of the lungs during a single normal, quiet breath — approximately 500 mL in adults. It does not include reserve volumes. Inspiratory reserve volume (IRV) is the additional air that can be forcibly inhaled above TV. Expiratory reserve volume (ERV) is the additional air that can be forcibly exhaled below TV.

questions.q: Vital capacity is:
opts: A. The volume of air remaining after maximum forced expiration (residual volume)
B. The maximum volume of air that can be exhaled after a maximum inhalation — the total range of breathing
C. Tidal volume × respiratory rate per minute
D. FRC + inspiratory reserve volume
E. Total lung capacity minus tidal volume
F. Option F
questions.exp: Vital capacity (VC) = the maximum volume of air that can be forcibly exhaled after a maximum inhalation = IRV + TV + ERV. It represents the functional range of breathing. VC ≠ Total Lung Capacity — TLC includes the residual volume (air that cannot be exhaled). Residual volume cannot be measured by spirometry alone (requires gas dilution or body plethysmography).

questions.q: Residual volume (RV) cannot be measured by spirometry alone because:
opts: A. Patients cannot perform forced manoeuvres reliably
B. It is the air that remains in the lungs after maximum expiration and cannot be voluntarily exhaled, so spirometry cannot measure what cannot be exhaled
C. RV changes with each breath, making measurement unreliable
D. Spirometry measures only inspiratory volumes
E. RV is below the threshold of spirometer sensitivity
F. Option F
questions.exp: Residual volume (RV) is the air remaining in the lungs after a maximum forced expiration — it cannot be expelled. Since spirometry only measures air that moves in and out, it cannot capture RV. RV is measured by indirect techniques: helium dilution, nitrogen washout, or body plethysmography (whole-body box). RV keeps the lungs from fully collapsing and maintains alveolar shape.

questions.q: Sympathetic stimulation of the airways produces bronchodilation via which receptor type, and what is the clinical application?
opts: A. α1-adrenergic receptors; used in anaphylaxis treatment
B. β2-adrenergic receptors; the basis for bronchodilator therapy (e.g. salbutamol/Ventolin)
C. Muscarinic receptors; basis for anticholinergic inhalers
D. β1-adrenergic receptors; basis for cardiac pacing in asthma
E. Nicotinic receptors; basis for smoking cessation therapy
F. Option F
questions.exp: Sympathetic stimulation causes bronchodilation via β2-adrenergic receptors on airway smooth muscle. This is the basis for β2-agonist bronchodilators (salbutamol/Ventolin) — the most commonly used first-line asthma therapy. Parasympathetic stimulation (via muscarinic/M3 receptors) causes bronchoconstriction, mediated by vagal fibres — anticholinergics (ipratropium) block this.

questions.q: Parasympathetic stimulation of the airways causes:
opts: A. Bronchodilation and reduced mucus secretion
B. Bronchoconstriction, increased airway resistance, and mediates reflexes like coughing and mucus secretion
C. Vasodilation of pulmonary arterioles
D. Increased respiratory rate via the medulla
E. Relaxation of the trachealis muscle
F. Option F
questions.exp: Parasympathetic innervation (vagus nerve) of the airways causes: bronchoconstriction (smooth muscle contraction → narrows lumen → increases resistance), mucus secretion (goblet cells and submucosal glands), and mediates reflexes such as coughing and bronchospasm. This is why vagal stimulation (e.g. irritants, cold air) can precipitate asthma attacks.

questions.q: The pulmonary plexus is formed at the hilum from which sources, and what does it innervate?
opts: A. Phrenic nerve only; innervates the diaphragm and pleura
B. Vagus nerve fibres and thoracic sympathetic ganglia (T2-5); innervates bronchial muscle, glands, and vessels — but notably NO pain fibres in the lungs
C. Intercostal nerves; innervates the pleura and intercostal muscles
D. Vagus nerve only; innervates the larynx and trachea
E. Thoracic ganglia T1-3; innervates pulmonary vasculature only
F. Option F
questions.exp: The pulmonary plexus forms at the hilum from: vagus nerve fibres (parasympathetic) + thoracic sympathetic trunk ganglia (T2-5). It innervates: bronchial smooth muscle, glands, and blood vessels. Crucially, there are NO pain-sensitive afferent fibres in the lung parenchyma itself — this is why lung cancer and pneumonia can be painless until the parietal pleura (pain-sensitive) is involved.

questions.q: A patient with asthma uses a salbutamol (Ventolin) inhaler. Which mechanism directly causes bronchodilation?
opts: A. Blocking muscarinic receptors on airway smooth muscle
B. Stimulating β2-adrenergic receptors → cAMP → smooth muscle relaxation → bronchodilation
C. Inhibiting carbonic anhydrase to reduce CO2 and relieve bronchoconstriction
D. Stimulating α1 receptors to constrict bronchial vessels, reducing mucosal oedema
E. Activating the parasympathetic vagal reflex to counter bronchoconstriction
F. Option F
questions.exp: Salbutamol is a selective β2-adrenergic agonist. It binds β2 receptors on bronchial smooth muscle → activates adenylyl cyclase → increases cAMP → activates protein kinase A → relaxes smooth muscle → bronchodilation. This mimics sympathetic stimulation. Ipratropium works via mechanism A (anticholinergic). The two can be combined (e.g. Combivent) for additive bronchodilation.

questions.q: CO2 is the PRIMARY driver of respiratory rate. The mechanism is:
opts: A. CO2 directly stimulates peripheral chemoreceptors in the carotid body at normal levels
B. CO2 diffuses across the blood-brain barrier, increasing CSF acidity, which stimulates central chemoreceptors to increase rate and depth of breathing
C. CO2 binds to haemoglobin, reducing O2 carrying capacity, stimulating the DRG
D. CO2 activates J receptors in the alveolar walls to signal the medulla
E. CO2 causes bronchodilation which reflexly increases respiratory rate
F. Option F
questions.exp: CO2 is the primary respiratory drive. CO2 crosses the blood-brain barrier (unlike H+ ions, which cannot) → reacts with water in CSF → forms H+ → central chemoreceptors in the medulla detect the pH drop → increase respiratory rate and depth to expel CO2 and restore pH. Low CO2 → slow shallow breathing. Peripheral chemoreceptors respond to O2 (secondary drive).

questions.q: During intense exercise, a patient's respiratory rate increases dramatically despite only a modest rise in CO2. The additional stimulus most responsible is:
opts: A. Activation of central chemoreceptors by low CSF pH from CO2
B. Peripheral chemoreceptors detecting a significant drop in PaO2 to 60 mmHg
C. Peripheral chemoreceptors detecting rising H+ (lactic acidosis), proprioceptors from muscles, and sympathetic stimulation
D. J receptor activation from pulmonary capillary engorgement
E. Hering-Breuer inflation reflex limiting deep breathing
F. Option F
questions.exp: During intense exercise, several stimuli augment ventilation beyond CO2 alone: (1) Peripheral chemoreceptors detecting lactic acidosis (H+); (2) Proprioceptors in muscles/joints signalling increased metabolic demand; (3) Sympathetic activation (hypothalamus/limbic system stimulates respiratory rate). PaO2 drops to 60 mmHg only in extreme exertion — it is less critical due to haemoglobin's buffer capacity. Together these causes the ventilatory response to exercise.

questions.q: The Ventral Respiratory Group (VRG) of the medulla is primarily responsible for:
opts: A. Integrating input from peripheral chemoreceptors
B. Generating the basic respiratory rhythm and stimulating the diaphragm and intercostal muscles
C. Inhibiting DRG neurons during expiration
D. Controlling the depth of inspiration during deep breathing only
E. Detecting CO2 changes in the cerebrospinal fluid
F. Option F
questions.exp: The Ventral Respiratory Group (VRG) in the medulla generates the basic respiratory rhythm and sends efferents to the diaphragm and intercostal muscles. The Dorsal Respiratory Group (DRG) integrates afferent input from stretch receptors and peripheral chemoreceptors. The Pneumotaxic Centre (pons) inhibits DRG neurons to regulate rate; the Apneustic Centre (pons) stimulates deep inspiration.

questions.q: A patient on a ward is given excessive opioid analgesia. Their respiratory rate drops to 4 breaths/minute. Which physiological effect of opioids explains this?
opts: A. Opioids stimulate peripheral chemoreceptors, reducing the drive to breathe
B. Opioids suppress central respiratory centres in the medulla, reducing sensitivity to CO2 and H+
C. Opioids cause bronchoconstriction, reducing effective tidal volume
D. Opioids deplete 2,3-DPG, causing a left shift and tissue hypoxia
E. Opioids paralyse the diaphragm via the phrenic nerve
F. Option F
questions.exp: Opioids (morphine, fentanyl, heroin) suppress central respiratory centres in the medulla — particularly the pre-Bötzinger complex and the chemoreceptor response to CO2/H+. This reduces the respiratory drive, causing bradypnoea (slow breathing), hypoventilation, hypercapnia, and respiratory acidosis. In overdose this leads to apnoea and death. Naloxone (opioid antagonist) reverses this.

questions.q: Normal adult respiratory rate at rest is:
opts: A. 5-8 breaths/minute
B. 12-16 breaths/minute
C. 20-30 breaths/minute
D. 30-60 breaths/minute
E. 8-12 breaths/minute
F. Option F
questions.exp: Normal adult respiratory rate is 12-16 breaths/minute, controlled by the medullary respiratory centres. Children under 1 year breathe 30-60 times/minute (higher metabolic rate, smaller lung volumes). The rate decreases through childhood to adult levels by adolescence. Tachypnoea (>20/min in adults) and bradypnoea (<12/min) are important clinical signs.

questions.q: A patient presents with a displaced trachea on examination. The trachea is deviated to the RIGHT. Which combination of findings would MOST support a large RIGHT-sided pleural effusion as the cause?
opts: A. Hyperresonant right chest, reduced breath sounds right, trachea deviated left
B. Stony dull right chest, reduced breath sounds right, trachea deviated left
C. Stony dull right chest, reduced breath sounds right, trachea deviated right
D. Dull right chest, bronchial breath sounds right, trachea deviated right
E. Hyperresonant right chest, trachea deviated right, increased vocal fremitus
F. Option F
questions.exp: A large pleural effusion on the RIGHT: pushes (deviates) the trachea and mediastinum to the RIGHT (massive effusion) or sometimes left depending on degree. The ipsilateral chest is stony dull on percussion, breath sounds are reduced/absent, and vocal fremitus is reduced. Bronchial breath sounds above the effusion level can occur. Note: tension pneumothorax deviates trachea AWAY from the affected side (contralateral).

questions.q: Which of the following lung volumes CANNOT be directly measured by spirometry, and must use gas dilution or body plethysmography?
opts: A. Tidal volume
B. Inspiratory reserve volume
C. Expiratory reserve volume
D. Residual volume
E. Vital capacity
F. Option F
questions.exp: Residual volume (RV) — the air remaining after maximum forced expiration — cannot be measured by spirometry because it cannot be exhaled. Spirometry measures only volumes that can be moved in and out: TV, IRV, ERV, VC, FVC, FEV1. RV (and therefore FRC and TLC, which include RV) requires gas dilution (helium), nitrogen washout, or body plethysmography.

questions.q: Pulmonary compliance refers to:
opts: A. The resistance of the airways to airflow, measured by FEV1/FVC
B. The ability of the thoracic wall and lungs to stretch under pressure; low compliance makes expansion harder
C. The elasticity of haemoglobin to bind and release oxygen
D. The capacity of the alveolar epithelium to produce surfactant
E. The ability of the chest wall to recoil during expiration
F. Option F
questions.exp: Pulmonary (thoracic) compliance is the ability of the thoracic wall and lungs to stretch under pressure — the volume change per unit pressure change. Low compliance (stiff lungs, e.g. fibrosis, ARDS) means more work is required to expand the thorax, causing dyspnoea and rapid shallow breathing. High compliance (e.g. emphysema — loss of elastic tissue) means lungs overinflate but cannot recoil efficiently.

questions.q: The trachealis muscle on the posterior wall of the trachea can contract to:
opts: A. Increase the tracheal diameter to reduce airway resistance during exercise
B. Reduce the tracheal diameter, increasing airflow velocity to assist coughing and clearing debris
C. Close the trachea during swallowing, preventing aspiration
D. Regulate the passage of air between the trachea and oesophagus
E. Generate the cough reflex by compressing the carina
F. Option F
questions.exp: The trachealis muscle spans the posterior gap in the tracheal rings. Contraction reduces the tracheal diameter — this increases airflow velocity (like a garden hose nozzle), which helps propel mucus and debris upward during coughing. During deep breathing, the fibroelastic membrane allows the trachea to expand. The oesophagus lies immediately posterior to the trachea.

questions.q: Mucociliary clearance is described as the 'escalator' of the respiratory tract. Which of the following would MOST impair this mechanism?
opts: A. Increased goblet cell number increasing mucus production
B. Chronic cigarette or vape smoke paralyzing cilia so mucus cannot be moved toward the throat
C. Alkaline pH of respiratory secretions reducing lysozyme activity
D. Increased respiratory rate mechanically disrupting mucociliary flow
E. Mouth breathing bypassing the nasal filtering mechanism
F. Option F
questions.exp: Mucociliary clearance depends on coordinated ciliary beating. Cigarette smoke and vaping contain compounds (acrolein, formaldehyde, nicotine) that paralyse cilia — mucus then accumulates rather than being swept upward to be swallowed. This leads to productive cough (smoker's cough), increased infection risk, and contributes to chronic bronchitis and COPD pathogenesis. Primary ciliary dyskinesia (genetic) has the same consequence.

questions.q: The olfactory epithelium is located in which part of the nasal cavity, and what is its additional function beyond smell detection?
opts: A. Inferior concha — it also filters large particles
B. Ethmoid bone at the roof of the nasal cavity — bronchial epithelium nearby can also metabolise some airborne carcinogens
C. Hard palate — it also detects food flavours during eating
D. Nasopharynx — it also detects bacterial toxins for immune activation
E. Septal cartilage — it is stimulated by temperature changes
F. Option F
questions.exp: Olfactory epithelium is located at the roof of the nasal cavity on the cribriform plate of the ethmoid bone. Beyond smell, bronchial epithelium (deeper in the respiratory tract) can metabolise some airborne carcinogens via cytochrome P450 enzymes — this is clinically significant as it can activate pro-carcinogens (e.g. benzo[a]pyrene from cigarette smoke) into DNA-damaging compounds, contributing to lung cancer.

questions.q: Which of the following best describes the blood supply to the lungs — distinguishing between the two supplies?
opts: A. Pulmonary arteries (oxygenated) supply parenchyma; bronchial arteries (deoxygenated) supply airways
B. Pulmonary arteries (deoxygenated) carry blood for gas exchange; bronchial arteries (oxygenated from aorta) supply the lung tissue itself
C. Both pulmonary and bronchial arteries carry deoxygenated blood to the lungs
D. Pulmonary veins supply blood; pulmonary arteries drain it
E. The azygous system supplies all lung tissue; pulmonary arteries are for gas exchange only
F. Option F
questions.exp: The lungs have a dual blood supply: (1) Pulmonary arteries — carry deoxygenated blood from the right ventricle for gas exchange at the alveoli; (2) Bronchial arteries — branch from the thoracic aorta and carry oxygenated blood to nourish the lung tissue itself (bronchi, pleura, etc.). Pulmonary veins return oxygenated blood to the left atrium; bronchial veins drain into the azygos system.

questions.q: Lymphatic drainage of the lungs flows centripetally (from periphery toward hilum). Lymph passes through which nodes at the hilum?
opts: A. Mediastinal nodes → tracheobronchial nodes → bronchopulmonary nodes
B. Bronchopulmonary nodes (hilum) → tracheobronchial nodes (at carina) → paratracheal nodes → mediastinal lymph trunks → brachiocephalic veins
C. Axillary nodes → intercostal nodes → bronchopulmonary nodes
D. Subcarinal nodes → pulmonary ligament nodes → thoracic duct
E. Peribronchial nodes → pulmonary nodes → azygos system
F. Option F
questions.exp: Pulmonary lymphatics drain centripetally from pleura/parenchyma to: bronchopulmonary nodes at the hilum → tracheobronchial nodes at the tracheal bifurcation (carina) → paratracheal nodes → mediastinal lymph trunks → usually drain directly into the brachiocephalic veins (or rarely indirectly via the right lymphatic duct on the right, thoracic duct on the left). This pathway is relevant for lung cancer staging (mediastinal lymph node involvement = N2 disease).

questions.q: A patient presents with a hoarse voice after thyroid surgery. Which nerve is most likely damaged, and how does it relate to the larynx?
opts: A. Phrenic nerve — it controls the diaphragm and indirectly affects phonation
B. Recurrent laryngeal nerve (branch of vagus) — it innervates the intrinsic muscles of the larynx that move the vocal cords
C. Glossopharyngeal nerve — it innervates the oropharynx and soft palate affecting voice
D. Hypoglossal nerve — it controls tongue movement needed for speech
E. Superior laryngeal nerve alone — it controls all laryngeal motor function
F. Option F
questions.exp: The recurrent laryngeal nerve (RLN), a branch of the vagus nerve, loops around the subclavian artery (right) and the aortic arch (left) before ascending to the larynx. It innervates all intrinsic laryngeal muscles (which move the vocal cords) except the cricothyroid. Thyroid surgery risks RLN damage → hoarse voice (unilateral) or aphonia/stridor (bilateral). The left RLN has a longer course and is more at risk.

questions.q: During forced expiration (e.g. blowing a balloon), which muscles are actively recruited?
opts: A. Diaphragm and external intercostals only
B. Accessory muscles including scalenes, sternocleidomastoids, and internal intercostals/abdominals actively driving expiration
C. Only the pectoralis major and trapezius
D. The trachealis muscle and glottic muscles
E. No muscles — forced expiration is always passive
F. Option F
questions.exp: Quiet expiration is passive (elastic recoil). Forced expiration actively recruits internal intercostals (depress ribs, reduce thoracic volume) and abdominal muscles (compress abdominal contents, raise diaphragm). Accessory muscles (scalenes, sternocleidomastoids) assist forced INSPIRATION. This distinction is important: scalenes/SCM are inspiratory accessories; internal intercostals/abdominals are forced expiratory muscles.

questions.q: A patient with severe emphysema is observed to have a 'barrel chest' (increased anteroposterior diameter) and uses accessory muscles to breathe at rest. The pathophysiological reason for the barrel chest is:
opts: A. Chronic hypertrophy of the diaphragm causing dome elevation
B. Loss of lung elastic recoil causing chronic hyperinflation — air trapping pushes the chest wall outward permanently
C. Increased muscle mass from chronic accessory muscle use
D. Pleural effusion pushing the chest wall outward bilaterally
E. Reduced FRC causing the chest wall to spring outward
F. Option F
questions.exp: Emphysema destroys alveolar walls and elastic tissue, eliminating elastic recoil. Without recoil, air cannot be fully expelled (air trapping), increasing FRC and RV. The chronic hyperinflation permanently pushes the chest wall outward → barrel chest (increased AP diameter, horizontal ribs). Accessory muscles are used at rest because diaphragm efficiency is reduced (flattened by hyperinflation). FEV1/FVC is markedly reduced.

questions.q: Which of the following correctly defines Inspiratory Capacity (IC)?
opts: A. IC = ERV + RV
B. IC = TV + IRV (the maximum volume that can be inhaled from FRC)
C. IC = TLC − FRC
D. Both B and C are correct
E. IC = VC − ERV
F. Option F
questions.exp: Inspiratory Capacity (IC) = TV + IRV = the maximum volume that can be inhaled from the end-expiratory position (FRC). Since TLC = FRC + IC, then IC = TLC − FRC. Therefore both B (TV + IRV) and C (TLC − FRC) are mathematically equivalent and correct definitions. This is a classic SBA where two answers are correct but D ('both B and C') is the BEST answer.

questions.q: The philtrum is the anatomical structure that:
opts: A. Connects the root of the nose to the bridge
B. Forms the lateral cartilaginous walls of the nostrils
C. Is the concave groove/surface connecting the apex of the nose to the upper lip
D. Is the midline bony septum dividing the nasal cavity
E. Is the flexible cartilage between the ethmoid bone and nasal bone
F. Option F
questions.exp: The philtrum is the vertical groove/concave surface on the upper lip, running from the base of the nose (apex) to the upper lip. It is thought to be a remnant of embryological fusion of the facial processes. In fetal alcohol syndrome, the philtrum is flattened/absent. It is distinct from the ala (lateral nostril walls), septal cartilage (midline partition), and bridge (root-to-dorsum).

questions.q: A patient develops acute sinusitis. They report a headache worsening when bending forward and tenderness on pressing below the eyes. Which sinuses are most likely infected?
opts: A. Frontal sinuses
B. Sphenoidal sinuses
C. Ethmoidal sinuses
D. Maxillary sinuses
E. All sinuses equally
F. Option F
questions.exp: Maxillary sinusitis classically presents with: pain/pressure below the eyes (over the cheekbones/maxillae), worsening on forward bending (fluid shifts), and dental pain. The maxillary sinuses are the most commonly infected — partly because their drainage ostium is located near the roof of the sinus (gravity-dependent drainage is poor). Frontal sinusitis causes forehead pain; sphenoidal causes deep, posterior headache.

questions.q: The hard palate forms which border of the nasal cavity, and what separates the nasal from the oral cavity?
opts: A. The hard palate forms the lateral wall of the nasal cavity
B. The hard palate forms the anterior floor of the nasal cavity, separating it from the oral cavity below
C. The hard palate forms the posterior roof of the nasopharynx
D. The soft palate forms the floor throughout
E. The vomer bone forms the floor of the nasal cavity
F. Option F
questions.exp: The hard palate forms the anterior floor of the nasal cavity (and the roof of the oral cavity), separating the two compartments. It is composed of bone (palatine process of maxilla anteriorly + horizontal plate of palatine bone posteriorly). The soft palate forms the posterior floor of the nasal cavity, and during swallowing, it elevates to seal the nasopharynx (uvula assists this). A cleft palate disrupts this separation.

questions.q: Which of the following spirometry findings correctly predicts the diagnosis?
opts: A. FEV1 3.0L, FVC 4.0L, FEV1/FVC 0.75 — obstructive pattern
B. FEV1 2.5L, FVC 2.8L, FEV1/FVC 0.89 — restrictive pattern
C. FEV1 3.5L, FVC 4.5L, FEV1/FVC 0.78 — restrictive pattern
D. FEV1 1.8L, FVC 4.2L, FEV1/FVC 0.43 — restrictive pattern
E. FEV1 2.0L, FVC 2.4L, FEV1/FVC 0.83 — obstructive pattern
F. Option F
questions.exp: FEV1/FVC = 0.89 (>0.8 = 80%) with reduced FVC is characteristic of restrictive lung disease. In restriction, both FEV1 and FVC fall proportionally, preserving the ratio or making it high. Option D (ratio 0.43) is obstructive. Option E (ratio 0.83) looks borderline but with reduced volumes is restrictive. This question tests systematic interpretation: always calculate ratio first, then assess volumes.

questions.q: What type of cartilage makes up the epiglottis, and why is this clinically important?
opts: A. Hyaline cartilage — resists deformation during swallowing
B. Fibrocartilage — allows progressive calcification with age
C. Elastic cartilage — allows the epiglottis to flex and spring back during swallowing without permanent deformation
D. Elastic cartilage — calcifies in adults, causing dysphagia in the elderly
E. Hyaline cartilage — calcifies early, explaining epiglottitis presentations
F. Option F
questions.exp: The epiglottis is composed of elastic cartilage, which allows it to be flexible — it can flex downward to cover the laryngeal inlet during swallowing and spring back to its upright position to reopen the airway. This elasticity is essential for its function. Elastic cartilage (also found in the pinna and Eustachian tube) maintains shape without calcifying. Hyaline cartilage (tracheal rings, thyroid, cricoid) can calcify with age.

questions.q: A medical student percusses a patient's chest and finds 'resonant' sounds throughout both lungs. What does this indicate?
opts: A. The patient has a pneumothorax — hyperresonance on that side
B. The patient has consolidation — resonance is the expected finding
C. Normal air-filled lung tissue — resonance is the expected finding in healthy lungs
D. The patient has a pleural effusion — resonance is normal over effusion
E. The patient has emphysema — resonance becomes dull in hyperinflation
F. Option F
questions.exp: Percussion of normal, air-filled lung produces a resonant note. This is the expected baseline finding. Abnormal percussion findings: Dull = consolidation or pleural effusion (mild); Stony dull = large pleural effusion or haemothorax; Hyperresonant = pneumothorax or emphysema (increased air). Always start on the 'normal' side to establish a baseline before moving to the affected side.

questions.q: Accessory muscles of respiration used during forced inspiration include:
opts: A. Internal intercostals, abdominal muscles, and the diaphragm
B. Scalenes, sternocleidomastoids, pectoralis major, and trapezius
C. External intercostals alone
D. The diaphragm and rectus abdominis
E. Only the scalenes and external obliques
F. Option F
questions.exp: Accessory muscles of forced inspiration: scalenes (elevate upper ribs), sternocleidomastoids (elevate sternum and clavicles), pectoralis major (pulls chest wall outward when arms are fixed), and trapezius (elevates shoulder girdle). Their use at rest indicates significant respiratory distress. The diaphragm and external intercostals are PRIMARY muscles of quiet inspiration, not accessories.

questions.q: A patient with COPD exacerbation is sitting upright leaning forward with hands on knees (tripod position). The physiological reason this helps breathing is:
opts: A. It compresses the abdomen, pushing the diaphragm up to improve recoil
B. It fixes the upper limbs, allowing pectoralis major and accessory muscles to act on the thorax rather than the arms
C. It reduces venous return to the right heart, decreasing pulmonary pressures
D. It opens the glottis wider by extending the neck
E. It directly increases FRC by elevating the diaphragm
F. Option F
questions.exp: The tripod position fixes the upper extremities (arms braced on knees). This allows pectoralis major, scalenes, and sternocleidomastoids to act as accessory muscles of inspiration — pulling on the chest wall rather than on the arms. Patients in respiratory distress instinctively adopt this position. It does NOT increase FRC — FRC is determined by the balance of lung/chest wall recoil, not posture (though supine position reduces FRC).

questions.q: FEV1 stands for Forced Expiratory Volume in 1 second. What does a reduced FEV1 with a normal FVC indicate?
opts: A. Restrictive lung disease with a proportional volume loss
B. Pure obstructive pattern — early airflow is limited (FEV1 falls) while total capacity is preserved (FVC normal)
C. Normal spirometry — the ratio will still be 0.75
D. A technical error in spirometry technique
E. Gas trapping from a large pneumothorax
F. Option F
questions.exp: Reduced FEV1 with normal (or near-normal) FVC gives a reduced FEV1/FVC ratio — the hallmark of obstruction. In early/mild obstructive disease, FVC may be normal because patients can exhale all their air — it just takes longer (>6 seconds). Only in advanced disease does FVC fall (air trapping/dynamic hyperinflation). This pattern is classic for mild-moderate asthma or early COPD.

questions.q: The primary bronchi enter the lungs at the hilum. What other structures enter or exit at the hilum?
opts: A. Phrenic nerve, diaphragmatic artery, and thoracic duct
B. Pulmonary arteries, pulmonary veins, bronchial vessels, lymphatics, and autonomic nerve fibres (pulmonary plexus)
C. Intercostal vessels, azygos vein, and vagus nerve directly
D. Superior and inferior vena cava, aorta, and trachea
E. Oesophageal branches and left recurrent laryngeal nerve
F. Option F
questions.exp: The hilum is the 'gateway' of the lung — structures entering/exiting include: pulmonary arteries (in), pulmonary veins (out), bronchial arteries (in), bronchial veins (out), lymphatic vessels, and autonomic nerve fibres forming the pulmonary plexus. The parietal and visceral pleura are continuous at the hilum. Hilar lymphadenopathy (enlarged hilar nodes) is visible on chest X-ray and suggests infection, sarcoidosis, or malignancy.

questions.q: A 4-year-old is brought to ED with sudden onset stridor and respiratory distress after eating. Lateral neck X-ray shows a coin lodged at the cricopharyngeus (C6 level). This location corresponds to:
opts: A. The junction of the oropharynx and nasopharynx
B. The upper oesophageal sphincter — the transition from laryngopharynx to oesophagus at the level of the cricoid cartilage (C6)
C. The glottis — between the vocal cords
D. The transition from nasopharynx to oropharynx at the soft palate
E. The tracheal bifurcation at the carina (T4/5 level)
F. Option F
questions.exp: The cricopharyngeus muscle forms the upper oesophageal sphincter at the level of C6 — the transition from the laryngopharynx to the oesophagus. This is the most common site for foreign body (e.g. coin) impaction in children. The laryngopharynx terminates at C6 (cricoid cartilage level). The carina is at T4/T5. This is also the level of cricoid pressure (Sellick's manoeuvre) used during rapid sequence intubation.

questions.q: Which of the following best explains why surfactant is critical for alveolar stability, particularly for smaller alveoli?
opts: A. Smaller alveoli have lower surface tension, which surfactant neutralises
B. By reducing surface tension, surfactant prevents smaller alveoli from collapsing into larger ones (LaPlace's Law: smaller radius = higher pressure to collapse)
C. Surfactant increases elastic recoil to prevent overinflation of large alveoli
D. Surfactant creates an osmotic gradient drawing fluid into alveoli to stabilise them
E. Surfactant lines the capillaries, preventing fluid leak into alveoli
F. Option F
questions.exp: LaPlace's Law: P = 2T/r — the pressure required to keep an alveolus open is inversely proportional to its radius. Smaller alveoli have higher collapsing pressure. Without surfactant, smaller alveoli would collapse into larger ones (atelectasis). Surfactant (produced by Type II pneumocytes) reduces surface tension — critically, it does so more at smaller radii (concentration increases as size decreases), equalising pressures across alveoli of different sizes.

questions.q: A premature neonate at 28 weeks gestation develops progressive respiratory distress from birth, requiring increasing oxygen. The most likely underlying pathology, given the developmental anatomy of the lung, is:
opts: A. Aspiration of meconium blocking the conducting airways
B. Surfactant deficiency due to immature Type II pneumocytes causing widespread alveolar collapse (Neonatal Respiratory Distress Syndrome)
C. Pulmonary hypertension from a patent ductus arteriosus
D. Diaphragmatic hernia reducing left lung volume
E. Tracheomalacia from underdeveloped cartilage rings
F. Option F
questions.exp: Type II pneumocytes begin producing surfactant from ~24 weeks gestation, with adequate levels typically achieved by 35–36 weeks. In premature infants (<34 weeks), surfactant deficiency causes Neonatal Respiratory Distress Syndrome (NRDS/hyaline membrane disease) — progressive atelectasis as alveoli collapse without surfactant. Treatment: antenatal corticosteroids to accelerate lung maturation, postnatal exogenous surfactant (e.g. poractant alfa/Curosurf).

questions.q: The normal resting intrapleural pressure is approximately −4 mmHg. During a forced inspiration, this becomes more negative (approximately −8 mmHg). What would happen if the intrapleural pressure became 0 or positive?
opts: A. The lungs would hyperinflate — positive pressure would expand them further
B. The lung would collapse (pneumothorax physiology) — without negative intrapleural pressure, the lung's elastic recoil causes it to retract
C. Breathing would continue normally — small pressure changes have no effect on lung volume
D. The diaphragm would descend further to compensate
E. Surfactant production would increase to compensate for reduced expansion
F. Option F
questions.exp: The negative intrapleural pressure is what keeps the lung inflated against its own elastic recoil. If intrapleural pressure reaches 0 (atmospheric) or positive (tension pneumothorax), the lung's elastic recoil causes it to collapse inward — pneumothorax. In tension pneumothorax, pressure becomes positive on the affected side, compressing the contralateral lung and mediastinum, causing cardiovascular collapse — a life-threatening emergency requiring immediate needle decompression.

questions.q: A patient is told by their doctor that their 'peak expiratory flow rate' (PEFR) is reduced. This measurement is most useful for monitoring which condition?
opts: A. Pulmonary fibrosis — to track disease progression
B. Asthma — PEFR reflects large airway obstruction and diurnal variation correlates with asthma severity
C. Pneumothorax — PEFR drops proportionally to lung collapse
D. Pleural effusion — PEFR reflects fluid volume
E. Pulmonary embolism — PEFR reflects dead space
F. Option F
questions.exp: Peak Expiratory Flow Rate (PEFR) measures the maximum flow rate during a forced expiration. It is most useful for asthma monitoring — asthma causes airway obstruction reducing PEFR; there is typically a morning dip (diurnal variation >20% suggests asthma). Patients use home peak flow meters for self-monitoring. PEFR is less useful in fibrosis (restriction — FVC more informative) or pneumothorax/PE.

questions.q: Which of the following is the correct classification of white blood cells into granulocytes and agranulocytes?
opts: A. Neutrophils, monocytes, and eosinophils are granulocytes; basophils and lymphocytes are agranulocytes
B. Neutrophils, eosinophils, and basophils are granulocytes; monocytes and lymphocytes are agranulocytes
C. Neutrophils, lymphocytes, and basophils are granulocytes; monocytes and eosinophils are agranulocytes
D. All five types are granulocytes depending on staining technique
E. Monocytes and lymphocytes are granulocytes; the remaining three are agranulocytes
F. Eosinophils and basophils are agranulocytes; the remaining three are granulocytes
