questions.exp: Granulocytes (neutrophils, eosinophils, basophils) have visible cytoplasmic granules under light microscopy. Agranulocytes (monocytes and lymphocytes) lack visible granules. This classification is based on the presence or absence of granules visible with a standard light microscope.

questions.q: A 24-year-old man presents with a bacterial skin infection. His white cell count shows a marked neutrophilia. Which of the following best describes the typical appearance of a neutrophil on a blood film?
opts: A. Spherical shape with a large purple nucleus occupying most of the cell
B. Bilobed nucleus with bright red-orange granules in the cytoplasm
C. Multi-lobed nucleus with lilac granules, with nuclear lobe number increasing with cell age
D. Horseshoe-shaped nucleus, the largest of the leukocytes
E. Bilobed nucleus with dense dark purple granules
F. Large granular lymphocyte with a faint lilac cytoplasm and eccentric nucleus
questions.exp: Neutrophils have a characteristically multi-lobed nucleus (the number of lobes increases with cell age) and lilac-coloured cytoplasmic granules. They are the most abundant leukocyte. Bilobed nucleus with red-orange granules describes eosinophils (B). Horseshoe-shaped nucleus describes monocytes (D). Dark purple dense granules describe basophils (E).

questions.q: A patient is found to have a parasitic infection with Strongyloides. Which white blood cell type would you expect to be most elevated on the full blood count differential?
opts: A. Neutrophils, as they are the most abundant and first-line phagocytes
B. Basophils, due to their release of histamine during parasitic infections
C. Monocytes, as they are the most effective phagocytes and become macrophages
D. Eosinophils, which contain toxic proteins and free radicals targeting parasitic infections
E. B lymphocytes, which will produce specific IgE antibodies against the parasite
F. Natural killer cells, which non-specifically destroy parasitic organisms
questions.exp: Eosinophils are specifically elevated in parasitic infections. They contain highly toxic proteins and free radicals that target parasitic organisms and also secrete antihistamines. Neutrophils (A) are the first-line response to bacteria. Basophils (B) release histamine in allergic responses but are not the primary anti-parasitic cell.

questions.q: Which of the following correctly describes the function of basophil granules?
opts: A. Contain lysozymes for bacterial cell wall destruction
B. Contain histamine which contributes to inflammation, and heparin which opposes clotting and moderates the inflammatory response
C. Contain toxic proteins and free radicals targeting parasitic organisms
D. Contain cytotoxic chemicals released against bacterial pathogens
E. Contain antibodies (immunoglobulins) for targeting foreign antigens
F. Contain perforin and granzymes to destroy virally infected cells
questions.exp: Basophil granules contain histamine (which promotes and contributes to inflammation) and heparin (which opposes blood clotting and moderates the inflammatory response). Toxic proteins targeting parasites are found in eosinophils (C). Cytotoxic chemicals against bacteria are in neutrophils (D). Antibodies are produced by plasma cells derived from B lymphocytes (E).

questions.q: A 32-year-old woman has a blood film showing cells with a spherical shape, a large purple nucleus that dominates the cell, and very little visible cytoplasm. Which cell type does this describe?
opts: A. Monocyte
B. Neutrophil
C. Eosinophil
D. Basophil
E. Lymphocyte
F. Natural killer cell
questions.exp: Lymphocytes appear spherical with a large purple nucleus that occupies most of the cell with a thin rim of cytoplasm. Monocytes (A) are the largest leukocyte with a horseshoe-shaped nucleus. Neutrophils (B) have multi-lobed nuclei with lilac granules. Eosinophils (C) have bilobed nuclei and red-orange granules.

questions.q: Natural killer (NK) cells differ from B and T lymphocytes in which of the following ways?
opts: A. NK cells produce antibodies whereas B cells mediate cellular immunity
B. NK cells require prior antigen sensitisation to function, whereas T cells do not
C. NK cells are non-specific and recognise 'non-self' cells including cancer cells without prior sensitisation, whereas B and T cells require antigen-specific activation
D. NK cells are classified as granulocytes due to their granule content
E. NK cells are found only in lymph nodes, whereas B and T cells circulate freely
F. NK cells are the primary mediators of adaptive immunity
questions.exp: NK cells are non-specific lymphocytes that recognise and attack cells classified as 'non-self' — including cancer cells and infected cells — without requiring prior antigen sensitisation. B cells produce antibodies and T cells provide cellular immunity, both as part of the adaptive (acquired) immune system requiring specific activation.

questions.q: A 45-year-old man with chronic liver disease and cirrhosis presents with generalised oedema. His serum albumin is 18 g/L (normal 35-50 g/L). What is the primary mechanism causing his oedema?
opts: A. Increased hydrostatic pressure from portal hypertension directly forcing fluid into tissues
B. Reduced plasma oncotic pressure from low albumin, causing fluid to move out of capillaries into the interstitial space
C. Sodium retention by the kidneys causing osmotic fluid shift into the extravascular space
D. Increased capillary permeability from inflammatory cytokines released by the damaged liver
E. Loss of globulins from the damaged liver reducing the total plasma protein osmotic effect
F. Reduced fibrinogen causing inadequate clot formation and subsequent haemorrhage into tissues
questions.exp: Albumin is the primary determinant of plasma oncotic (colloid osmotic) pressure. Low albumin reduces oncotic pressure, so fluid moves out of capillaries into the interstitial space by osmosis, causing oedema. This is a classic clinical consequence of hypoalbuminaemia from liver disease, nephrotic syndrome, or malnutrition.

questions.q: Which plasma protein group makes up approximately 60% of total plasma proteins and is synthesised exclusively by the liver?
opts: A. Gamma globulins
B. Beta globulins
C. Alpha globulins
D. Albumin
E. Fibrinogen
F. Immunoglobulins
questions.exp: Albumin comprises approximately 60% of total plasma proteins and is synthesised by hepatocytes (liver cells). It has a lifespan of approximately 20 days. Gamma globulins (A) are produced by plasma cells (differentiated B lymphocytes). Fibrinogen (E) is produced by the liver but constitutes only ~4% of plasma proteins.

questions.q: A patient is prescribed warfarin and requires monitoring of their coagulation. Which laboratory test primarily evaluates the extrinsic coagulation pathway and is used to monitor warfarin therapy?
opts: A. Activated partial thromboplastin time (aPTT)
B. Thrombin time (TT)
C. Prothrombin time (PT)
D. Bleeding time
E. D-dimer assay
F. Platelet aggregation studies
questions.exp: Prothrombin time (PT) primarily evaluates the extrinsic pathway of coagulation and is used to monitor warfarin therapy (which affects Factor VII amongst other vitamin K-dependent factors). aPTT (A) evaluates the intrinsic and common pathways and is used to monitor heparin therapy. D-dimer (E) detects fibrin degradation products, indicating active clot formation and breakdown.

questions.q: Which of the following correctly describes the ABO blood group biochemistry?
opts: A. Type A blood: non-functional glycosyltransferase leaves the H antigen unmodified
B. Type B blood: adds N-acetyl galactosamine to the H antigen backbone
C. Type O blood: non-functional transferase leaves the H antigen unmodified
D. Type AB blood: adds only galactose to the H antigen
E. All blood groups express unique H antigen backbones specific to each group
F. ABO antigens are glycolipids rather than carbohydrates or glycoproteins
questions.exp: Type O blood has a non-functional ABO glycosyltransferase enzyme, leaving the H antigen unmodified. Type A adds N-acetyl galactosamine (not described in option B). Type B adds galactose. Type AB adds both sugars. All red cells share the same H antigen backbone — the difference is in what sugar is added to it.

questions.q: A 28-year-old Rh-negative woman is pregnant with her second child. Her first pregnancy was with an Rh-positive child and she did not receive anti-D prophylaxis. Which of the following best explains the risk to her current pregnancy?
opts: A. ABO incompatibility is the primary risk since IgM anti-A and anti-B antibodies can cross the placenta
B. During her first pregnancy, she was sensitised to RhD antigen, generating IgG anti-D antibodies that can now cross the placenta and cause haemolytic disease of the newborn
C. Rh antibodies are naturally occurring IgM that will cross the placenta irrespective of prior sensitisation
D. She is at risk only if the current foetus is also Rh-positive and this is their third pregnancy
E. Anti-D IgM produced during the first pregnancy persists and accumulates to dangerous levels in subsequent pregnancies
F. The risk only applies if feto-maternal haemorrhage was documented during the first delivery
questions.exp: During the first Rh-positive pregnancy (or delivery), feto-maternal haemorrhage exposes the Rh-negative mother to RhD antigen. She generates IgM initially, then undergoes class switching to IgG. Memory B-cells are generated making sensitisation permanent. In the second pregnancy, a rapid anamnestic IgG anti-D response occurs — IgG (unlike IgM) can cross the placenta and cause haemolytic disease of the newborn.

questions.q: What is the correct order of stages in erythropoiesis from stem cell to mature red blood cell?
opts: A. Haemocytoblast → Erythroblast → Proerythroblast → Reticulocyte → Erythrocyte
B. Haemocytoblast → Common myeloid progenitor → Proerythroblast → Erythroblast → Reticulocyte → Erythrocyte
C. Haemocytoblast → Common lymphoid progenitor → Erythroblast → Reticulocyte → Erythrocyte
D. Haemocytoblast → Megakaryocyte → Proerythroblast → Erythrocyte
E. Common myeloid progenitor → Haemocytoblast → Erythroblast → Erythrocyte
F. Haemocytoblast → Common myeloid progenitor → Reticulocyte → Proerythroblast → Erythroblast → Erythrocyte
questions.exp: The correct sequence is: haematopoietic stem cell (haemocytoblast) → common myeloid progenitor (CMP) → proerythroblast (haemoglobin begins forming, nucleus present) → erythroblast/normoblast (cells divide and produce haemoglobin) → reticulocyte (loses nucleus, enters bloodstream) → mature erythrocyte (final form, carries oxygen).

questions.q: At which anatomical site does erythropoiesis occur in a healthy adult?
opts: A. Yolk sac
B. Liver and spleen
C. Bone marrow of all bones including long bones
D. Only the red bone marrow of vertebrae, ribs, sternum, sacrum, pelvis, and proximal femur
E. Thymus and lymph nodes
F. Periosteum of flat bones only
questions.exp: In adults, erythropoiesis is restricted to the red bone marrow of specific flat and irregular bones: vertebrae, ribs, sternum, sacrum, pelvis, and proximal femur. In the early fetus it occurs in the yolk sac (A), then the liver and spleen from 2-5 months gestation (B), then progressively in bone marrow of most bones through childhood before being restricted to axial sites in adulthood.

questions.q: Which statement correctly describes the structure and function of red blood cells?
opts: A. Red blood cells are nucleated biconcave cells containing haemoglobin with a lifespan of 60 days
B. Red blood cells are anucleate biconcave discs containing haemoglobin, making up 45% of blood volume, with a lifespan of approximately 120 days
C. Red blood cells contain a nucleus to support haemoglobin synthesis throughout their lifespan
D. Red blood cells are rigid cells that can only travel through vessels larger than their diameter
E. Red blood cells make up 55% of blood volume with the remainder being plasma
F. Red blood cells are confined to the bloodstream and cannot leave, unlike platelets which extravasate freely
questions.exp: Red blood cells (erythrocytes) are anucleate (no nucleus), biconcave discs — the biconcave shape increases surface area for gas exchange efficiency. They contain haemoglobin for oxygen transport, make up approximately 45% of blood volume (haematocrit), are flexible to squeeze through small vessels, and have a lifespan of approximately 120 days.

questions.q: A blood bank receives a request for emergency transfusion for an unknown patient. Which blood type should be given?
opts: A. AB positive, as AB is the universal recipient and can receive any blood
B. A positive, as it is the most common blood group globally
C. O negative un-crossmatched blood, as O lacks A and B antigens and negative lacks RhD antigen
D. O positive, as the RhD antigen rarely causes reactions in first exposures
E. AB negative, as AB plasma contains no anti-A or anti-B antibodies
F. Group-specific blood should always be waited for regardless of urgency
questions.exp: In emergencies with an unknown blood group, O negative un-crossmatched blood is the standard of care. Group O lacks A and B antigens (no ABO incompatibility), and Rh-negative lacks the RhD antigen (avoiding Rh sensitisation). This is especially important for women of childbearing age. AB positive describes the universal recipient (A), not donor.

questions.q: What is the haematocrit (packed cell volume) and what are its normal values?
opts: A. The concentration of haemoglobin per litre of blood; normal 130-170 g/L
B. The total white cell count as a proportion of blood; normal 4-11 × 10⁹/L
C. The volume of red blood cells as a proportion of total blood volume; normal 40-45%
D. The number of platelets per microlitre of blood; normal 150-400 × 10⁹/L
E. The erythrocyte sedimentation rate measured in mm/hour; normal <20 mm/hr
F. The proportion of plasma to formed elements; normal 55% plasma to 45% cells
questions.exp: The haematocrit (packed cell volume, PCV) is the volume of red blood cells expressed as a proportion of total blood volume, normally 40-45%. It is measured by centrifuging blood and measuring the proportion occupied by the red cell layer. Haemoglobin concentration (A) and platelet count (D) are separate measurements.

questions.q: A patient with rheumatoid arthritis has an ESR of 95 mm/hr (normal <20 mm/hr for women). What is the mechanism underlying this elevated ESR?
opts: A. Increased platelet count causing the blood to thicken and cells to sediment more slowly
B. Anaemia causing red cells to be less dense and sediment faster under gravity
C. Elevated acute phase proteins, particularly fibrinogen, increasing rouleaux formation and causing red cells to stack and sediment faster
D. Elevated immunoglobulins neutralising red cell surface charge, causing repulsion between cells
E. Reduced albumin reducing plasma viscosity, allowing red cells to fall more freely
F. Increased white cell count adding mass to the blood column, pushing red cells to the bottom faster
questions.exp: Inflammation increases production of acute phase proteins, particularly fibrinogen. Fibrinogen coats red blood cells and promotes rouleaux formation (stacking like coins), increasing the mass and reducing the surface area to volume ratio, causing red cells to sediment faster. The ESR is a non-specific marker of inflammation — elevated fibrinogen is the key mediator.

questions.q: Which of the following plasma proteins is described as a 'negative acute phase protein'?
opts: A. C-reactive protein (CRP)
B. Fibrinogen
C. Alpha-1 antitrypsin
D. Transferrin
E. Haptoglobin
F. Ceruloplasmin
questions.exp: Transferrin is a negative acute phase protein — its concentration falls during acute inflammation (the opposite of positive acute phase proteins like CRP and fibrinogen which rise). Transferrin is a beta globulin synthesised in the liver that transports iron (Fe3+) to various tissues including bone marrow for red blood cell synthesis.

questions.q: Alpha-1 antitrypsin deficiency is an important clinical condition. What is the mechanism by which this causes lung damage?
opts: A. Deficiency allows unregulated elastase activity from neutrophils, leading to destruction of the alveolar walls
B. Deficiency reduces surfactant production by Type II pneumocytes
C. Deficiency allows unregulated histamine release from mast cells causing bronchoconstriction
D. Deficiency impairs iron transport to lung tissue, reducing cellular respiration
E. Deficiency reduces mucus clearance allowing recurrent bacterial infections
F. Deficiency reduces complement activation needed to clear pulmonary pathogens
questions.exp: Alpha-1 antitrypsin is a protective alpha globulin protein produced by the liver. It normally inhibits elastase — a protease enzyme released by neutrophils during inflammation. When deficient, unregulated elastase destroys the alveolar walls, leading to emphysema. This is classically seen in non-smokers with early-onset emphysema and is associated with liver disease.

questions.q: A 35-year-old man with blood type AB negative requires a blood transfusion. Which of the following correctly describes his compatibility as a recipient?
opts: A. He can only receive AB negative blood
B. He can receive red cells of any ABO type since he has no anti-A or anti-B antibodies, but must receive Rh-negative blood
C. He is the universal donor and can give blood to any recipient
D. He can receive any blood type including Rh-positive without risk
E. He can receive O negative and O positive blood only
F. He can receive AB positive blood since his anti-D status is irrelevant as a recipient
questions.exp: AB is the universal recipient for red cells — AB individuals have both A and B antigens on their red cells and therefore produce neither anti-A nor anti-B antibodies in their plasma. As an Rh-negative individual, he has not been sensitised to RhD, so to avoid sensitisation (especially in a male where it is less critical but still best practice) he should ideally receive Rh-negative blood. He is NOT the universal donor (that is O negative).

questions.q: Monocytes are described as antigen-presenting cells that 'become macrophages'. Which of the following best describes the appearance of a monocyte on a blood film?
opts: A. Small cell with a spherical nucleus and minimal cytoplasm
B. Multi-lobed nucleus with lilac granules
C. The largest leukocyte with a horseshoe-shaped (kidney-shaped) nucleus
D. Bilobed nucleus with bright red-orange granules
E. Bilobed nucleus with large dark purple granules
F. Oval nucleus with abundant pale cytoplasm and no granules
questions.exp: Monocytes are the largest leukocytes, with a characteristic horseshoe-shaped (kidney-shaped or reniform) nucleus. They are highly effective phagocytes that engulf pathogens and sick cells, present antigens to other immune cells, and migrate into tissues where they differentiate into macrophages.

questions.q: A 19-year-old woman presents following a road traffic accident. She has group B positive blood. She requires 4 units of red cells urgently and group-specific blood is available. Which donor red cell types could safely be given to her?
opts: A. B positive and B negative only
B. B positive, B negative, O positive, and O negative
C. Any blood type as she is effectively a universal recipient
D. AB positive and AB negative as AB is the universal donor
E. B positive, A negative, O negative only
F. O negative only in all emergency scenarios regardless of known blood group
questions.exp: Group B individuals have B antigens on their red cells and anti-A antibodies in their plasma. They can safely receive B (B+ or B-) or O red cells (O has no A or B antigens). Both Rh-positive and Rh-negative can be given to an Rh-positive recipient without causing sensitisation. O negative (F) is used when the group is UNKNOWN — when the group is known, ABO-identical blood is preferred.

questions.q: What is the Kell blood group system's clinical significance?
opts: A. Kell antigens serve as receptors for Plasmodium vivax malaria
B. Kell antibodies are naturally occurring IgM and arise without prior sensitisation
C. Kell is the third most important blood group system after ABO and Rh, is highly immunogenic, and Kell matching is essential especially for women of childbearing age
D. Kell antibodies cause only mild, clinically insignificant transfusion reactions
E. Kell antigens are only expressed during fetal development and are lost postnatally
F. Kell incompatibility causes delayed haemolytic reactions exclusively and never acute reactions
questions.exp: The Kell system is the third most clinically important blood group system after ABO and Rh. Kell (K antigen) is highly immunogenic — exposure through transfusion or pregnancy generates antibodies that can cause severe haemolytic transfusion reactions and haemolytic disease of the newborn. Kell matching is essential for women of childbearing age. The Duffy system (not Kell) serves as a receptor for Plasmodium vivax malaria (A).

questions.q: The Duffy blood group system has an important clinical relevance beyond transfusion medicine. What is it?
opts: A. Duffy antibodies are the most common cause of acute haemolytic transfusion reactions
B. The Duffy antigen acts as a red cell receptor for Plasmodium vivax malaria
C. Duffy-negative individuals are at increased risk of haemolytic disease of the newborn
D. The Duffy antigen is only expressed on platelets, not red blood cells
E. Duffy incompatibility causes the most severe delayed haemolytic reactions
F. Duffy antibodies are naturally occurring IgM and do not require prior sensitisation
questions.exp: The Duffy antigen (Fya, Fyb) serves as the red cell receptor for Plasmodium vivax malaria. Individuals who are Duffy-negative (common in populations of West African descent) are naturally resistant to P. vivax malaria infection. This is a key clinical fact beyond standard transfusion compatibility.

questions.q: What is the primary function of von Willebrand Factor (VWF) in primary haemostasis?
opts: A. VWF directly activates the extrinsic coagulation pathway by binding tissue factor
B. VWF bridges platelets to exposed subendothelial collagen via the platelet glycoprotein Ib receptor following vessel injury
C. VWF converts fibrinogen to fibrin to stabilise the platelet plug
D. VWF activates thrombin through the intrinsic pathway of coagulation
E. VWF inhibits platelet aggregation in undamaged vessel walls to prevent inappropriate clotting
F. VWF carries Factor VIII in the circulation, protecting it from premature degradation only
questions.exp: Von Willebrand Factor (VWF) is a glycoprotein synthesised by endothelial cells and megakaryocytes. Its primary role in primary haemostasis is to bridge platelets to exposed subendothelial collagen via the platelet glycoprotein Ib (GpIb) receptor following vessel injury, facilitating platelet adhesion. VWF also carries Factor VIII (F), but bridging platelets to collagen is its key haemostatic role.

questions.q: A patient presents with prolonged bleeding after minor cuts, normal platelet count, normal PT, but elevated aPTT. Blood tests reveal markedly reduced Factor VIII levels. Which condition does this describe and which pathway is primarily affected?
opts: A. von Willebrand disease affecting primary haemostasis with an elevated PT
B. Haemophilia A (Factor VIII deficiency) affecting the intrinsic pathway, detected by elevated aPTT
C. Vitamin K deficiency affecting the extrinsic pathway and prolonging PT
D. Warfarin toxicity affecting the common pathway and prolonging both PT and aPTT
E. Thrombocytopenia causing failure of primary haemostasis with normal aPTT
F. Disseminated intravascular coagulation consuming all clotting factors equally
questions.exp: Haemophilia A is a Factor VIII deficiency. Factor VIII is part of the intrinsic pathway (XII→XI→IX→VIII→X). The intrinsic pathway is measured by the aPTT — a prolonged aPTT with normal PT indicates an intrinsic pathway defect. The PT (extrinsic pathway) is normal as Factor VII (extrinsic) is unaffected.

questions.q: The coagulation cascade converges on a common pathway. Which of the following correctly describes the sequence of events in the common pathway?
opts: A. Factor XIIIa → Fibrinogen → Fibrin → Thrombin → Clot
B. Thrombin → Factor Xa → Prothrombinase complex → Fibrin mesh
C. Factor Xa + cofactor Va → Prothrombinase complex → Prothrombin → Thrombin → Fibrinogen cleaved to fibrin → cross-linked by Factor XIIIa
D. Factor VIIa + Tissue Factor → Factor X → Thrombin → Fibrin
E. Prothrombin → Fibrinogen → Thrombin → Factor XIII → Stable clot
F. Factor Xa + Factor VIIa → Prothrombin → Thrombin → Factor XIII → Fibrin
questions.exp: The common pathway: Factor Xa (with cofactor Va) forms the prothrombinase complex on a phospholipid surface, which converts prothrombin (Factor II) to thrombin (Factor IIa). Thrombin then cleaves fibrinogen to fibrin monomers, which polymerise. Factor XIIIa (activated by thrombin) cross-links the fibrin monomers to form a stable fibrin mesh — the clot.

questions.q: A patient with sepsis develops disseminated intravascular coagulation (DIC). The notes state that 'loss of thrombomodulin (as in sepsis) removes this brake, allowing uncontrolled coagulation.' What is the normal role of thrombomodulin?
opts: A. Thrombomodulin directly inhibits Factor Xa and IXa in the propagation phase
B. Thrombomodulin is a membrane protein on endothelial cells that binds thrombin to activate Protein C, which then inactivates Factors Va and VIIIa, dampening coagulation
C. Thrombomodulin directly degrades fibrin clots by activating plasmin
D. Thrombomodulin is secreted by damaged endothelium to activate the extrinsic pathway
E. Thrombomodulin binds antithrombin to neutralise circulating thrombin
F. Thrombomodulin inhibits the intrinsic pathway by blocking Factor XII activation
questions.exp: Thrombomodulin is a membrane protein on healthy endothelial cells. When thrombin binds thrombomodulin, thrombin is redirected from being a procoagulant to activating Protein C. Activated Protein C then inactivates Factors Va and VIIIa through proteolytic cleavage, dampening the amplification phase of coagulation. In sepsis, endothelial damage removes thrombomodulin, eliminating this brake and allowing uncontrolled coagulation leading to DIC.

questions.q: What are the three phases of physiological haemostasis in the cell-based model?
opts: A. Adhesion, activation, and aggregation of platelets
B. Initiation, amplification, and propagation
C. Vasoconstriction, primary haemostasis, and secondary haemostasis
D. Extrinsic pathway, intrinsic pathway, and fibrinolysis
E. Platelet plug, fibrin mesh, and fibrinolysis
F. Tissue factor release, thrombin generation, and clot retraction
questions.exp: The three phases of physiological haemostasis in the cell-based model are: (1) Initiation — on tissue factor-bearing cells, TF-FVIIa complex initiates coagulation; (2) Amplification — thrombin activates platelets and cofactors; (3) Propagation — massive thrombin generation occurs on activated platelet surfaces. The options in C describe the classical stepwise haemostasis but are not the three phases of the cell-based model specifically.

questions.q: Fibrinolysis is the final stage of haemostasis. Which enzyme is responsible for dissolving the fibrin clot?
opts: A. Thrombin cleaves fibrinogen to dissolve the fibrin clot through the reverse reaction
B. Antithrombin directly degrades the fibrin mesh by proteolysis
C. Plasmin cuts fibrin threads into smaller fragments that are removed in the urine
D. Factor XIIIa dissolves the cross-linked fibrin after wound healing is complete
E. Tissue Factor Pathway Inhibitor (TFPI) degrades fibrin by blocking Factor Xa
F. Protein C degrades the fibrin mesh by activating plasminogen in healthy endothelium
questions.exp: Fibrinolysis is the process by which fibrin is destroyed by plasmin. Plasmin cuts fibrin threads into smaller fragments (fibrin degradation products, including D-dimers) which are removed from the body, primarily in the urine. Plasmin is generated from its precursor plasminogen by tissue plasminogen activator (tPA). This completes the haemostatic cycle by dissolving the clot once healing has occurred.

questions.q: A patient is started on heparin anticoagulation. Which laboratory test should be used to monitor therapy and which pathway does it primarily evaluate?
opts: A. Prothrombin time (PT); evaluates the extrinsic pathway
B. Thrombin time (TT); evaluates the common pathway final step
C. Activated partial thromboplastin time (aPTT); evaluates the intrinsic and common pathways
D. D-dimer; evaluates fibrinolytic activity
E. Fibrinogen level; evaluates the precursor to clot formation
F. Platelet count; evaluates primary haemostasis
questions.exp: Heparin works by enhancing antithrombin III, which inhibits serine protease enzymes including thrombin, Xa, IXa, and XIa — primarily in the intrinsic and common pathways. The aPTT measures the intrinsic and common pathways and is therefore the appropriate monitoring test for heparin. PT/INR (A) is used for warfarin monitoring (extrinsic pathway via Factor VII).

questions.q: What is serum, and how does it differ from plasma?
opts: A. Serum is plasma that has been filtered through the kidneys; plasma contains all original components
B. Serum is plasma minus fibrinogen (and other clotting factors consumed in clotting); it is what remains after blood has clotted
C. Serum contains red blood cells whereas plasma does not
D. Serum has a higher albumin concentration than plasma due to concentration during clotting
E. Serum and plasma are identical — both are the liquid component of blood after cells are removed
F. Serum is the product of fibrinolysis where all clotting factors have been degraded
questions.exp: Serum is plasma minus fibrinogen and other clotting factors that are consumed during the clotting process. When blood clots, fibrinogen is converted to fibrin (forming the clot) and other factors are consumed — the remaining supernatant liquid is serum. Plasma is the liquid portion of unclotted blood containing all soluble proteins including fibrinogen.

questions.q: A 22-year-old woman presents with easy bruising and prolonged bleeding from minor cuts. Her platelet count is normal. Her PT and aPTT are both normal. However, specific factor assays show markedly reduced VWF activity. What is the most likely diagnosis?
opts: A. Haemophilia A — Factor VIII deficiency causing intrinsic pathway dysfunction
B. Haemophilia B — Factor IX deficiency causing intrinsic pathway dysfunction
C. Von Willebrand disease — reduced VWF impairs platelet adhesion to subendothelial collagen in primary haemostasis
D. Bernard-Soulier syndrome — absent glycoprotein Ib preventing VWF binding
E. Glanzmann's thrombasthenia — absent GpIIb/IIIa preventing platelet aggregation
F. Liver disease causing reduced synthesis of all clotting factors equally
questions.exp: Von Willebrand disease is the most common inherited bleeding disorder. VWF bridges platelets to subendothelial collagen via GpIb — reduced VWF impairs platelet adhesion (primary haemostasis). Normal PT and aPTT can be seen in mild VWD as the coagulation cascade is intact. In severe VWD, aPTT may be prolonged as VWF also carries and stabilises Factor VIII.

questions.q: Which of the following correctly describes the role of thrombin beyond fibrin formation?
opts: A. Thrombin only converts fibrinogen to fibrin and has no other haemostatic functions

B. Thrombin converts fibrinogen to fibrin, activates Factor XIII for cross-linking, activates platelets, amplifies its own production by activating Factors V, VIII, and XI, and regulates its own shutdown via thrombomodulin/Protein C
C. Thrombin activates antithrombin to terminate coagulation once a clot has formed
D. Thrombin directly activates fibrinolysis by converting plasminogen to plasmin
E. Thrombin acts only in the propagation phase and has no role in initiation or amplification
F. Thrombin's sole anticoagulant function is to bind TFPI to shut down the extrinsic pathway
questions.exp: Thrombin is the central effector of coagulation with multiple functions: converts fibrinogen to fibrin (structural clot), activates Factor XIII for cross-linking, activates platelets (amplifying aggregation and phospholipid surface), amplifies its own production through positive feedback (activating Factors V, VIII, and XI), and regulates its own shutdown via binding thrombomodulin which activates Protein C (negative feedback). 'Ultimately, thrombin is boss.'

questions.q: A patient with IgA nephropathy undergoes serum protein electrophoresis. This test separates proteins based on which physical properties?
opts: A. Molecular weight and solubility in water only
B. Size and electric charge, separating proteins into distinct bands across agarose gel using an electric current
C. Density and colour, using centrifugation to separate protein fractions
D. Antigenicity, using antibodies to identify and separate specific proteins
E. Temperature stability, heating the sample to denature specific proteins sequentially
F. Lipid content and hydrophobicity, separating lipoproteins from non-lipid proteins
questions.exp: Serum protein electrophoresis separates proteins based on size and electric charge. A blood sample is collected, and an electric current moves proteins across an agarose gel, separating them into distinct bands corresponding to albumin and the globulin fractions (alpha, beta, and gamma). It is used clinically to detect myeloma (monoclonal band), hypoalbuminaemia, and other protein disorders.

questions.q: B lymphocytes, T lymphocytes, and Natural Killer cells are all classified as lymphocytes. Which statement correctly distinguishes their functions?
opts: A. B lymphocytes provide cellular immunity; T lymphocytes produce antibodies; NK cells present antigens

B. B lymphocytes produce antibodies targeting bacteria, viruses and foreign materials; T lymphocytes provide cellular immunity attacking foreign/diseased cells; NK cells non-specifically attack 'non-self' cells including cancer cells
C. All three types produce antibodies but in different quantities depending on the antigen encountered
D. NK cells are the primary mediators of adaptive immunity; B and T cells mediate innate immunity
E. T lymphocytes produce antibodies for humoral immunity; B lymphocytes physically attack cancer cells
F. NK cells require prior antigen presentation to activate, whereas B and T cells activate non-specifically
questions.exp: B lymphocytes produce antibodies (immunoglobulins) that bind to bacteria, viruses, and foreign materials — this is humoral immunity. T lymphocytes provide cellular immunity, physically attacking foreign and diseased cells. NK cells are non-specific lymphocytes that recognise and attack 'non-self' cells (cancer cells, virally infected cells) without prior sensitisation — they are part of innate immunity despite being lymphocytes.

questions.q: Platelets originate from which precursor cell, and where does this process occur?
opts: A. From lymphoblasts in the thymus, released into the circulation after maturation
B. From megakaryocytes that remain in the red bone marrow and shed platelets into the circulation
C. From monocytes that differentiate in the spleen under conditions of haemorrhage
D. From erythroblasts that fail to expel their nucleus and differentiate into platelets instead
E. From haemocytoblasts in the yellow bone marrow of long bones
F. From myeloblasts in lymph nodes that are triggered by platelet-derived growth factor
questions.exp: Platelets (thrombocytes) are cellular fragments formed from megakaryocytes — large cells that remain in the red bone marrow and shed platelets into the circulation. Platelets contain granules (which release ADP and thromboxane during activation), stain purple, are surrounded by a plasma membrane, and function in haemostasis and release growth factors for tissue repair.

questions.q: What is the intrinsic (contact) pathway of coagulation, and what triggers Factor XII activation?
opts: A. The intrinsic pathway is triggered by tissue factor released from damaged perivascular cells activating Factor VIIa
B. The intrinsic pathway involves sequential activation of Factors VII→IX→XI→XII and is measured by PT

C. The intrinsic pathway begins when Factor XII contacts negatively charged surfaces (artificial or pathological surfaces like bacteria or damaged proteins) triggering sequential activation XII→XI→IX→X
D. The intrinsic pathway requires ionised calcium and Tissue Factor as mandatory cofactors for initiation
E. The intrinsic pathway is faster than the extrinsic pathway, initiating within 8-10 seconds
F. The intrinsic pathway is initiated by endothelin release from damaged endothelial cells
questions.exp: The intrinsic (contact) pathway begins when Factor XII contacts negatively charged surfaces — not normal cell surfaces, but artificial surfaces (catheters, glass, plastic) or pathological surfaces (bacteria, damaged proteins). This triggers sequential activation: XII→XI→IX→X, converging on Factor X in the common pathway. It is slower than the extrinsic pathway (40-45 seconds vs 8-10 seconds). It is measured by aPTT.

questions.q: Which of the following correctly describes the aggregation step of primary haemostasis?
opts: A. VWF bridges platelets to exposed subendothelial collagen via the GpIb receptor
B. Adherent platelets release ADP and thromboxane A2, recruiting more platelets
C. Activated platelets bind to one another via fibrinogen bridging GpIIb/IIIa receptors on adjacent platelets, forming the primary haemostatic plug
D. Thrombin converts fibrinogen to fibrin, stabilising the platelet aggregate
E. Factor Xa and Va form the prothrombinase complex on the platelet phospholipid surface
F. Endothelin released from damaged endothelium causes vasoconstriction to limit blood flow
questions.exp: Aggregation is the third step of primary haemostasis: activated platelets bind to one another via fibrinogen, which bridges GpIIb/IIIa receptors on adjacent platelets, forming the primary haemostatic plug. Option A describes adhesion. Option B describes activation. Options D and E describe secondary haemostasis. Option F describes vasoconstriction.

questions.q: A 55-year-old man with chronic kidney disease has a haemoglobin of 72 g/L. His reticulocyte count is very low. The most likely cause is reduced production of which hormone from which organ?
opts: A. Reduced thrombopoietin from the liver, impairing platelet-driven erythropoiesis
B. Reduced erythropoietin from the kidneys, impairing erythropoiesis in the red bone marrow
C. Reduced transferrin from the liver, preventing iron delivery to erythroid precursors
D. Reduced cortisol from the adrenal glands, which normally stimulates haematopoiesis
E. Reduced intrinsic factor from the stomach, preventing B12 absorption for erythropoiesis
F. Reduced aldosterone from the adrenal glands, causing sodium retention and dilutional anaemia
questions.exp: Erythropoietin (EPO) is produced by the peritubular cells of the kidney in response to hypoxia. In chronic kidney disease, EPO production is impaired, reducing the stimulus for erythropoiesis in the red bone marrow. The result is a normocytic normochromic anaemia with an inappropriately low reticulocyte count (indicating failure of production rather than destruction or loss).

questions.q: Which of the following is a function of blood that goes beyond simple oxygen transport?
opts: A. Blood is involved only in gas exchange (O2 and CO2 transport) and nutrient delivery

B. Blood transports nutrients, oxygen, hormones, and waste products; contains WBCs for immune defence and NK cells to destroy mutated cells; platelets and clotting factors prevent blood loss; and maintains temperature and chemical balance including pH buffering
C. Blood only transports hormones from endocrine glands and does not participate in temperature regulation
D. Blood maintains chemical balance through the kidneys alone, with plasma proteins playing no role in pH buffering
E. Blood provides immune defence through plasma proteins only — cellular components are purely for oxygen transport
F. Blood functions are limited to the formed elements (cells and platelets); plasma is a passive carrier with no active function
questions.exp: Blood has multiple functions: transport (nutrients, O2, CO2, hormones, waste); immune defence (WBCs phagocytose pathogens, NK cells destroy cancer cells); haemostasis (platelets and clotting factors); temperature regulation; and chemical balance (pH buffering via bicarbonate, fluid and electrolyte distribution). Plasma proteins (albumin, globulins) actively contribute to osmotic pressure, transport, and immunity.

questions.q: The Tissue Factor Pathway Inhibitor (TFPI) acts on which phase of coagulation, and what is its function?
opts: A. TFPI acts in the propagation phase, directly inhibiting thrombin activity
B. TFPI targets the amplification phase by inactivating Factors Va and VIIIa
C. TFPI shuts down the initiation phase by inhibiting the Tissue Factor pathway, preventing the coagulation system from responding to every minor signal
D. TFPI activates Protein C to dampen thrombin generation in the common pathway
E. TFPI directly activates antithrombin to neutralise thrombin and Factor Xa
F. TFPI stimulates fibrinolysis by activating plasminogen to prevent excessive clot formation
questions.exp: TFPI (Tissue Factor Pathway Inhibitor) shuts down the initiation phase by inhibiting the TF-FVIIa complex. This prevents the coagulation system from responding to every minor tissue factor signal. However, when injury is significant and the intrinsic amplification pathway is engaged, TFPI cannot block it — allowing a full coagulation response when truly needed. Protein C (B, D) targets the amplification phase. Antithrombin (E) targets the propagation phase.

questions.q: A 17-year-old male presents with a prolonged bleeding time after a dental extraction. His mother had similar problems. Laboratory results show normal platelet count, normal PT, prolonged aPTT, and reduced Factor IX activity. Which diagnosis fits?
opts: A. Haemophilia A — X-linked Factor VIII deficiency
B. Haemophilia B — X-linked Factor IX deficiency affecting the intrinsic pathway
C. Von Willebrand disease — most common inherited bleeding disorder
D. Bernard-Soulier syndrome — absent GpIb preventing platelet adhesion
E. Glanzmann's thrombasthenia — absent GpIIb/IIIa preventing platelet aggregation
F. Vitamin K deficiency — affecting Factors II, VII, IX, and X simultaneously
questions.exp: Haemophilia B is an X-linked recessive deficiency of Factor IX (Christmas factor). Factor IX is part of the intrinsic pathway (XII→XI→IX→X), so aPTT is prolonged while PT is normal (extrinsic pathway Factor VII is unaffected). The male gender and family history fit X-linked inheritance. Factor VIII deficiency (A) would present identically clinically but with reduced Factor VIII on assay.

questions.q: In the ABO blood group system, which blood group has the highest prevalence in the general population?
opts: A. Group A at 40%
B. Group B at 10%
C. Group AB at 5%
D. Group O at 45%
E. Group A and O are equal at 35% each
F. Group B is most common in all populations worldwide
questions.exp: Group O is the most common blood group at approximately 45% of the population. Group A is next at approximately 40%, Group B at 10%, and Group AB is the least common at approximately 5%. These figures apply to the general (predominantly Caucasian) population — frequencies vary between ethnic groups.

questions.q: Which of the following best describes the role of antithrombin in haemostasis?
opts: A. Antithrombin converts thrombin back to prothrombin, reversing clot formation
B. Antithrombin directly degrades fibrin by plasmin-like proteolytic activity
C. Antithrombin directly inhibits serine protease enzymes including thrombin, Xa, IXa, and XIa, controlling the propagation phase of coagulation
D. Antithrombin activates Protein C to dampen amplification of coagulation
E. Antithrombin is activated by thrombomodulin on endothelial cell surfaces
F. Antithrombin blocks platelet aggregation by competitively inhibiting GpIIb/IIIa receptors
questions.exp: Antithrombin is a serine protease inhibitor (serpin) that directly neutralises serine protease clotting enzymes including thrombin, Factor Xa, IXa, and XIa. It controls the propagation phase of coagulation by neutralising thrombin generation. Heparin works by enhancing antithrombin's activity approximately 1000-fold. Thrombomodulin/Protein C (D, E) is a separate anticoagulant system targeting the amplification phase.

questions.q: What property of red blood cells allows them to traverse capillaries smaller than their own diameter?
opts: A. Red blood cells have no nucleus, reducing their overall size to fit through capillaries
B. Red blood cells produce ATP via oxidative phosphorylation to actively deform through narrow vessels
C. Red blood cells are flexible and deformable due to their biconcave disc shape and membrane composition
D. Red blood cells shed their plasma membrane temporarily to reduce volume in narrow capillaries
E. Red blood cells have surface proteins that lubricant the capillary wall, allowing passive sliding
F. Red blood cells contain spectrin-free membranes that allow passive pressure-driven deformation
questions.exp: Red blood cells are highly flexible and deformable, which allows them to squeeze through capillaries smaller than their 7-8 μm diameter. The biconcave disc shape and the elastic properties of the red cell membrane (supported by the spectrin-actin cytoskeleton) enable this deformation. Loss of deformability (as in sickle cell disease or hereditary spherocytosis) causes haemolysis and vascular occlusion.

questions.q: A 45-year-old woman with long-standing liver cirrhosis has a coagulation screen showing prolonged PT, near-normal aPTT, low fibrinogen, and low albumin. What is the underlying mechanism?
opts: A. Vitamin K deficiency from poor dietary intake causing failure of carboxylation of clotting factors
B. The liver synthesises the majority of coagulation factors and albumin — cirrhosis impairs this synthetic function, reducing Factor VII (extrinsic, prolonging PT), fibrinogen, and albumin
C. Hypersplenism from portal hypertension destroying circulating clotting factors and platelets in the spleen
D. Biliary obstruction preventing vitamin K absorption, reducing all vitamin K-dependent factors equally
E. Disseminated intravascular coagulation consuming all clotting factors simultaneously
F. Reduced erythropoiesis impairing the platelet-derived clotting pathway
questions.exp: The liver synthesises the majority of coagulation factors including fibrinogen, prothrombin, and Factors V, VII, IX, X, and XI. Albumin is also exclusively hepatic. In cirrhosis, impaired hepatic synthetic function reduces Factor VII (the extrinsic pathway factor with the shortest half-life of 4-6 hours), causing PT prolongation first. Low albumin causes oedema, and low fibrinogen impairs clot formation. This distinguishes it from vitamin K deficiency where Factors II, VII, IX, and X are all reduced.

questions.q: Platelets are described as 'cellular fragments' — they are not complete cells. What structure gives rise to platelets and how?
opts: A. Erythroblasts expel their nuclei which then acquire a plasma membrane to become platelets
B. Lymphoblasts in the thymus fragment into smaller pieces during differentiation to form thrombocytes
C. Megakaryocytes are large polyploid cells in the red bone marrow that extend cytoplasmic projections into sinusoids and shed platelet fragments into the circulation
D. Monocytes in the spleen differentiate into platelet-producing cells under conditions of haemorrhage
E. Neutrophil granules are released into the circulation where they acquire lipid membranes to form platelets
F. Haemocytoblasts directly differentiate into platelet fragments without an intermediate megakaryocyte stage
questions.exp: Platelets are formed from megakaryocytes — large cells with polyploid nuclei that remain in the red bone marrow. Megakaryocytes extend long cytoplasmic projections (proplatelets) into bone marrow sinusoids, from which individual platelets are shed into the circulation. Each megakaryocyte can produce thousands of platelets. This is described in your notes as megakaryocytes that 'remain in the red bone marrow and shed platelets into circulation.'

questions.q: Which of the following correctly explains why haemostasis is described as a 'dynamic system' in your notes?
opts: A. Haemostasis is dynamic because it only occurs during injury and is completely switched off at all other times

B. Haemostasis is dynamic because tiny amounts of clotting and breakdown occur continuously in balance, and even shows circadian variation with higher coagulation and lower fibrinolysis in the morning
C. Haemostasis is dynamic because the balance shifts permanently towards anticoagulation as individuals age
D. Haemostasis is dynamic because different pathways activate depending on the severity of the injury only
E. Haemostasis is dynamic because it alternates between procoagulant and anticoagulant states on a 24-hour cycle
F. Haemostasis is dynamic because Factor XII constantly activates and deactivates through surface contact with blood vessel walls
questions.exp: Haemostasis doesn't simply turn 'on' or 'off' — tiny amounts of clotting and breakdown occur all the time, kept in balance by anticoagulant (TFPI, Protein C, antithrombin) and fibrinolytic systems. When injury occurs, the system accelerates dramatically. Remarkably, it even shows circadian variation — healthy individuals have higher coagulation activity and lower fibrinolysis in the morning, which may contribute to the increased incidence of MI and stroke in the morning.

questions.q: A medical student notes that Factor XII (Hageman factor) is described as 'like scaffolding, useful for building but not the final structure.' What does this mean clinically?
opts: A. Factor XII deficiency causes a severe bleeding disorder because it is essential for intrinsic pathway activation

B. Factor XII deficiency paradoxically causes NO clinical bleeding despite a markedly prolonged aPTT, because Factor XII is important for in vitro clotting tests but not for physiological haemostasis in vivo
C. Factor XII is critical for fibrinolysis but not for primary haemostasis
D. Factor XII deficiency causes thrombosis rather than bleeding, as it normally activates the fibrinolytic system
E. Factor XII is redundant and its deficiency has no effect on either bleeding time or aPTT
F. Factor XII only functions during the amplification phase and can be substituted by Factor XI
questions.exp: Factor XII (Hageman factor) deficiency is clinically paradoxical — it causes a markedly prolonged aPTT on laboratory testing (because aPTT depends on negatively charged surface contact activation via Factor XII) but does NOT cause clinical bleeding. This is because physiological coagulation in vivo is primarily initiated by the extrinsic pathway (Tissue Factor), with the intrinsic pathway serving to amplify, not initiate. Factor XII is important for the lab test but not for real haemostasis — hence 'scaffolding, useful for building but not the final structure.'

questions.q: What is the composition of plasma by percentage?
opts: A. Water 70%, plasma proteins 20%, electrolytes 10%
B. Water 90%, with electrolytes, nutrients, metabolites, plasma proteins, vitamins, enzymes, and hormones making up the remainder
C. Water 55%, plasma proteins 35%, other solutes 10%
D. Red blood cells 45%, plasma proteins 50%, water 5%
E. Water 80%, electrolytes 15%, hormones and nutrients 5%
F. Albumin 60%, globulins 38%, fibrinogen 2% with no water component
questions.exp: Plasma is approximately 90% water. The remaining 10% consists of dissolved substances including electrolytes (Na+, K+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Cl-, HCO3-), nutrients (glucose, amino acids, lipids), metabolites, plasma proteins (albumin, globulins, fibrinogen), vitamins, enzymes, and hormones. Plasma makes up approximately 55% of total blood volume.

questions.q: Innate immunity involves both cellular and plasma components. Which of the following lists only innate immune components as described in your notes?
opts: A. B lymphocytes, T lymphocytes, and immunoglobulins
B. Neutrophils, eosinophils, basophils, mast cells; plasma enzymes (lysozymes), complement proteins, acute phase proteins, and cytokines
C. NK cells, B lymphocytes, and gamma globulins
D. Monocytes, T lymphocytes, and transferrin
E. Neutrophils, T lymphocytes, complement proteins, and immunoglobulins
F. Memory B cells, plasma cells, and antigen-presenting cells
questions.exp: Innate immunity (born with it, non-specific) includes: cellular components — neutrophils, eosinophils, basophils, and mast cells; and plasma components — enzymes (lysozymes), complement proteins, acute phase proteins (CRP, fibrinogen), and soluble cytokines. Adaptive immunity (acquired, specific) involves B lymphocytes (antibodies), T lymphocytes (cellular immunity), and memory cells. NK cells are innate despite being lymphocytes.

questions.q: A patient with nephrotic syndrome loses large amounts of protein in the urine. Which plasma protein would be most severely depleted, and what would be the clinical consequence?
opts: A. Fibrinogen is most depleted, causing a severe coagulopathy and haemorrhagic tendency
B. Gamma globulins are most depleted, causing severe immunodeficiency and recurrent infections
C. Albumin is most depleted due to its abundance and size, causing severe hypoalbuminaemia with reduced oncotic pressure, oedema, and ascites
D. Alpha-1 antitrypsin is most depleted, causing progressive lung emphysema
E. Transferrin is most depleted, causing severe iron deficiency anaemia resistant to oral supplementation
F. Complement proteins are most depleted, causing failure of innate immune opsonisation
questions.exp: Albumin is the most abundant plasma protein (60% of total) and also the smallest major protein, making it most susceptible to loss through damaged glomerular filtration membranes in nephrotic syndrome. Severe hypoalbuminaemia reduces plasma oncotic pressure, causing fluid to shift into the interstitial space — causing peripheral oedema, ascites, and pleural effusions. Hypoalbuminaemia also affects drug binding and transport of hormones and fatty acids.

questions.q: Your notes state that 'ABO antibodies are naturally occurring IgM' and 'arise without prior blood exposure.' How does this occur?
opts: A. ABO antibodies are maternally transferred at birth and persist throughout life

B. They develop from early-life exposure to environmental antigens with similar structures to ABO antigens, such as gut bacteria and food, triggering antibody formation against the ABO antigens you don't carry
C. They are genetically encoded and produced constitutively from birth without any external stimulus
D. They develop from repeated minor blood exposures through insect bites or environmental cuts
E. They are produced by NK cells that detect foreign ABO antigens through non-specific recognition
F. They are produced in response to the first blood transfusion received in life, usually in early childhood
questions.exp: ABO antibodies are 'naturally occurring' because they arise without intentional prior blood exposure. They develop in the first years of life from exposure to environmental antigens — gut bacteria, food, and other environmental sources — that have carbohydrate structures similar to ABO blood group antigens. The immune system produces antibodies against the ABO antigens it does not express (e.g. Group A individuals make anti-B). This is why ABO incompatibility is immediately dangerous even on first transfusion.

questions.q: Which of the following correctly describes what happens during the 'activation' step of primary haemostasis?
opts: A. VWF bridges the first platelets to exposed subendothelial collagen through GpIb
B. Activated platelets bind to each other via fibrinogen and GpIIb/IIIa to form the primary plug
C. Adherent platelets become activated, change shape, and release granule contents including ADP and thromboxane A2, which recruit additional platelets
D. Thrombin converts fibrinogen to fibrin to stabilise the platelet plug
E. The prothrombinase complex is formed on the platelet phospholipid surface
F. Vasoconstriction reduces blood flow through local myogenic reflexes and endothelin release
questions.exp: Activation is Step 2 of primary haemostasis: adherent platelets (already bound to collagen via VWF) become activated, undergo shape change, and release granule contents including ADP, thromboxane A2 (TXA2), and serotonin. These mediators are potent platelet recruiters that amplify the response, drawing more platelets to the site of injury. This sets the stage for aggregation (Step 3).

questions.q: The extrinsic pathway is described as the 'main initiator of coagulation.' How fast does it initiate compared to the intrinsic pathway?
opts: A. The extrinsic pathway initiates in 40-45 seconds; the intrinsic pathway in 8-10 seconds
B. Both pathways initiate at the same speed of approximately 20 seconds
C. The extrinsic pathway initiates in 8-10 seconds; the intrinsic pathway takes 40-45 seconds
D. The extrinsic pathway requires 2-3 minutes; the intrinsic pathway initiates within seconds
E. The intrinsic pathway is faster because it is amplified by positive feedback loops
F. Speed is identical — both pathways are limited by Factor X activation in the common pathway
questions.exp: The extrinsic pathway (Tissue Factor pathway) initiates rapidly in 8-10 seconds because tissue factor is immediately exposed when vessels are damaged. The intrinsic (contact) pathway takes 40-45 seconds to initiate. The extrinsic pathway is the primary initiator of coagulation, while the intrinsic pathway serves to amplify and sustain thrombin generation — this is why TFPI can shut down the extrinsic initiation while the intrinsic amplification continues.

questions.q: A 62-year-old man with advanced prostate cancer develops disseminated intravascular coagulation (DIC). His coagulation screen shows prolonged PT and aPTT, low fibrinogen, high D-dimers, and low platelets. What is the underlying mechanism?
opts: A. The cancer has directly destroyed all bone marrow haematopoietic cells, reducing all blood components

B. Cancer cells express tissue factor, triggering systemic activation of the extrinsic coagulation pathway, simultaneously consuming clotting factors and platelets and activating fibrinolysis, causing both clotting and bleeding
C. Cancer chemotherapy has inhibited vitamin K recycling, causing factor deficiency in all vitamin K-dependent factors
D. Metastatic spread has obstructed the bile duct, preventing vitamin K absorption
E. Tumour necrosis factor from the cancer has directly activated antithrombin, consuming all clotting factors
F. Bone marrow infiltration has reduced platelet production causing thrombocytopenia with secondary coagulopathy
questions.exp: In DIC, systemic activation of the coagulation cascade — often driven by tissue factor expressed on cancer cells, sepsis, or obstetric complications — causes simultaneous widespread clotting (microvascular thrombosis) and consumption of clotting factors and platelets. Secondary fibrinolysis is activated to break down clots, generating D-dimers. The result is paradoxical bleeding (from factor consumption) and thrombosis (from microvascular clots). Low fibrinogen, elevated D-dimers, and prolonged PT/aPTT confirm DIC.

questions.q: Your notes describe lymphocytes as playing 'a significant role in adaptive (acquired) immunity where the immune system develops specialised responses over time.' What is the key feature of adaptive immunity?
opts: A. Adaptive immunity responds identically to all pathogens without any specificity
B. Adaptive immunity only involves antibody production and has no cellular component

C. Adaptive immunity involves antigen-specific responses that improve with repeated exposure — when re-exposed to a previously encountered pathogen, the immune system provides a faster and more effective response
D. Adaptive immunity is identical to innate immunity but slower to activate
E. Adaptive immunity is mediated entirely by NK cells which remember previous infections
F. Adaptive immunity is only effective against bacterial pathogens and not viruses or cancer
questions.exp: Adaptive (acquired) immunity is characterised by specificity and memory. The immune system develops specialised responses as it encounters pathogens, toxins, or vaccinations. Memory B-cells and T-cells are generated — upon re-exposure, the response is faster and more effective (anamnestic response). This is the basis of vaccination. It contrasts with innate immunity which responds non-specifically without memory.

questions.q: Red bone marrow and yellow bone marrow have different compositions and functions. Which statement is correct?
opts: A. Red bone marrow contains mostly fat; yellow bone marrow contains blood stem cells
B. Both red and yellow bone marrow produce equal numbers of blood cells throughout adult life
C. Red bone marrow contains blood stem cells that can become RBCs, WBCs, or platelets; yellow bone marrow consists mostly of fat and contains stem cells that can become cartilage, fat, or bone cells
D. Yellow bone marrow replaces red bone marrow throughout the skeleton from birth
E. Red bone marrow is found only in the skull and vertebral bodies in adults
F. Yellow bone marrow is the primary site of lymphocyte production in adults
questions.exp: Red bone marrow contains haematopoietic stem cells (haemocytoblasts) capable of producing all blood cells — RBCs, WBCs, and platelets. Yellow bone marrow consists mostly of adipose (fat) tissue and contains mesenchymal stem cells that can produce cartilage, fat, or bone cells. In adults, red bone marrow is restricted to flat and irregular bones (vertebrae, ribs, sternum, pelvis, proximal femur), while the shafts of long bones contain yellow marrow.

questions.q: C-reactive protein (CRP) is an acute phase protein used clinically. Which of the following best describes its properties?
opts: A. CRP is a gamma globulin produced by plasma cells in response to antigen stimulation
B. CRP is produced by the liver in response to inflammation, rises rapidly with inflammatory stimuli, and is a useful but non-specific marker of inflammation and infection
C. CRP is a negative acute phase protein that falls in response to inflammation
D. CRP is primarily used to monitor anticoagulation therapy in patients with deep vein thrombosis
E. CRP is produced by neutrophils as part of the innate immune granule release mechanism
F. CRP directly activates complement proteins as its primary anti-inflammatory mechanism
questions.exp: CRP is a positive acute phase protein synthesised by the liver in response to IL-6 released from sites of inflammation. It rises rapidly (within hours) in response to inflammatory stimuli — infection, autoimmune disease, tissue injury. It is a sensitive but non-specific marker: high CRP indicates inflammation but not its cause. CRP can be used to monitor treatment response. Transferrin (not CRP) is a negative acute phase protein.

questions.q: A patient is found to have Group A Rh-negative blood on pre-operative testing. For an elective operation, which blood should ideally be prepared?
opts: A. O negative blood only, to ensure universal compatibility
B. Group A Rh-negative blood, as ABO-identical blood should always be given when time permits
C. Group A Rh-positive blood is acceptable as the patient is already sensitised
D. Group AB Rh-negative as AB is the universal recipient
E. Any Rh-negative blood as Rh status is the only compatibility that matters
F. Group O Rh-positive as the small risk of Rh sensitisation is acceptable in an adult male
questions.exp: Your notes state: 'In practice, always give ABO-identical blood when time permits.' For an elective procedure with time to crossmatch, Group A Rh-negative blood should be prepared. O negative is reserved for emergencies when the blood group is unknown. ABO-identical blood reduces the risk of alloimmunisation to minor antigens and ensures optimal compatibility.

questions.q: What role do gamma globulins play in immunity, and which cells produce them?
opts: A. Gamma globulins are carrier proteins for fat-soluble vitamins, produced by the liver
B. Gamma globulins (immunoglobulins/antibodies) are produced by plasma cells (differentiated B lymphocytes) and defend the body against pathogens by binding to foreign antigens
C. Gamma globulins are complement proteins produced by hepatocytes as part of innate immunity
D. Gamma globulins are produced by T lymphocytes and provide cellular immunity
E. Gamma globulins are acute phase reactants that rise non-specifically in response to any inflammation
F. Gamma globulins are produced by NK cells and specifically target cancer antigens
questions.exp: Gamma globulins are immunoglobulins (antibodies) produced by plasma cells — which are fully differentiated B lymphocytes. They defend the body against pathogens by specifically binding to foreign antigens (bacteria, viruses, fungi, foreign proteins). They are the basis of adaptive humoral immunity and are used clinically in serum electrophoresis to detect monoclonal gammopathies (myeloma) or hypogammaglobulinaemia.

questions.q: Your notes describe the 'contact activation' that occurs in the intrinsic pathway. What pathological and clinical significance does this have?
opts: A. Contact activation is important because it is the primary mechanism of physiological clot formation in response to tissue injury
B. Contact activation has no clinical relevance as Factor XII deficiency causes no bleeding

C. Contact activation explains pathological thrombosis on artificial surfaces like catheters, heart valves, and plastic/glass, and explains clot formation with inflammation such as in sepsis where immune activation triggers widespread coagulation
D. Contact activation is used exclusively to perform aPTT tests in the laboratory and has no in vivo relevance
E. Contact activation is the mechanism underlying haemolytic transfusion reactions
F. Contact activation triggers fibrinolysis rather than coagulation, protecting against thrombosis on artificial surfaces
questions.exp: Contact activation (Factor XII by negatively charged surfaces) is not primarily relevant to physiological haemostasis (Factor XII deficiency causes no bleeding) but is clinically significant for: (1) explaining thrombosis on artificial surfaces — catheters, prosthetic heart valves, extracorporeal circuits, plastic/glass; (2) explaining thrombosis with inflammation in sepsis, where immune activation and bacterial surfaces trigger widespread coagulation (DIC); and (3) making the aPTT laboratory test work. Your notes explicitly state all three points.

questions.q: Which of the following correctly describes the difference between the intrinsic and extrinsic coagulation pathways?
opts: A. Intrinsic pathway is activated by Tissue Factor from outside the vessel lumen; extrinsic by platelet phospholipids inside the vessel

B. Intrinsic pathway is activated by exposed collagen/phospholipids on activated platelets inside the vessel lumen (activating XII, XI, IX, VIII); extrinsic is activated by Tissue Factor from outside the lumen (activating Factor VII)
C. Both pathways are activated simultaneously by the same mechanism and cannot be distinguished clinically
D. Extrinsic pathway activates Factors VIII, IX, XI, XII; intrinsic activates Factor VII and Tissue Factor
E. Intrinsic pathway requires only ionised calcium; extrinsic requires both calcium and phospholipids
F. The intrinsic pathway is activated by damaged endothelium releasing endothelin; extrinsic by platelet ADP release
questions.exp: The key distinction: the intrinsic pathway is triggered by exposure of negatively charged collagen or phospholipids from activated platelets inside the vessel lumen, activating Factors XII→XI→IX→X (40-45 seconds). The extrinsic pathway is triggered by Tissue Factor (TF) released from perivascular and subendothelial cells outside the lumen, activating Factor VIIa (8-10 seconds). Both converge on Factor X in the common pathway.

questions.q: A 70-year-old woman on warfarin for atrial fibrillation presents with a supratherapeutic INR of 9.2 and haematuria. Which clotting factors are most affected by warfarin?
opts: A. Factors VIII, IX, XI, and XII — all intrinsic pathway factors
B. Factors II (prothrombin), VII, IX, and X — all vitamin K-dependent factors — plus Protein C and Protein S
C. Only Factor VII as warfarin exclusively inhibits the extrinsic pathway
D. Fibrinogen and Factor XIII — the final common pathway factors
E. Platelet activation factors including thromboxane A2 and ADP receptors
F. All clotting factors equally through competitive inhibition of the clotting enzyme active sites
questions.exp: Warfarin inhibits vitamin K epoxide reductase, preventing recycling of vitamin K. Vitamin K is required for the carboxylation of clotting factors II, VII, IX, and X (and the anticoagulant proteins C and S). Factor VII has the shortest half-life (4-6 hours), so the PT/INR prolongs first. Haematuria with INR 9.2 represents dangerous supratherapeutic anticoagulation requiring urgent reversal (vitamin K, prothrombin complex concentrate).

questions.q: Transferrin is described as a 'principal transport protein for Iron (Fe3+).' In the context of blood physiology, why is iron transport critical?
opts: A. Iron is needed for plasma membrane synthesis in all blood cells
B. Iron (Fe3+) carried by transferrin to the bone marrow is incorporated into haem — the oxygen-binding component of haemoglobin — making it essential for erythropoiesis and oxygen transport
C. Iron is needed for complement protein synthesis in the liver
D. Iron is required as a cofactor for platelet activation during primary haemostasis
E. Iron transported by transferrin is used exclusively for white blood cell granule synthesis
F. Iron is needed for the glycosyltransferase enzymes that modify ABO blood group antigens
questions.exp: Iron (Fe3+) is transported by transferrin (a beta globulin synthesised in the liver) to the bone marrow where it is incorporated into haem — the iron-containing portion of haemoglobin. Each haemoglobin molecule contains 4 haem groups, each capable of binding one oxygen molecule. Iron deficiency reduces haem synthesis, causing microcytic hypochromic anaemia. Transferrin also plays an important role in iron homeostasis (it is a negative acute phase protein).

questions.q: Eosinophils are described as secreting antihistamines. Why is this paradoxical and what is the functional significance?
opts: A. It is not paradoxical — eosinophils and basophils both primarily promote inflammation with no regulatory function

B. Basophils release histamine to drive inflammation, while eosinophils secrete antihistamines to moderate the allergic and inflammatory response — creating a regulatory balance between pro-inflammatory and anti-inflammatory mediators at the site of infection
C. Eosinophils secrete antihistamines to destroy parasites directly, as histamine is toxic to helminths
D. Antihistamines from eosinophils are used to attract more neutrophils to the site of infection
E. Eosinophils secrete antihistamines only in response to bacterial infections, not parasitic ones
F. The antihistamines released are used to regulate the vasoconstriction phase of haemostasis
questions.exp: This creates an elegant regulatory balance: basophils (and mast cells) release histamine to drive the inflammatory and allergic response. Eosinophils, which are elevated in the same allergic and parasitic settings, secrete antihistamines to moderate and regulate this inflammatory response, preventing excessive tissue damage. Eosinophils thus serve a dual function — attacking parasites with toxic proteins while simultaneously modulating the inflammatory response.

questions.q: A patient has blood group O positive. Which of the following statements about their plasma antibodies is correct?
opts: A. Their plasma contains anti-A antibodies only, as they are O positive
B. Their plasma contains no antibodies as O is the universal donor
C. Their plasma contains both anti-A and anti-B antibodies (IgM), making O plasma dangerous to give to A, B, or AB recipients in large volumes
D. Their plasma contains anti-D antibodies as they are Rh positive
E. Their plasma contains anti-A, anti-B, and anti-D antibodies
F. Their plasma contains only anti-D antibodies as the ABO system produces no natural antibodies in O individuals
questions.exp: Group O individuals have no A or B antigens on their red cells but have both anti-A and anti-B IgM antibodies in their plasma. This is why your notes state 'Type O plasma contains anti-A and anti-B, so use judiciously.' While O negative red cells are the universal red cell donor, O plasma should not be given freely to non-O recipients as the anti-A and anti-B antibodies can haemolyse their red cells. AB plasma (no antibodies) is the universal plasma donor.

questions.q: Following a major vessel injury, the sequence of haemostatic responses is: vasoconstriction, primary haemostasis, then secondary haemostasis. What is the specific purpose of each stage?
opts: A. Vasoconstriction forms the platelet plug; primary haemostasis activates the coagulation cascade; secondary haemostasis causes fibrinolysis

B. Vasoconstriction reduces blood flow to limit blood loss; primary haemostasis forms a temporary platelet plug; secondary haemostasis stabilises the plug with a cross-linked fibrin mesh via the coagulation cascade
C. Vasoconstriction permanently seals small vessels; primary haemostasis activates complement; secondary haemostasis recruits immune cells
D. All three stages occur simultaneously within the first 30 seconds of vessel injury
E. Vasoconstriction is mediated by serotonin only; primary haemostasis by thrombin; secondary haemostasis by plasmin
F. Primary haemostasis forms the fibrin clot; secondary haemostasis forms the platelet plug; vasoconstriction occurs last as a consolidation step
questions.exp: The three stages of haemostasis are sequential and complementary: (1) Vasoconstriction — immediate response mediated by local myogenic reflexes and endothelin from damaged endothelium, reducing blood flow to limit loss; (2) Primary haemostasis — platelet adhesion (via VWF), activation (ADP, TXA2 release), and aggregation (via fibrinogen/GpIIb-IIIa) form the temporary platelet plug; (3) Secondary haemostasis — the coagulation cascade generates a cross-linked fibrin mesh to stabilise the platelet plug and form a durable clot.

questions.q: In your notes, it states that the Protein C system 'targets the cofactors (Va and VIIIa) to dampen the amplification phase.' What triggers Protein C activation?
opts: A. Protein C is activated directly by Factor Xa in the common pathway
B. Protein C is activated by thrombomodulin-bound thrombin — thrombin bound to thrombomodulin on healthy endothelial cells redirects thrombin from procoagulant to anticoagulant activity
C. Protein C is activated by TFPI as part of the initiation phase shutdown
D. Protein C is activated by antithrombin in the propagation phase
E. Protein C is activated automatically once a clot has formed and reached a critical mass
F. Protein C is constitutively active and only inhibited during vessel injury to allow clotting to occur
questions.exp: Thrombomodulin is a membrane protein always present on healthy endothelial cells. When thrombin binds thrombomodulin, the complex activates Protein C. Activated Protein C then inactivates Factors Va and VIIIa — the key amplification cofactors — through proteolytic cleavage. This creates a negative feedback loop: more thrombin → more Protein C activation → more Factor Va/VIIIa inactivation → less thrombin generation. Thrombomodulin thus converts thrombin from a clot-promoting to an anticoagulant-generating enzyme.

questions.q: A 3-year-old child is found to have hepatomegaly and hypersplenism. Investigations reveal that erythropoiesis is occurring in the liver and spleen. This is normal at which stage of development?
opts: A. This pattern is normal in adults under physiological stress
B. Hepatic and splenic erythropoiesis is the normal pattern between approximately 2-5 months of gestation
C. This is always pathological and indicates bone marrow failure regardless of age
D. This pattern is normal only in the early fetus (yolk sac), not in the liver or spleen at any stage
E. Splenic erythropoiesis is normal throughout childhood up to age 10
F. Hepatic erythropoiesis is normal from 5 months gestation onwards through to adulthood
questions.exp: Erythropoiesis occurs at different sites at different developmental stages: early fetus — yolk sac; 2-5 months gestation — liver and spleen; after 5 months to childhood — bone marrow of most bones; adults — restricted to red bone marrow of vertebrae, ribs, sternum, sacrum, pelvis, and proximal femur only. Hepatic or splenic erythropoiesis in a 3-year-old would be pathological (extramedullary haematopoiesis), suggesting the bone marrow cannot meet demand.

questions.q: Haemophilia A (Factor VIII deficiency) and von Willebrand disease both cause bleeding. Which clinical feature best distinguishes the typical bleeding pattern of von Willebrand disease from haemophilia A?
opts: A. VWD causes haemarthroses (joint bleeds) as the primary manifestation; haemophilia causes predominantly mucosal bleeding

B. VWD typically causes mucosal bleeding (epistaxis, menorrhagia, bruising, gum bleeding) reflecting defective primary haemostasis; haemophilia A causes deep tissue and joint bleeds reflecting defective secondary haemostasis
C. Both conditions cause identical bleeding patterns and cannot be clinically distinguished without laboratory testing
D. VWD causes delayed bleeding hours after injury; haemophilia causes immediate haemorrhage
E. VWD predominantly affects males; haemophilia affects females more severely
F. Both are identical clinically but VWD has a longer aPTT than haemophilia A
questions.exp: VWD is primarily a defect of primary haemostasis (platelet adhesion) — this causes mucosal-type bleeding: epistaxis, menorrhagia, gum bleeding, easy bruising, and prolonged bleeding from cuts. Haemophilia A/B is a defect of secondary haemostasis (coagulation) — this causes deep tissue bleeding: haemarthroses, intramuscular haematomas, and retroperitoneal bleeds. This distinction reflects whether the primary platelet plug or the secondary fibrin clot is the defective step.

questions.q: Your notes describe fibrinogen as 'precursor to fibrin in clot formation.' What other acute phase protein role does fibrinogen play?
opts: A. Fibrinogen is a carrier protein for iron, equivalent in function to transferrin
B. Fibrinogen is a positive acute phase protein that rises during inflammation and promotes rouleaux formation of red blood cells, elevating the ESR
C. Fibrinogen is a negative acute phase protein that falls during inflammation
D. Fibrinogen acts as an anticoagulant by competing with thrombin for fibrin binding sites
E. Fibrinogen directly activates complement proteins during the acute phase response
F. Fibrinogen acts as a carrier for fat-soluble vitamins including Vitamin D during the inflammatory response
